topics.title: Design Equation for Single Reaction System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT745 - POLYMER REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Equation for Single Reaction System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Single Reaction System** in Polymer Reaction Engineering refers to any reactive system in which the overall polymerization (or depolymerization) can be represented by **one independent stoichiometric equation**. The **Design Equation** is the fundamental mathematical relationship obtained by performing a **mole balance** on a specific reactant (typically the monomer A) over a chosen reactor, integrated with the **rate law** (`-r_A`) and the **reaction stoichiometry** to relate the **reactor volume (V)**, **space time (τ)**, **feed conditions (F_A0)**, and **conversion (X_A)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:**
> *"A single reaction is one that can be represented by a single stoichiometric equation, and its progress can be described by a single variable — the conversion X_A of a key reactant."*

The three canonical reactor design equations are:
- **Batch Reactor (BR):**  $t = N_{A0}\displaystyle\int_0^{X_A} \dfrac{dX_A}{(-r_A)V}$
- **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR):**  $V = \dfrac{F_{A0}X_A}{(-r_A)\big|_{out}}$
- **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR):**  $V = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int_0^{X_A} \dfrac{dX_A}{(-r_A)}$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are brewing a large vat of tea for a college fest (a real-world KTU moment 🍵):

- **The recipe** = the **stoichiometry** (how much sugar per litre of tea).
- **The cooking time** = the **space time (τ)** needed to reach the right taste.
- **The strength of the tea** = the **conversion (X_A)** — how much of the original "raw" tea has turned into the final drinkable form.
- **The size of the vessel** = the **reactor volume (V)**.

If you want **5 litres of strongly brewed tea** vs. **5 litres of mild tea**, you need a **bigger vessel** or **more time**. The *design equation* simply tells you the exact relationship between vessel size, time, and strength (conversion). In polymerization, the "strength" becomes **monomer conversion**, and the "vessel" must deliver the target **number-average degree of polymerization (X̄_n)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Polymer Link:**
> In polymer reaction engineering, the design equation is not only used to size the reactor but also to **predict molecular weight (M_n, M_w) and the polydispersity index (PDI)** as functions of conversion. This is what distinguishes polymer CRE from small-molecule CRE.

## 1.3 Important Physical Quantities (Standard KTU Notation)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $F_{A0}$ | Molar feed rate of monomer A | **kmol/s** |
| $N_{A0}$ | Initial moles of A in batch | **kmol** |
| $X_A$ | Fractional conversion of A | **dimensionless (0–1)** |
| $-r_A$ | Rate of disappearance of A | **kmol/(m³·s)** |
| $V$ | Reactor volume | **m³** |
| $\tau$ | Space time = $V/\nu_0$ | **seconds (s)** |
| $C_{A0}$ | Initial concentration of A | **kmol/m³** |
| $\bar{X}_n$ | Number-average degree of polymerization | **dimensionless** |

> [!TIP]
> **Always carry the unit consistency check:** when a numerical problem is solved, the left-hand side and right-hand side of the design equation **must** reduce to identical units (typically m³ or seconds). This is one of the most common KTU valuation traps.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundation — The General Mole Balance Equation (GMBE)

The **General Mole Balance** is the bedrock of every reactor design equation. For any species **j** in any control volume (reactor):

$$\text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation}$$

Mathematically expressed for reactant **A**:

$$F_{A0} - F_A + \int^V r_A \, dV = \frac{dN_A}{dt}$$

For **steady-state** operation, the accumulation term vanishes. By introducing the conversion $X_A = (F_{A0} - F_A)/F_{A0}$, the three canonical reactor design equations emerge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the General Mole Balance important for KTU?**
> Almost every 14-mark derivation question starts with this balance. Examiners allocate **2–3 marks** simply for writing the GMBE correctly.

## 2.2 The Three Reactor Design Equations — Logic Steps

### 2.2.1 Batch Reactor (BR)
- **No inflow, no outflow**: $F_{A0} = F_A = 0$ in flow terms, but $N_A$ varies with time.
- The mole balance reduces to: $\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A V$
- Substituting $N_A = N_{A0}(1-X_A)$:
  - $-N_{A0}\dfrac{dX_A}{dt} = r_A V$
- **Design Equation:**
  $$t = N_{A0}\int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{(-r_A)V}$$

### 2.2.2 Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)
- **Steady state**, **perfect mixing** (uniform composition throughout).
- Mole balance: $F_{A0} - F_{A,out} + r_A V = 0$
- Since $-r_A$ is evaluated at the outlet (well-mixed):
  - $F_{A0}X_A = -r_A \cdot V$
- **Design Equation:**
  $$V = \frac{F_{A0}X_A}{-r_A}$$

### 2.2.3 Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)
- **Steady state**, **no axial mixing**; composition varies along the length.
- Differential mole balance over slice $dV$: $F_A - (F_A + dF_A) + r_A\,dV = 0$
- Substituting $F_A = F_{A0}(1-X_A)$ and $dF_A = -F_{A0}dX_A$:
  - $F_{A0}\,dX_A = -r_A\,dV$
- **Design Equation:**
  $$V = F_{A0}\int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymer Application Insight:**
> In **step-growth polymerization** (e.g., Nylon-6,6), the design equation for a batch reactor is directly coupled to the **Carothers equation**: $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$, where $p = X_A$ is the fractional conversion. Hence the design equation simultaneously determines **reactor volume AND product molecular weight**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Reactor Type | Design Equation | Special Form for $n^{th}$-order kinetics $-r_A = kC_A^n$ | Polymer-Specific Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch** | $t = N_{A0}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{dX_A}{(-r_A)V}$ | $t = \dfrac{1}{kC_{A0}^{n-1}}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{(1-X_A)^{1-n}\,dX_A}{(1+\varepsilon X_A)^n}$ | Used for batch polymerization (suspension, emulsion) |
| **CSTR** | $V = \dfrac{F_{A0}X_A}{-r_A}$ | $V = \dfrac{\nu_0 X_A}{kC_{A0}^{n-1}(1-X_A)^n}$ | Used for control of MWD in free-radical polymerization |
| **PFR** | $V = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | $V = \dfrac{\nu_0}{kC_{A0}^{n-1}}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{dX_A}{(1-X_A)^n}$ | Used for continuous bulk polymerization |
| **Carothers (Step-Growth)** | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ (with non-stoichiometric ratio $r$) | Links conversion to molecular weight |

Where:
- $\nu_0$ = volumetric feed rate (m³/s)
- $\varepsilon$ = fractional change in volume per mole of A reacted
- $p$ = extent of reaction (fraction of functional groups consumed)
- $r$ = stoichiometric imbalance ratio $(N_B/N_A)$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Students often confuse the **conversion (X_A)** of monomer with the **extent of reaction (p)** for step-growth systems. For step-growth, $p$ refers to the fraction of **functional groups** consumed, which is numerically equal to monomer conversion **only** when the system is equimolar.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Polymer Manufacturing** | Sizing industrial batch autoclaves for polystyrene, PVC, Nylon |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Designing continuous-flow reactors for drug-polymer conjugates |
| **Coatings & Adhesives** | Predicting cure time and final crosslink density |
| **Biopolymer Engineering** | PLA, PHA bioreactor scale-up |
| **Recycling** | Depolymerization reactor design (chemical recycling of PET) |

## 2.5 Polymerization-Specific Design Considerations

1. **Gel Effect (Trommsdorff Effect):** At high conversion in free-radical polymerization, viscosity rises sharply, reducing termination rate. This makes $-r_A$ a **strong function of X_A** and demands numerical integration of the design equation.

2. **Glass Effect:** Near the glass transition temperature, chain mobility freezes and reaction effectively stops — a **kinetic ceiling** independent of reactor volume.

3. **Chain-Growth vs Step-Growth:** The same design equation governs volume, but the **rate law** $-r_A$ differs vastly:
   - Free-radical chain-growth: $-r_A = k_p[M][M\cdot]$ (initiation–propagation–termination kinetics)
   - Step-growth: $-r_A = k C_A C_B$ (second-order, equimolar)

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 Module-1, students are expected to write the **general design equation** and apply it to **first-order** and **second-order** polymerizations, then compare batch vs CSTR performance for the **same conversion**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the General Mole Balance Equation (GMBE)

Consider an arbitrary control volume $V$ (the reactor) with a flowing stream containing species A. Let:
- $F_A$ = molar flow rate of A entering (kmol/s)
- $F_{A+\Delta V}$ = molar flow rate leaving a slice of volume $\Delta V$
- $r_A$ = rate of generation of A per unit volume [kmol/(m³·s)]

**Step 1:** Write the balance over a differential slice of volume $\Delta V$:

$$\text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation}$$

$$F_A \big|_{V} - F_A \big|_{V+\Delta V} + r_A \, \Delta V = \frac{dN_A}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to isolate the net outflow:

$$-\Delta F_A + r_A \, \Delta V = \frac{dN_A}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Take the limit as $\Delta V \to 0$:

$$\lim_{\Delta V \to 0}\left(-\frac{\Delta F_A}{\Delta V}\right) = \frac{dN_A}{dt\,dV} - r_A$$

$$-\frac{dF_A}{dV} + r_A = \frac{dN_A}{dt\,dV}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply both sides by $-1$ to express in terms of disappearance:

$$\frac{dF_A}{dV} - r_A = -\frac{dN_A}{dt\,dV}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting the *rate of disappearance* convention $-r_A = -r_A$ (positive quantity) and noting $\frac{dN_A}{dt} = \frac{d(F_A V)}{dt}$ for a constant-volume system:

$$-\frac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A + \frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}$$

**Final GMBE:**

$$\boxed{\frac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A + \frac{1}{V}\frac{dN_A}{dt}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **master equation** from Fogler & Levenspiel. Every specific reactor design equation (BR, CSTR, PFR, PBR) is obtained by imposing physical constraints on this master equation.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Batch Reactor Design Equation

**Step 1:** In a batch reactor, there is no inflow or outflow. Hence $F_A = 0$ at the boundaries, and the system is **unsteady-state**.

The GMBE reduces to:

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A \, V$$

**Step 2:** Express $N_A$ in terms of conversion. By definition:

$$X_A = \frac{N_{A0} - N_A}{N_{A0}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_A = N_{A0}(1 - X_A)$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = -N_{A0}\frac{dX_A}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back into the mole balance:

$$-N_{A0}\frac{dX_A}{dt} = r_A V$$

**Step 5:** Recall $r_A = -(-r_A)$:

$$N_{A0}\frac{dX_A}{dt} = -r_A V$$

**Step 6:** Separate variables and integrate from $t=0$ (where $X_A = 0$) to $t$ (where $X_A = X_{A,f}$):

$$\int_0^t dt = N_{A0}\int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{(-r_A)V}$$

**Final Batch Design Equation:**

$$\boxed{t = N_{A0}\int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{(-r_A)V}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the CSTR Design Equation

**Step 1:** For a CSTR operating at steady state, the contents are perfectly mixed, so the composition inside the reactor equals the exit composition.

**Step 2:** The GMBE reduces to:

$$F_{A0} - F_{A,out} + r_A V = 0$$

**Step 3:** Express $F_{A,out}$ in terms of conversion:

$$F_{A,out} = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and rearrange:

$$F_{A0} - F_{A0}(1 - X_A) = -r_A V$$

$$F_{A0} X_A = -r_A V$$

**Step 5:** Solve for V:

$$\boxed{V = \frac{F_{A0} X_A}{-r_A}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** In the CSTR, the rate $-r_A$ is evaluated at the **outlet** (and hence at the conversion in the reactor), because the entire reactor is at the exit composition. This is why a CSTR always gives a **lower** conversion than a PFR for the same volume and kinetics (the rate is the slowest possible value throughout the reactor).

## 3.4 Derivation of the PFR Design Equation

**Step 1:** For a PFR, steady state with no axial mixing. Consider a differential slice $dV$ at position $z$.

**Step 2:** Apply the GMBE to the slice:

$$F_A - (F_A + dF_A) + r_A \, dV = 0$$

**Step 3:** Express in terms of conversion. Since $F_A = F_{A0}(1-X_A)$:

$$dF_A = -F_{A0}\,dX_A$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$-(-F_{A0}\,dX_A) + r_A\, dV = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{A0}\,dX_A = -r_A\,dV$$

**Step 5:** Separate variables and integrate from inlet ($V=0$, $X_A=0$) to outlet ($V$, $X_A$):

$$F_{A0}\int_0^{X_A} dX_A = \int_0^V (-r_A)\,dV$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to solve for V:

$$\boxed{V = F_{A0}\int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}}$$

## 3.5 Worked Example — Application to a Polymerization System

**Problem (KTU-style):** A free-radical polymerization of styrene is carried out in a **batch reactor** at constant volume. The disappearance of styrene follows **first-order kinetics**: $-r_A = kC_A$ with $k = 0.001\, \text{s}^{-1}$. The initial concentration is $C_{A0} = 5\,\text{kmol/m}^3$. Determine the time required to achieve **80% conversion**.

**Step 1:** Identify the design equation (Batch, constant volume):

$$t = N_{A0}\int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{(-r_A)V}$$

**Step 2:** For a constant-volume batch, $N_{A0} = C_{A0}V$, so:

$$t = C_{A0}V \int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{kC_A V} = \frac{1}{k}\int_0^{X_A} \frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $-r_A = kC_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ and integrate:

$$t = \frac{1}{k} \int_0^{0.8} \frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A}$$

$$t = \frac{1}{k} \big[-\ln(1 - X_A)\big]_0^{0.8}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the limits:

$$t = \frac{1}{0.001}\big[-\ln(1 - 0.8) - (-\ln 1)\big]$$

$$t = 1000 \times [-\ln(0.2)] = 1000 \times 1.6094$$

$$\boxed{t = 1609.4\,\text{s} \approx 26.8\,\text{min}}$$

**Step 5 (Polymer bonus):** If this is a **chain-growth** system, the number-average degree of polymerization at 80% conversion is:

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{[M]_0}{[M]} = \frac{1}{1 - X_A} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5$$

This simple cross-link illustrates how the design equation is **simultaneously** a reactor-sizing tool and a **molecular-weight predictor** — a key polymer-CRE distinction.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Reactor Type vs. Conversion Performance

| Reactor Type | Conversion Equation (1st order, constant V) | Relative Volume to Reach 90% $X_A$ | Best Use in Polymerization |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch** | $t = -\dfrac{1}{k}\ln(1-X_A)$ | (time-based, not volume) | Suspension, emulsion, batch casting |
| **CSTR** | $V = \dfrac{\nu_0 X_A}{kC_{A0}(1-X_A)}$ | $V/\nu_0 = 9/k$ | Continuous solution polymerization |
| **PFR** | $V = -\dfrac{\nu_0}{k}\ln(1-X_A)$ | $V/\nu_0 = 2.303/k$ | Tubular polyethylene, continuous bulk |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Single Reaction System Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Monofunctional Feed FA0"] --> B["Reactor Control Volume V"]
    B --> C["Rate Law -rA k fn CA"]
    B --> D["Mole Balance"]
    C --> E["Design Equation"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Reactor Sizing V or t"]
    E --> G["Conversion XA"]
    E --> H["Degree of Polymerization Xn"]
    H --> I["Polymer Molecular Weight Mn"]
    G --> I
    F --> J["Industrial Scale Output"]
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 Reactor Type Comparison — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Batch["BATCH REACTOR"]
        B1["Charged Monomer C_A0"] --> B2["Reaction Time t"]
        B2 --> B3["Final Conversion X_A"]
    end

    subgraph CSTR["CSTR"]
        C1["Continuous Inlet F_A0"] --> C2["Perfectly Mixed Vessel V"]
        C2 --> C3["Outlet at X_A Single Point Evaluation"]
    end

    subgraph PFR["PFR"]
        P1["Continuous Inlet F_A0"] --> P2["Tubular Differential Slices dV"]
        P2 --> P3["Integration along length to X_A"]
    end

    Batch --> Q["Same Design Equation Different Integration"]
    CSTR --> Q
    PFR --> Q
    Q --> R["Reactor Volume V or Time t"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Reactor Selection in Polymerization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Polymerization System Identified"] --> Q1{"Mechanism?"}
    Q1 -->|"Step-Growth"| Step["Carothers Equation p = XA / Xn = 1 / 1-p"]
    Q1 -->|"Chain-Growth Free Radical"| Chain["Apply Smith-Ewart or Bulk Kinetics"]
    Q1 -->|"Chain-Growth Ionic Coordination"| Coord["Living Polymerization Kinetics"]

    Step --> Rec1{"Reactor Choice?"}
    Chain --> Rec1
    Coord --> Rec1

    Rec1 -->|"High MWD Control"| CSTR
    Rec1 -->|"High Conversion per Volume"| PFR
    Rec1 -->|"Flexible Batch Sizes"| Batch

    CSTR --> Design["Apply Design Equation V = F_A0 XA / -rA"]
    PFR --> Design
    Batch --> Design
    Design --> Output["Reactor Volume and Time Predicted"]
```

## 4.4 Single Reaction System — Mole Balance Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["General Mole Balance"] --> B["Accumulation Term dN/dt"]
    A --> C["Flow Terms F_in minus F_out"]
    A --> D["Generation Term rA V"]

    B --> E1{"Steady or Unsteady?"}
    C --> E2{"Well-Mixed or PFR?"}
    D --> E3{"Single or Multiple Reactions?"}

    E1 -->|"Unsteady"| BR["Batch Reactor Design Equation"]
    E1 -->|"Steady"| FlowReactors["Flow Reactors"]

    E2 -->|"Well-Mixed"| CSTRDesign["CSTR Design Equation"]
    E2 -->|"PFR"| PFRDesign["PFR Design Equation"]

    E3 -->|"Single"| SingleSys["Design Eqn as derived today"]
    E3 -->|"Multiple"| MultipleSys["Selectivity and Yield Analysis Module 2"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
**Define a single reaction system. Why is the concept of a "single" reaction important in reactor design?**

**Model Answer:**
A single reaction system is one in which the progress of reaction can be described by a single independent stoichiometric equation, and hence by a single variable — the conversion $X_A$ of one key reactant. The importance lies in the fact that, for such systems, **selectivity and yield are not variables** (they are either 0% or 100% by definition), so the design equation can be written in a single integrated form relating $V$, $X_A$, $F_{A0}$, and $-r_A$. For multiple reactions, the design must track more than one species, requiring the **selectivity concept** (Module 2).

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Frequent Type]**
**State the general mole balance equation for a chemical species A in a reactor of volume V. Identify each term and its units.**

**Model Answer:**

$$\text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation}$$

$$F_{A0} - F_A + \int_V r_A \, dV = \frac{dN_A}{dt}$$

| Term | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $F_{A0}$ | Molar inflow of A | kmol/s |
| $F_A$ | Molar outflow of A | kmol/s |
| $\int r_A \, dV$ | Net generation | kmol/s |
| $dN_A/dt$ | Accumulation | kmol/s |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the design equation for an isothermal **batch reactor** operating at constant volume for a single reaction system. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A free-radical polymerization of methyl methacrylate is carried out in a constant-volume batch reactor. The rate of disappearance of monomer is given by $-r_A = 0.02\,C_A^2\, \text{kmol/(m}^3\cdot\text{min)}$ with $C_{A0} = 2\,\text{kmol/m}^3$. Calculate the time required to reach 75% conversion. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

1. **Mole balance on species A in batch reactor:** No inflow, no outflow; system is unsteady state.

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A \, V$$

2. **Convert to conversion form:** $X_A = \dfrac{N_{A0} - N_A}{N_{A0}} \Rightarrow N_A = N_{A0}(1-X_A)$

3. **Differentiate:** $\dfrac{dN_A}{dt} = -N_{A0}\dfrac{dX_A}{dt}$

4. **Substitute and rearrange:** $N_{A0}\dfrac{dX_A}{dt} = -r_A \, V$

5. **Integrate:** $t = N_{A0}\displaystyle\int_0^{X_A} \dfrac{dX_A}{(-r_A)V}$

6. **For constant V:** Simplify to $t = C_{A0}\displaystyle\int_0^{X_A} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Substitute $-r_A = 0.02\,C_A^2$ with $C_A = C_{A0}(1-X_A) = 2(1-X_A)$:

$$t = \int_0^{0.75} \frac{2\,dX_A}{0.02 \times [2(1-X_A)]^2} = \int_0^{0.75} \frac{2\,dX_A}{0.08(1-X_A)^2}$$

**Step 2:** Pull out constants:

$$t = \frac{2}{0.08}\int_0^{0.75} \frac{dX_A}{(1-X_A)^2} = 25\int_0^{0.75} (1-X_A)^{-2}\,dX_A$$

**Step 3:** Integrate:

$$\int (1-X_A)^{-2} dX_A = \frac{1}{1-X_A}$$

**Step 4:** Apply limits:

$$t = 25\left[\frac{1}{1-0.75} - \frac{1}{1-0}\right] = 25[4 - 1] = 75 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{t = 75\,\text{minutes}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the design equation for an ideal **CSTR** and an ideal **PFR** for a single reaction. Show clearly the assumptions used. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Vinyl acetate is polymerized in a CSTR at steady state. The feed contains $5\,\text{kmol/m}^3$ vinyl acetate at $1\,\text{m}^3/\text{min}$. The rate law is $-r_A = 0.05\,C_A\,\text{min}^{-1}$. Calculate the CSTR volume required for 60% conversion. Compare the volume with a PFR delivering the same conversion. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**CSTR Derivation:**
- Assumptions: steady state, perfect mixing, single inlet/outlet.
- Mole balance: $F_{A0} - F_{A,out} + r_A V = 0$
- Express in terms of conversion: $F_{A0}X_A = -r_A V$
- Final equation: $V = \dfrac{F_{A0}X_A}{-r_A}$ *(evaluated at outlet composition)*

**PFR Derivation:**
- Assumptions: steady state, no axial mixing, plug flow, radial uniformity.
- Differential balance on slice $dV$: $F_{A0}\,dX_A = -r_A\,dV$
- Integrate: $V = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int_0^{X_A} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**CSTR Volume:**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters: $F_{A0} = C_{A0} \cdot \nu_0 = 5 \times 1 = 5\,\text{kmol/min}$, $X_A = 0.60$, $k = 0.05\,\text{min}^{-1}$.

**Step 2:** Outlet concentration: $C_{A,out} = C_{A0}(1-X_A) = 5(0.4) = 2\,\text{kmol/m}^3$.

**Step 3:** Rate at outlet: $-r_A = 0.05 \times 2 = 0.10\,\text{kmol/(m}^3\cdot\text{min)}$

**Step 4:** Apply design equation:

$$V_{CSTR} = \frac{F_{A0}X_A}{-r_A} = \frac{5 \times 0.60}{0.10} = 30\,\text{m}^3$$

**PFR Volume:**

**Step 1:** Apply PFR design equation for first-order kinetics:

$$V_{PFR} = \frac{\nu_0}{k}\left[-\ln(1-X_A)\right] = \frac{1}{0.05}\left[-\ln(0.4)\right]$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate:

$$V_{PFR} = 20 \times 0.9163 = 18.33\,\text{m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Comparison:

$$\frac{V_{CSTR}}{V_{PFR}} = \frac{30}{18.33} = 1.64$$

**Conclusion:** The CSTR requires **1.64 times** the volume of a PFR to achieve the same 60% conversion. This is the classic KTU illustration of why PFRs are preferred for first-order single reactions, but CSTRs are sometimes chosen for **temperature control** in highly exothermic polymerizations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $-r_A$:** The rate of *disappearance* is positive, so write $-r_A$, not $r_A$. Marks deducted if sign is wrong.
> 2. **Mixing up the CSTR rate evaluation:** Students often evaluate $-r_A$ at $C_{A0}$ (inlet) instead of at the outlet concentration. The CSTR is well-mixed, so it operates at the **lowest rate** in the system — use the **outlet** concentration.
> 3. **Ignoring the "constant volume" assumption:** For non-constant-volume systems, the $\varepsilon$ term must be included in the design equation. KTU questions often test this.
> 4. **Confusing conversion $X_A$ with extent of reaction $p$:** In step-growth polymerization, $p$ refers to functional-group conversion. The two are numerically equal **only** for equimolar feeds.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert time units:** When $k$ is in $\text{min}^{-1}$ and $V$ is in $\text{m}^3$, the time answer will be in minutes — be consistent.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and pin it to your study wall!**

- ✅ **Single reaction system** = one stoichiometric equation, one conversion variable $X_A$.
- ✅ **General Mole Balance (GMBE):** $F_{A0} - F_A + \int r_A\,dV = dN_A/dt$
- ✅ **Batch design equation:** $t = N_{A0}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{dX_A}{(-r_A)V}$ — for **unsteady** reactors.
- ✅ **CSTR design equation:** $V = \dfrac{F_{A0}X_A}{-r_A}$ — evaluate $-r_A$ at the **outlet**.
- ✅ **PFR design equation:** $V = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ — evaluate $-r_A$ as a function of $X_A$.
- ✅ **First-order batch solution:** $t = -\dfrac{1}{k}\ln(1-X_A)$
- ✅ **First-order CSTR solution:** $V = \dfrac{\nu_0 X_A}{kC_{A0}(1-X_A)}$
- ✅ **First-order PFR solution:** $V = -\dfrac{\nu_0}{k}\ln(1-X_A)$
- ✅ **For same $X_A$, PFR volume < CSTR volume** (always, for $n > 0$ kinetics).
- ✅ **Polymer link (Step-Growth):** $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ (Carothers).
- ✅ **Polymer link (Chain-Growth):** $\bar{X}_n$ depends on ratio of propagation to termination rate constants.
- ✅ **Remember the gel effect** in free-radical polymerization — it makes $-r_A$ a strong function of $X_A$ at high conversion.
- ✅ **Always carry units** in the design equation and **check unit consistency** at the end of every numerical problem.
- ✅ **KTU's favorite trick question:** "What if $\nu \neq \nu_0$?" — Answer: introduce $\varepsilon$ and modify the design equation accordingly.

---

> **END OF MODULE 1 — DESIGN EQUATION FOR SINGLE REACTION SYSTEM**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme — PEPOT745 Polymer Reaction Engineering*
