topics.title: Parallel and Series Reactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT745 - POLYMER REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parallel and Series Reactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Parallel Reactions — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parallel (Competing) Reactions:** A reaction network in which a single reactant species undergoes **two or more simultaneous, independent reaction pathways** to yield different products. In polymer reaction engineering, the most important parallel reactions are **chain transfer vs. propagation**, **combination vs. disproportionation termination**, and **branching vs. linear chain growth**.

For the simplest case in polymer engineering context (e.g., a radical that may either propagate or undergo chain transfer):

$$A \xrightarrow{k_1} R \quad (\text{desired, e.g., propagation})$$

$$A \xrightarrow{k_2} S \quad (\text{undesired, e.g., chain transfer})$$

The overall disappearance rate of A is the sum of the two parallel paths:

$$-r_A = r_R + r_S = k_1 C_A + k_2 C_A = (k_1 + k_2) C_A$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Polymer Engineering Interpretation:** The branching ratio $k_1 / k_2$ determines whether we get high-molecular-weight polymer (propagation dominant) or low-molecular-weight oligomer (transfer dominant). This is the kinetic foundation of the **Mayo–Lewis equation** for chain transfer.

---

### 1.2 Series (Consecutive) Reactions — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Series / Consecutive Reactions:** A reaction sequence in which the product of one reaction becomes the reactant for the next. In polymer engineering, this is the heart of **free-radical polymerization kinetics**: Initiation $\rightarrow$ Propagation $\rightarrow$ Termination, and also the basis of **polymer degradation** (chain scission) and **step-growth polymerization** (monomer $\rightarrow$ dimer $\rightarrow$ trimer $\rightarrow$ … $\rightarrow$ polymer).

$$A \xrightarrow{k_1} R \xrightarrow{k_2} S$$

- **A** = initiator-derived radical (or active chain end)
- **R** = propagating radical (or growing polymer chain) — *the desired intermediate*
- **S** = dead polymer (terminated chain) — *final product*

$$-r_A = k_1 C_A, \quad r_R = k_1 C_A - k_2 C_R, \quad r_S = k_2 C_R$$

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Two Roads from Mountain Peak (A) to Two Towns (R and S):**
> Imagine a hiker at peak **A**. Two downhill paths exist — one short path to town **R** (desirable, high polymer) and one long path to town **S** (waste, oligomer). The faster path "wins" most of the time, but the hiker chooses between them. **Parallel kinetics** is exactly this — reactant molecules choose between competing routes, and selectivity is fixed by $k_1/k_2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Assembly Line (Series):**
> A car factory has three sequential stations: **A** (frame), **R** (painted frame), **S** (finished car). A frame spends time at station 1 (rate $k_1$), moves to station 2 (rate $k_2$). If station 1 is very slow, frames pile up at start; if station 2 is slow, painted frames pile up between stations — the intermediate concentration **R peaks** at some time. This is why batch polymerizations show a maximum radical concentration.

---

### 1.4 Standard Polymer-Engineering Metrics (in **bold**)

- **Selectivity** $S = r_R / r_S$ — ratio of desired to undesired rates
- **Yield / Fractional Conversion** $X_A$ — fraction of A consumed
- **Overall fractional yield of R** $\Phi_R = C_R / C_{A,0}$ — polymer produced per initiator
- **Number-average degree of polymerization** $\overline{X}_n$ — directly affected by $k_2/k_1$ ratio in series case
- **Rate constant ratio** $k_2/k_1$ — the central design parameter

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Parallel Reaction Analysis — Step by Step

**Step 1: Stoichiometric network.** Reactant A splits into two products R (desired) and S (undesired). Both reactions are irreversible and occur in the same phase.

**Step 2: Rate expressions (both first order in A).**
$$-r_A = (k_1 + k_2) C_A, \quad r_R = k_1 C_A, \quad r_S = k_2 C_A$$

**Step 3: Instantaneous selectivity** is the ratio of formation rates at any moment.
$$S = \frac{r_R}{r_S} = \frac{k_1}{k_2}$$

**Why this matters:** The selectivity is **independent of $C_A$** when both reactions are first order. If the orders differ (e.g., propagation first order, transfer zero-order in monomer at high $[M]$), selectivity becomes concentration-dependent — a key reason why polymer molecular weight varies with conversion.

**Step 4: Batch reactor design equation.**
$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -(k_1+k_2) C_A \;\Rightarrow\; C_A = C_{A,0}\, e^{-(k_1+k_2)t}$$

**Step 5: Overall fractional yield of R in batch.**
$$\Phi_R = \frac{C_R}{C_{A,0}} = \frac{k_1}{k_1+k_2}\left[1 - e^{-(k_1+k_2)t}\right]$$

**Step 6: CSTR analysis.** At steady state:
$$\tau = \frac{C_{A,0} - C_A}{(k_1+k_2) C_A} \;\Rightarrow\; C_A = \frac{C_{A,0}}{1 + (k_1+k_2)\tau}$$
$$\Phi_R^{CSTR} = \frac{k_1 \tau}{1 + (k_1+k_2)\tau}$$

**Step 7: PFR analysis.** Integrating the design equation $\tau = \int_{C_{A,0}}^{C_A} dC_A / (-r_A)$:
$$C_A = C_{A,0}\, e^{-(k_1+k_2)\tau}, \quad \Phi_R^{PFR} = \frac{k_1}{k_1+k_2}\left[1 - e^{-(k_1+k_2)\tau}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The **maximum yield of desired product R is bounded by $k_1/(k_1+k_2)$** as $t \to \infty$ (batch) or $\tau \to \infty$ (PFR). You can never convert 100% of A to R if there is a parallel side reaction. This is why catalyst/condition optimization in chain-transfer-dominated polymerizations is critical.

---

### 2.2 Series Reaction Analysis — Step by Step

**Step 1: Mass balances for the three species in a batch reactor.**
$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -k_1 C_A$$
$$\frac{dC_R}{dt} = k_1 C_A - k_2 C_R$$
$$\frac{dC_S}{dt} = k_2 C_R$$

**Step 2: Solution for A.** Simple first-order decay:
$$C_A = C_{A,0}\, e^{-k_1 t}$$

**Step 3: Solution for R** (by substituting $C_A$ and solving the linear ODE):
$$C_R = \frac{k_1 C_{A,0}}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right), \quad k_1 \neq k_2$$

**Step 4: Solution for S** (by material balance $C_S = C_{A,0} - C_A - C_R$):
$$C_S = C_{A,0}\left[1 - \frac{k_2}{k_2-k_1} e^{-k_1 t} + \frac{k_1}{k_2-k_1} e^{-k_2 t}\right]$$

**Step 5: Maximum R and its location.** Differentiating $C_R(t)$ and setting to zero:
$$t_{\max} = \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{k_2 - k_1}$$
$$C_{R,\max} = C_{A,0}\left(\frac{k_1}{k_2}\right)^{k_1/(k_2-k_1)}$$

**Step 6: Polymer engineering link — termination timing.** In free-radical polymerization, $k_2$ is the termination rate constant. The maximum radical concentration $C_{R,\max}$ determines the polymerization rate $R_p = k_p [M][R^\bullet]$. Reactor design aims to operate **near or below** $t_{\max}$ to maximize radical flux.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Condition |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | Parallel — overall rate of A consumption | $-r_A = (k_1+k_2) C_A$ | $\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}\,s^{-1}}$ | Both rxns 1st order in A |
| 2 | Parallel — instantaneous selectivity | $S = r_R/r_S = k_1/k_2$ | dimensionless | Same reaction order |
| 3 | Parallel — batch yield of R | $\Phi_R = \dfrac{k_1}{k_1+k_2}\left[1 - e^{-(k_1+k_2)t}\right]$ | dimensionless | Batch, 1st order |
| 4 | Parallel — CSTR yield of R | $\Phi_R^{CSTR} = \dfrac{k_1\tau}{1+(k_1+k_2)\tau}$ | dimensionless | Steady-state CSTR |
| 5 | Parallel — maximum attainable $\Phi_R$ | $\Phi_R^{\infty} = k_1/(k_1+k_2)$ | dimensionless | As $t \to \infty$ or $\tau \to \infty$ |
| 6 | Series — A concentration vs. time | $C_A = C_{A,0}\, e^{-k_1 t}$ | $\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$ | Batch, 1st order |
| 7 | Series — R concentration vs. time | $C_R = \dfrac{k_1 C_{A,0}}{k_2-k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right)$ | $\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$ | $k_1 \neq k_2$, batch |
| 8 | Series — S concentration vs. time | $C_S = C_{A,0}\!\left[1 - \dfrac{k_2\, e^{-k_1 t} - k_1\, e^{-k_2 t}}{k_2-k_1}\right]$ | $\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$ | Material balance |
| 9 | Series — time of $C_{R,\max}$ | $t_{\max} = \ln(k_2/k_1)/(k_2-k_1)$ | s | $k_2 > k_1$ |
| 10 | Series — maximum R | $C_{R,\max} = C_{A,0}\,(k_1/k_2)^{k_1/(k_2-k_1)}$ | $\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$ | Batch, $k_1 \neq k_2$ |
| 11 | Series — special case $k_1 = k_2$ | $C_R = k_1 C_{A,0}\, t\, e^{-k_1 t}$ | $\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$ | $k_1 = k_2$ |
| 12 | Special case location when $k_1=k_2$ | $t_{\max} = 1/k_1$ | s | $k_1 = k_2$ |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Polymer Engineering

- **Reactor type selection:** PFR gives **higher** yield of intermediate R in series reactions than CSTR at the same $\tau$ (Levenspiel plot). This is why **plug-flow tubular reactors are preferred** for polymerizations where active chain lifetime must be controlled.
- **Chain transfer control:** Lowering $k_2$ (transfer constant) is achieved by choosing low-transfer solvents — directly increases molecular weight.
- **Initiator half-life design:** $k_1$ in the series framework maps to initiator decomposition; matching $k_1$ and $k_2$ avoids radical starvation.
- **Degradation kinetics:** Polymer thermal degradation follows first-order series kinetics — used to predict service life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Parallel Reaction, Batch Yield of Desired Product R

We start with two parallel, irreversible, first-order reactions:
$$A \xrightarrow{k_1} R, \qquad A \xrightarrow{k_2} S$$

**Step 1: Overall rate of disappearance of A.** Summing the two parallel contributions:

$$-r_A = k_1 C_A + k_2 C_A = (k_1 + k_2) C_A$$

*Logic:* Each path consumes A independently, so rates add. The effective first-order rate constant is $k_{\text{eff}} = k_1 + k_2$.

**Step 2: Batch mole balance on A.**

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -(k_1+k_2) C_A$$

**Step 3: Separate and integrate** with $C_A(0) = C_{A,0}$:

$$\int_{C_{A,0}}^{C_A} \frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -(k_1+k_2)\int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_A}{C_{A,0}}\right) = -(k_1+k_2)\, t \;\Rightarrow\; C_A = C_{A,0}\, e^{-(k_1+k_2)t}$$

**Step 4: Mole balance on R** (it is produced by path 1, not consumed):

$$\frac{dC_R}{dt} = r_R = k_1 C_A = k_1 C_{A,0}\, e^{-(k_1+k_2)t}$$

**Step 5: Integrate** with $C_R(0) = 0$:

$$C_R = k_1 C_{A,0} \int_0^t e^{-(k_1+k_2)t}\, dt = k_1 C_{A,0} \left[\frac{1 - e^{-(k_1+k_2)t}}{k_1+k_2}\right]$$

**Step 6: Fractional yield of R.**

$$\Phi_R \equiv \frac{C_R}{C_{A,0}} = \frac{k_1}{k_1+k_2}\left[1 - e^{-(k_1+k_2)t}\right]$$

**Step 7: Limiting behavior.**
$$\lim_{t \to \infty} \Phi_R = \frac{k_1}{k_1+k_2}$$

*Logic:* The fraction of A that ever becomes R is fixed by the kinetic branching ratio. No reactor configuration can overcome this ceiling.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Series Reaction, Time of Maximum Intermediate R

For the reaction $A \xrightarrow{k_1} R \xrightarrow{k_2} S$ in a batch reactor:

**Step 1: Mass balance on A** (simple first-order decay):

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -k_1 C_A \;\Rightarrow\; C_A(t) = C_{A,0}\, e^{-k_1 t}$$

**Step 2: Mass balance on R** (formed from A, consumed to S):

$$\frac{dC_R}{dt} = k_1 C_A - k_2 C_R = k_1 C_{A,0}\, e^{-k_1 t} - k_2 C_R$$

**Step 3: Solve linear first-order ODE** using integrating factor $e^{k_2 t}$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(C_R\, e^{k_2 t}\right) = k_1 C_{A,0}\, e^{(k_2-k_1)t}$$

**Step 4: Integrate** with $C_R(0) = 0$:

$$C_R\, e^{k_2 t} - 0 = k_1 C_{A,0}\int_0^t e^{(k_2-k_1)t'}\, dt'$$

**Step 5: Evaluate integral** for $k_2 \neq k_1$:

$$C_R\, e^{k_2 t} = k_1 C_{A,0} \left[\frac{e^{(k_2-k_1)t} - 1}{k_2 - k_1}\right]$$

**Step 6: Multiply both sides by $e^{-k_2 t}$:**

$$C_R(t) = \frac{k_1 C_{A,0}}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right)$$

**Step 7: Locate the maximum.** Set $dC_R/dt = 0$:

$$\frac{dC_R}{dt} = k_1 C_A - k_2 C_R = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; k_1 C_A = k_2 C_R$$

**Step 8: Substitute the solutions** for $C_A$ and $C_R$:

$$k_1 C_{A,0}\, e^{-k_1 t_{\max}} = k_2 \cdot \frac{k_1 C_{A,0}}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t_{\max}} - e^{-k_2 t_{\max}}\right)$$

**Step 9: Cancel $k_1 C_{A,0}$** and rearrange:

$$(k_2 - k_1)\, e^{-k_1 t_{\max}} = k_2\left(e^{-k_1 t_{\max}} - e^{-k_2 t_{\max}}\right)$$

$$k_2 e^{-k_1 t_{\max}} - k_1 e^{-k_1 t_{\max}} = k_2 e^{-k_1 t_{\max}} - k_2 e^{-k_2 t_{\max}}$$

$$-k_1 e^{-k_1 t_{\max}} = -k_2 e^{-k_2 t_{\max}}$$

**Step 10: Rearrange to isolate the time:**

$$\frac{e^{-k_1 t_{\max}}}{e^{-k_2 t_{\max}}} = \frac{k_2}{k_1} \;\Rightarrow\; e^{(k_2-k_1)t_{\max}} = \frac{k_2}{k_1}$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{\max} = \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{k_2 - k_1}\,}$$

**Step 11: Maximum concentration.** Substitute $t_{\max}$ back into $C_R(t)$:

Let $\alpha \equiv k_1/(k_2-k_1)$. Then $e^{-k_1 t_{\max}} = (k_1/k_2)^{k_1/(k_2-k_1)}$ and $e^{-k_2 t_{\max}} = (k_1/k_2)^{k_2/(k_2-k_1)}$. Their difference is:

$$e^{-k_1 t_{\max}} - e^{-k_2 t_{\max}} = \left(\frac{k_1}{k_2}\right)^{k_1/(k_2-k_1)}\left[1 - \frac{k_1}{k_2}\right] = \left(\frac{k_1}{k_2}\right)^{k_1/(k_2-k_1)} \cdot \frac{k_2-k_1}{k_2}$$

Therefore:

$$C_{R,\max} = \frac{k_1 C_{A,0}}{k_2 - k_1} \cdot \left(\frac{k_1}{k_2}\right)^{k_1/(k_2-k_1)} \cdot \frac{k_2-k_1}{k_2}$$

$$\boxed{\,C_{R,\max} = C_{A,0}\left(\frac{k_1}{k_2}\right)^{k_1/(k_2-k_1)}\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-style numerical)

**Problem:** A polymerization initiator decomposes by two parallel first-order paths: $A \to R$ (productive, $k_1 = 0.05\,\mathrm{s^{-1}}$) and $A \to S$ (waste, $k_2 = 0.02\,\mathrm{s^{-1}}$). $C_{A,0} = 1.0\,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$. Find (a) the maximum theoretical yield of R, (b) $C_A$ and $C_R$ at $t = 10\,\mathrm{s}$, (c) compare with a CSTR of $\tau = 10\,\mathrm{s}$.

**Solution (a):**
$$\Phi_R^{\max} = \frac{k_1}{k_1+k_2} = \frac{0.05}{0.07} = 0.714 \;\;(\text{i.e., } 71.4\%)$$

**Solution (b):**
$$C_A(10) = 1.0\, e^{-0.07 \times 10} = 1.0\, e^{-0.7} = 0.4966\,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$$
$$C_R(10) = \frac{0.05}{0.07}\left(1.0\right)\left[1 - e^{-0.7}\right] = 0.7143 \times (1 - 0.4966) = 0.3596\,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$$
$$\Phi_R^{batch} = 0.3596 \;\;(\text{or } 35.96\%)$$

**Solution (c): CSTR at $\tau = 10\,\mathrm{s}$.**
$$C_A = \frac{C_{A,0}}{1 + (k_1+k_2)\tau} = \frac{1.0}{1 + 0.07 \times 10} = \frac{1.0}{1.7} = 0.5882\,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$$
$$\Phi_R^{CSTR} = \frac{k_1\tau}{1+(k_1+k_2)\tau} = \frac{0.05 \times 10}{1.7} = \frac{0.5}{1.7} = 0.2941 \;\;(\text{i.e., } 29.41\%)$$

**Comparison:** Batch at 10 s gives 35.96% yield, CSTR gives 29.41%. Batch outperforms CSTR for parallel reactions when total conversion is low, because the batch begins at high $C_A$ where both rates are maximum. At long times both approach the same asymptote 71.4%.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Parallel Reaction Network — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Reactant A] --> B1{Path Selection}
    B1 -->|k1 desired| C1[Product R]
    B1 -->|k2 undesired| D1[Product S]
    C1 -.->|Selectivity k1/k2| E1[Reactor Design]
    D1 -.->|Side reaction| E1
    E1 --> F1[Batch yields up to 71.4 percent]
    E1 --> G1[PFR yields up to 71.4 percent]
    E1 --> H1[CSTR yields up to 71.4 percent]
```

### 4.2 Series Reaction Network — Functional Flow with Polymerization Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Initiator A] -->|k1 Initiation| B2[Active Radical R]
    B2 -->|k2 Termination| C2[Dead Polymer S]
    B2 -.->|k1=k2 special| D2[Peak Radical at t equals 1 over k1]
    C2 --> E2[Final Molecular Weight]
    D2 --> E2
    E2 --> F2[Reactor PFR preferred]
    E2 --> G2[CSTR gives lower R yield]
```

### 4.3 Concentration Profile in a Batch Reactor (Series Reactions)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start of Reaction] --> P1[A decays exponentially]
    P1 --> P2[R rises and reaches maximum at tmax]
    P2 --> P3[R then decays]
    P3 --> P4[S rises monotonically and approaches CA0]
    P4 --> P5[Material balance verified]
```

### 4.4 Reactor Selection Logic for Polymerization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Identify Reaction Network] --> Q2{Parallel or Series}
    Q2 -->|Parallel| Q3[Goal maximize R]
    Q3 --> Q4[Select PFR or Batch at high CA0]
    Q2 -->|Series| Q5[Goal maximize intermediate R]
    Q5 --> Q6[Select PFR with residence time near tmax]
    Q6 --> Q7[Avoid long CSTR low R yield]
```

### 4.5 Comparative Yield Diagram (PFR vs CSTR for Series)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[PFR Series yield] --> R2[Always greater or equal to CSTR]
    R2 --> R3[Maximized when tau equals tmax]
    C1[CSTR Series yield] --> C2[Lower because of backmixing]
    C2 --> C3[Lowest intermediate R yield]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B1`, `P0`). Labels are plain uppercase text without markdown bolding or HTML tables, in compliance with the rendering safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
Define parallel reactions with a suitable example from polymer reaction engineering. Derive the expression for the maximum fractional yield of the desired product.

**Model Answer:**
Parallel reactions are competing reactions in which a single reactant undergoes two or more simultaneous pathways to form different products. In polymer reaction engineering, an example is the **propagation versus chain transfer** of a growing radical — the radical may either add monomer (propagation, $k_p$) or abstract a hydrogen from solvent/transfer agent (chain transfer, $k_{tr}$).

The overall disappearance rate of A is:
$$-r_A = (k_1 + k_2) C_A$$

The fractional yield of R in a batch reactor, as $t \to \infty$, is:
$$\Phi_R^{\infty} = \frac{k_1}{k_1 + k_2}$$

Thus the maximum attainable yield of the desired product is fixed by the ratio of the rate constants, regardless of reactor type.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
For a first-order series reaction $A \xrightarrow{k_1} R \xrightarrow{k_2} S$, obtain the expression for the time at which the concentration of R is maximum.

**Model Answer:**
The concentration of R in a batch reactor is:
$$C_R = \frac{k_1 C_{A,0}}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right)$$

Setting $dC_R/dt = 0$ yields $k_1 C_A = k_2 C_R$. Substituting the time-dependence and solving gives:
$$t_{\max} = \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{k_2 - k_1}$$

The corresponding maximum value is:
$$C_{R,\max} = C_{A,0}\left(\frac{k_1}{k_2}\right)^{k_1/(k_2-k_1)}$$

---

### Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1(a) Module 1]**

**(a)** For the parallel reaction scheme $A \xrightarrow{k_1} R$ (desired) and $A \xrightarrow{k_2} S$ (undesired), both first order in A, derive the integrated form of $C_A$ versus time and $C_R$ versus time in a **batch reactor**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A CSTR is operated at steady state with $\tau = 20\,\mathrm{s}$ for the same parallel scheme with $k_1 = 0.10\,\mathrm{s^{-1}}$, $k_2 = 0.05\,\mathrm{s^{-1}}$, $C_{A,0} = 2.0\,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$. Calculate the outlet concentrations of A, R, and S, and the fractional yield of R. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution (a)

**Step 1: Write rate expressions.**
$$-r_A = (k_1 + k_2) C_A, \quad r_R = k_1 C_A$$

**Step 2: Batch mole balance on A.**

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -(k_1 + k_2) C_A$$

**Step 3: Integrate** with $C_A(0) = C_{A,0}$:

$$C_A(t) = C_{A,0}\, e^{-(k_1+k_2)t}$$

**Step 4: Substitute into R balance.**

$$\frac{dC_R}{dt} = k_1 C_{A,0}\, e^{-(k_1+k_2)t}$$

**Step 5: Integrate** with $C_R(0) = 0$:

$$C_R(t) = \frac{k_1 C_{A,0}}{k_1 + k_2}\left[1 - e^{-(k_1+k_2)t}\right]$$

**Step 6: Comment.** As $t \to \infty$, $C_R \to k_1 C_{A,0}/(k_1+k_2)$, the **maximum theoretical yield**.

---

#### Model Solution (b)

**Step 1: Steady-state CSTR balance on A.**

$$\tau = \frac{C_{A,0} - C_A}{(k_1+k_2) C_A} \;\Rightarrow\; C_A = \frac{C_{A,0}}{1 + (k_1+k_2)\tau}$$

Substitute values: $(k_1+k_2)\tau = 0.15 \times 20 = 3.0$.

$$C_A = \frac{2.0}{1 + 3.0} = \frac{2.0}{4.0} = 0.50\,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$$

**Step 2: Steady-state balance on R.**

$$0 = k_1 C_A - \frac{C_R}{\tau} \;\Rightarrow\; C_R = k_1 \tau\, C_A = 0.10 \times 20 \times 0.50 = 1.00\,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$$

**Step 3: Balance on S** by total material balance:

$$C_S = C_{A,0} - C_A - C_R = 2.0 - 0.50 - 1.00 = 0.50\,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$$

**Step 4: Fractional yield of R.**

$$\Phi_R = \frac{C_R}{C_{A,0}} = \frac{1.00}{2.0} = 0.50 \;\;(\text{i.e., } 50\%)$$

**Step 5: Sanity check.** Maximum possible $\Phi_R = k_1/(k_1+k_2) = 0.10/0.15 = 66.7\%$. The CSTR achieves 50%, leaving headroom for design improvement (e.g., PFR or batch would push higher).

---

### Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1(b) Module 1]**

**(a)** For the first-order series reaction $A \xrightarrow{k_1} R \xrightarrow{k_2} S$ in a batch reactor, derive the integrated expressions for $C_A(t)$, $C_R(t)$, and $C_S(t)$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For $k_1 = 0.20\,\mathrm{min^{-1}}$, $k_2 = 0.05\,\mathrm{min^{-1}}$, and $C_{A,0} = 0.5\,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$, compute $t_{\max}$, $C_{R,\max}$, and the conversion of A at $t_{\max}$. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution (a)

**Step 1: Mass balance on A.**

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -k_1 C_A \;\Rightarrow\; C_A = C_{A,0}\, e^{-k_1 t}$$

**Step 2: Mass balance on R.**

$$\frac{dC_R}{dt} = k_1 C_A - k_2 C_R$$

**Step 3: Multiply by integrating factor $e^{k_2 t}$.**

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(C_R\, e^{k_2 t}\right) = k_1 C_{A,0}\, e^{(k_2-k_1)t}$$

**Step 4: Integrate from 0 to t** with $C_R(0) = 0$:

$$C_R\, e^{k_2 t} = \frac{k_1 C_{A,0}}{k_2 - k_1}\left[e^{(k_2-k_1)t} - 1\right]$$

$$C_R(t) = \frac{k_1 C_{A,0}}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right)$$

**Step 5: Mass balance on S** by closure:

$$C_S(t) = C_{A,0} - C_A(t) - C_R(t) = C_{A,0}\left[1 - \frac{k_2\, e^{-k_1 t} - k_1\, e^{-k_2 t}}{k_2 - k_1}\right]$$

---

#### Model Solution (b)

**Step 1: Compute $t_{\max}$.**

$$t_{\max} = \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{k_2 - k_1} = \frac{\ln(0.05/0.20)}{0.05 - 0.20} = \frac{\ln(0.25)}{-0.15} = \frac{-1.3863}{-0.15} = 9.24\,\mathrm{min}$$

**Step 2: Compute $C_{R,\max}$.**

The exponent is $k_1/(k_2-k_1) = 0.20/(-0.15) = -1.3333$.

$$C_{R,\max} = 0.5 \times (0.05/0.20)^{-1.3333} = 0.5 \times (0.25)^{-1.3333}$$

Compute $(0.25)^{-1.3333} = e^{-1.3333 \times \ln(0.25)} = e^{-1.3333 \times (-1.3863)} = e^{1.8484} = 6.351$

$$C_{R,\max} = 0.5 \times 6.351 = 3.176\,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$$

*Note:* This value exceeds $C_{A,0}$ because the original derivation assumes $C_R$ accumulates beyond the initial A. The physically meaningful value is the **fraction** relative to $C_{A,0}$. For a series reaction where $k_1 \gg k_2$, the peak of R as a fraction of A is bounded by 1. Using the standard textbook convention, report the dimensionless form $C_{R,\max}/C_{A,0} = 6.351 \times 10^{-1} = 0.6351$ — but for the given numbers, $k_1 > k_2$ means R is consumed faster than formed. Recompute:

For $k_1 = 0.20$, $k_2 = 0.05$: $k_1 > k_2$ so $k_2-k_1 = -0.15$ and $k_1/(k_2-k_1) = -1.333$. Then $(k_1/k_2)^{k_1/(k_2-k_1)} = 4^{-1.333} = 0.0992$. Hence $C_{R,\max}/C_{A,0} = 0.0992$, i.e., $C_{R,\max} = 0.0496\,\mathrm{mol\,L^{-1}}$.

*Important:* The dimensionless ratio convention $C_{R,\max}/C_{A,0} = (k_1/k_2)^{k_1/(k_2-k_1)}$ always yields a value between 0 and 1 when properly interpreted with sign-correct exponents.

**Step 3: Conversion of A at $t_{\max}$.**

$$X_A = 1 - \frac{C_A(t_{\max})}{C_{A,0}} = 1 - e^{-k_1 t_{\max}} = 1 - e^{-0.20 \times 9.24} = 1 - e^{-1.848} = 1 - 0.1576 = 0.8424$$

**Step 4: Polymer engineering interpretation.** At $t_{\max} = 9.24$ min, the active radical (intermediate R) reaches its maximum value. Beyond this, termination dominates and the active radical population declines — so the reactor should ideally operate at residence times near or below $t_{\max}$ for maximum radical flux and hence maximum polymerization rate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in series derivation:** When $k_1 \neq k_2$, students often write $C_R$ as a positive numerator over $k_1 - k_2$ instead of $k_2 - k_1$, causing a sign error in $t_{\max}$. Always re-derive step-by-step.
> 2. **Forgetting the $k_1 = k_2$ special case:** The general formula $t_{\max} = \ln(k_2/k_1)/(k_2-k_1)$ is **undefined** when $k_1 = k_2$. You must use $t_{\max} = 1/k_1$ separately. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for missing this.
> 3. **Confusing yield and selectivity:** Yield is $C_R/C_{A,0}$; selectivity is $r_R/r_S$. They are different quantities, and the KTU paper often swaps them in question phrasing.
> 4. **Forgetting the asymptotic limit for parallel reactions:** Students report $C_R$ at finite $t$ as the "maximum," but the true ceiling is $k_1 C_{A,0}/(k_1+k_2)$ as $t \to \infty$.
> 5. **Mixing reactor designs:** A question may say "CSTR" while the student writes the PFR design equation $\tau = \int dC_A/(-r_A)$. Always re-read the question stem.
> 6. **Units inconsistency:** $k$ in $\mathrm{s^{-1}}$ requires time in seconds, not minutes. Watch the units consistently.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Parallel reaction** = one reactant, multiple products; rate of A disappearance is sum of individual path rates.
- **Maximum fractional yield of R in parallel** = $k_1/(k_1+k_2)$; this is the **asymptotic ceiling** no reactor can surpass.
- **Selectivity** $S = k_1/k_2$ is **concentration-independent only when both reactions have the same order** in A.
- **Series reaction** = product of one step is reactant of the next; intermediate R has a **maximum** at $t_{\max} = \ln(k_2/k_1)/(k_2-k_1)$.
- **Special case $k_1 = k_2$:** $C_R = k_1 C_{A,0} t\, e^{-k_1 t}$ and $t_{\max} = 1/k_1$.
- **Reactor selection:** PFR (or batch) gives higher R yield than CSTR for series reactions — backmixing in CSTR destroys the intermediate.
- **Polymer engineering link:**
  - Free-radical polymerization: A = initiator, R = active radical, S = dead polymer.
  - Chain transfer: parallel reaction that reduces molecular weight.
  - Polymer degradation: series scission kinetics.
  - Step-growth polymerization: series of condensation reactions (monomer $\to$ dimer $\to$ trimer $\to$ ...).
- **Closure relation:** $C_A + C_R + C_S = C_{A,0}$ (constant mass, no volume change) is a **powerful check** in every series-reaction problem.
- **Order of magnitude check:** $C_{R,\max}$ is typically a small fraction of $C_{A,0}$ when $k_1 \ll k_2$ (fast decay), and approaches $C_{A,0}/e \approx 0.368\, C_{A,0}$ when $k_1 = k_2$.
- **Exam mantra:** "Sum the rates for parallel; integrate sequentially for series."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Dependent Terms
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT745 - POLYMER REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Temperature Dependent Terms

# 🌡️ Temperature Dependent Terms in Polymer Reaction Engineering

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Dependent Terms** in Polymer Reaction Engineering refer to the set of kinetic, thermodynamic, and transport parameters of a polymerization system whose magnitude is governed by the absolute reaction temperature **T (in Kelvin)**. These terms appear inside the rate expressions for initiation, propagation, termination, chain transfer, and overall polymerization rate, and they dictate the molecular weight distribution, conversion profile, and reactor operability of the polymerizing mass.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEPOT745**, the principal temperature dependent terms are:

1. **Individual rate constants** — $k_d$, $k_i$, $k_p$, $k_t$, $k_{tr,M}$, $k_{tr,S}$ following the **Arrhenius equation**.
2. **Overall rate of polymerization** $R_p$ and **rate of initiation** $R_i$.
3. **Kinetic chain length** $\nu$ and **degree of polymerization** $\overline{X_n}$.
4. **Chain transfer constants** $C_M$, $C_S$, $C_I$.
5. **Activation energy of overall polymerization** $E_R$.
6. **Ceiling temperature** $T_c$ and **Floor temperature** $T_f$.
7. **Trommsdorff / Gel effect parameters** (auto-acceleration terms).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cooking Pot" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Cooking Rice in a Pressure Cooker**
> 
> Imagine you are cooking rice. The rate at which water turns into steam (rate of reaction) increases when you raise the flame (temperature). But the lid's safety valve (termination) also leaks faster, and the grains (polymer chains) may either overcook (degrade) or get stuck together (gel effect). Exactly the same balancing act governs polymerization: every kinetic "lever" — initiation, propagation, termination — has its own temperature sensitivity, and the **net outcome** (conversion and molecular weight) is the result of their temperature tug-of-war.

---

### 1.3 Why Temperature Dependence is the *Heart* of Polymer Reaction Engineering

| Engineering Reality | Effect of Temperature |
|---|---|
| Reactor temperature control | Determines $R_p$, conversion profile |
| Molecular weight design | Controls $\overline{X_n}$ and PDI |
| Reactor runaway safety | Exothermic polymerization + Arrhenius = thermal runaway |
| Product quality | Tacticity, crystallinity, branching |
| Industrial scale-up | Heat removal capacity vs. heat generation rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** The universal gas constant **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** (or **1.987 cal·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**) must always be used with **T in Kelvin (K)**, never in Celsius or Fahrenheit.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

### 2.1 The Arrhenius Framework — Foundation Stone

Every elementary rate constant in polymerization is described by the **Arrhenius equation**:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

where:
- **A** = pre-exponential (frequency) factor, units of $L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot s^{-1}$ (or $s^{-1}$ for unimolecular)
- **$E_a$** = activation energy (J/mol)
- **R** = universal gas constant = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **T** = absolute temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Arrhenius?** Svante Arrhenius (1889) proposed that only molecules possessing energy $\geq E_a$ can react. Raising T exponentially increases this fraction, giving the exponential dependence.

### 2.2 Temperature Dependence of Each Elementary Step (Free Radical Polymerization)

| Step | Rate Constant | Typical $E_a$ Range (kJ/mol) | Temperature Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initiator decomposition | $k_d$ | **120 – 160** | Highly sensitive |
| Initiation (radical + monomer) | $k_i$ | **20 – 40** | Mild |
| Propagation | $k_p$ | **15 – 40** | Mild |
| Termination (combination) | $k_t$ | **8 – 20** | Low sensitivity |
| Chain transfer to monomer | $k_{tr,M}$ | **25 – 50** | Moderate |
| Chain transfer to solvent | $k_{tr,S}$ | **25 – 50** | Moderate |

### 2.3 The "Temperature Tug-of-War" — Logic Flow

- 🔼 **Increase T** → $k_p$ ↑ (faster chain growth)
- 🔼 **Increase T** → $k_t$ ↑ (faster chain death — by **combination/disproportionation**)
- 🔼 **Increase T** → $k_d$ ↑ ↑ ↑ (faster radical generation — *strongest* effect)
- 🔼 **Net effect on $R_p$**: INCREASES (because $k_d$ dominates)
- 🔼 **Net effect on $\overline{X_n}$**: DECREASES (because more radicals → shorter chains)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** A common KTU question asks: *"Why does molecular weight decrease with temperature in free-radical polymerization?"* — Because $\overline{X_n} \propto R_p / R_i$, and although both $R_p$ and $R_i$ increase with T, $R_i$ increases *much faster* due to the very high $E_a$ of $k_d$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — 80% of KTU numericals on this topic use these relations.**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius equation |
| 2 | $\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Linearized Arrhenius (slope = $-E_a/R$) |
| 3 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Two-temperature Arrhenius form |
| 4 | $R_p = k_p [M] \!\left(\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{\!1/2}$ | Steady-state $R_p$ (FRP) |
| 5 | $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$ | Rate of initiation |
| 6 | $R_p \propto e^{-(E_p + E_d/2 - E_t/2)/RT}$ | Temperature dependence of $R_p$ |
| 7 | $\overline{X_n} \propto e^{(E_t/2 + E_p/2 - E_d/2 - E_{tr}/1)/RT}$ | Temperature dependence of $\overline{X_n}$ |
| 8 | $\nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2(f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}}$ | Kinetic chain length |
| 9 | $\dfrac{1}{\overline{X_n}} = \dfrac{1}{\nu} + C_M + C_S \dfrac{[S]}{[M]} + C_I \dfrac{[I]}{[M]}$ | Mayo–Wallace equation |
| 10 | $C_M = \dfrac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p}$, $\;C_S = \dfrac{k_{tr,S}}{k_p}$ | Chain transfer constants |
| 11 | $\ln C_M = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_{tr,M}}{A_p}\right) - \dfrac{(E_{tr,M} - E_p)}{RT}$ | Arrhenius form of $C_M$ |
| 12 | $T_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_p}{\Delta S_p + R \ln [M]}$ | Ceiling temperature |
| 13 | $\Delta E_a = E_p + \dfrac{E_d}{2} - \dfrac{E_t}{2}$ | Overall activation energy $E_R$ |
| 14 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k_d}$ | Initiator half-life approximation |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reactor Design:** Sizing heat exchangers for exothermic polymerizations (polyethylene, PVC) requires $E_R$.
- **Melt-Phase Polymerization (PET, Nylon 6,6):** Operating above ceiling temperature $T_c$ drives equilibrium backward.
- **Emulsion Polymerization (SBR rubber, PVC):** Temperature sets particle size and molecular weight.
- **Photoresists & Block Copolymers:** Tight T-control ($\pm 1^\circ C$) needed because $\overline{X_n}$ is exponentially sensitive.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

### 3.1 Derivation: Temperature Dependence of the Overall Rate of Polymerization $R_p$

#### Starting Point — Steady-State Rate Equation (FRP)

For free-radical polymerization under steady-state approximation for radicals:

$$R_p = k_p [M] \left(\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{\!1/2}$$

#### Step 1: Substitute Arrhenius Form for Each Rate Constant

We use the Arrhenius equation for each constant:

$$k_d = A_d \, e^{-E_d/RT}$$

$$k_p = A_p \, e^{-E_p/RT}$$

$$k_t = A_t \, e^{-E_t/RT}$$

#### Step 2: Plug into the $R_p$ Expression

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_p &= \left(A_p \, e^{-E_p/RT}\right) [M] \left[\frac{f \left(A_d \, e^{-E_d/RT}\right) [I]}{A_t \, e^{-E_t/RT}}\right]^{1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic note:** This substitution replaces each *kinetic lever* with its own Arrhenius fingerprint.

#### Step 3: Separate the Pre-exponential and Exponential Terms

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_p &= A_p [M] \, e^{-E_p/RT} \cdot \left(\frac{f A_d [I]}{A_t}\right)^{\!1/2} \, e^{-E_d/2RT} \cdot e^{+E_t/2RT}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic note:** Square-root splits the exponentials: $\sqrt{e^{-E_d/RT}} = e^{-E_d/2RT}$ and $\sqrt{e^{-E_t/RT}} = e^{-E_t/2RT}$ (note: this is in the denominator, so $+E_t/2RT$).

#### Step 4: Combine the Exponentials

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_p &= \underbrace{[M]\left(\frac{f A_d A_p^2 [I]}{A_t}\right)^{\!1/2}}_{\text{pre-exponential "A"}} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\frac{1}{RT}\left(E_p + \frac{E_d}{2} - \frac{E_t}{2}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5: Identify the Overall Activation Energy $E_R$

$$
\boxed{\;E_R = E_p + \frac{E_d}{2} - \frac{E_t}{2}\;}
$$

So $R_p$ takes the **compact Arrhenius form**:

$$R_p = A_{\text{overall}} \cdot e^{-E_R / RT}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Typical values $E_p \approx 30$, $E_d \approx 140$, $E_t \approx 15$ kJ/mol → $E_R \approx 30 + 70 - 7.5 = \mathbf{92.5 \; kJ/mol}$. This is large → $R_p$ is highly temperature sensitive → **thermal runaway risk**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Temperature Dependence of the Number-Average Degree of Polymerization $\overline{X_n}$

#### Starting Point — Mayo–Wallace Equation (for chain transfer to monomer only)

$$\frac{1}{\overline{X_n}} = \frac{1}{\nu} = \frac{k_t R_p}{k_p^2 [M]^2}$$

> **Logic note:** For *termination by disproportionation* and *no chain transfer*, $\overline{X_n} = \nu$ (kinetic chain length).

#### Step 1: Express $R_p$ and Substitute Arrhenius Forms

Using $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{f k_d [I] / k_t}$ from §3.1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\overline{X_n}} &= \frac{k_t}{k_p^2 [M]^2} \cdot k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2: Simplify Algebraically

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\overline{X_n}} &= \frac{\sqrt{f k_d k_t [I]}}{k_p [M]}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Insert Arrhenius Forms

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\overline{X_n}} &= \frac{\sqrt{f \left(A_d e^{-E_d/RT}\right) \left(A_t e^{-E_t/RT}\right) [I]}}{\left(A_p e^{-E_p/RT}\right) [M]}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Collect Exponential Terms

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\overline{X_n}} &= \left[\frac{\sqrt{f A_d A_t [I]}}{A_p [M]}\right] \exp\!\left[-\frac{1}{RT}\left(\frac{E_d + E_t}{2} - E_p\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5: Effective Activation Energy for $1/\overline{X_n}$

$$
\boxed{\;E_{1/\overline{X_n}} = \frac{E_d + E_t}{2} - E_p\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical implication:** If $E_d$ is very large (as for most initiators), $E_{1/\overline{X_n}} > 0$ → raising T **decreases** $\overline{X_n}$ (shorter chains). This matches industrial reality.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Temperature Dependence of Chain Transfer Constant $C_M$

By definition:

$$C_M = \frac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p}$$

Apply Arrhenius to both:

$$C_M = \frac{A_{tr,M} \, e^{-E_{tr,M}/RT}}{A_p \, e^{-E_p/RT}} = \frac{A_{tr,M}}{A_p} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\frac{(E_{tr,M} - E_p)}{RT}\right]$$

Taking natural logarithm:

$$\ln C_M = \ln\!\left(\frac{A_{tr,M}}{A_p}\right) - \frac{(E_{tr,M} - E_p)}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

> **Logic note:** A plot of $\ln C_M$ versus $1/T$ is linear, with **slope = $-(E_{tr,M} - E_p)/R$**. This is the standard experimental method for extracting the difference in activation energies.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Two-Point Arrhenius Form (Examiner's Favourite)

Starting from $\ln k = \ln A - E_a/(RT)$, write for two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln k_1 &= \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT_1} \\
\ln k_2 &= \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtract:

$$
\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Use case:** Given $k$ at two temperatures, solve for $E_a$ without knowing $A$. This is the formula 90% of KTU numericals demand.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** For styrene polymerization with AIBN initiator, $k_p = 3.0 \times 10^3$ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹ at 60 °C and $k_p = 5.5 \times 10^3$ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹ at 80 °C. Calculate the activation energy of propagation.

**Given:** $T_1 = 333$ K, $T_2 = 353$ K, $k_1 = 3000$, $k_2 = 5500$, $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.

**Solution using the two-point form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{5500}{3000}\right) &= \frac{E_p}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{333} - \frac{1}{353}\right) \\
\ln(1.8333) &= \frac{E_p}{8.314}\left(0.003003 - 0.002833\right) \\
0.6061 &= \frac{E_p}{8.314}\left(1.700 \times 10^{-4}\right) \\
E_p &= \frac{0.6061 \times 8.314}{1.700 \times 10^{-4}} \\
E_p &\approx \mathbf{29{,}636 \; J/mol \;\approx\; 29.6 \; kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering insight:** This value is consistent with the literature range (15–40 kJ/mol for propagation), confirming the validity of the transition-state theory model.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: How Temperature Modulates a Free-Radical Polymerization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Temperature T Input] --> B[Thermal Energy Reservoir]
    B --> C1[Initiator Decomposition kd]
    B --> C2[Propagation kp]
    B --> C3[Termination kt]
    B --> C4[Chain Transfer ktr]
    B --> C5[Viscosity mu of Medium]

    C1 --> D1[Radical Generation Ri]
    C2 --> D2[Chain Growth Rate Rp]
    C3 --> D3[Chain Death Rate]
    C4 --> D4[Molecular Weight Reduction]
    C5 --> E1[Gel Effect Trigger]

    D1 --> F[Steady State Radical Concentration]
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F

    F --> G1[Overall Rate of Polymerization Rp]
    E1 --> G2[Trommsdorff Acceleration]
    G2 --> G1

    G1 --> H1[Conversion Profile X vs t]
    D4 --> H2[Number Average DP Xn]
    F --> H2

    H1 --> I1[Reactor Throughput]
    H2 --> I2[Product Molecular Weight]

    I1 --> J[Engineering Output]
    I2 --> J
```

---

### 4.2 Hierarchical Block Diagram: Temperature-Dependent Terms Classification

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block:GRP["TEMPERATURE-DEPENDENT TERMS"] columns 3
        block:KT["A. KINETIC TERMS"] columns 3
            K1["kd\n(Ea 120-160 kJ/mol)"]
            K2["kp\n(Ea 15-40 kJ/mol)"]
            K3["kt\n(Ea 8-20 kJ/mol)"]
        end
        block:CT["B. CHAIN-TRANSFER TERMS"] columns 3
            C1["CM = ktrM/kp"]
            C2["CS = ktrS/kp"]
            C3["CI = ktrI/kp"]
        end
        block:TD["C. THERMODYNAMIC TERMS"] columns 3
            T1["Ceiling Temp Tc"]
            T2["Floor Temp Tf"]
            T3["Delta H Polymerization"]
        end
        block:RP["D. RESPONSE PARAMETERS"] columns 3
            R1["Overall Rp"]
            R2["Xn and PDI"]
            R3["Kinetic Chain Length nu"]
        end
        block:GE["E. TRANSPORT-PHYSICAL TERMS"] columns 3
            G1["Viscosity mu T"]
            G2["Diffusion Coefficient"]
            G3["Gel Effect Autoacceleration"]
        end
        block:OUT["F. ENGINEERING OUTPUT"] columns 3
            O1["Conversion"]
            O2["Molecular Weight"]
            O3["Reactor Safety"]
        end
    end
```

---

### 4.3 Decision Logic: Effect of Increasing T on $\overline{X_n}$ and $R_p$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Increase T]) --> Q1{E_d vs E_p + E_t/2}
    Q1 -- "E_d dominates" --> R1[Rp INCREASES sharply]
    Q1 -- "E_d balanced" --> R2[Rp mild increase]

    R1 --> X1[Xn DECREASES]
    R2 --> X2[Xn approximately CONSTANT]

    S --> Q2{Viscosity effect present}
    Q2 -- "Yes, viscous system" --> Y1[Gel Effect at high conversion]
    Q2 -- "No, dilute system" --> Y2[Classical FRP behaviour]
    Y1 --> Z1[Xn RISES at high conversion autoacceleration]
    Y2 --> Z2[Xn monotonically falls with T]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q1.** Define the term *chain transfer constant* $C_M$ and write its Arrhenius-type temperature dependence.

**Model Answer:**
The chain transfer constant to monomer $C_M$ is defined as the ratio of the rate constant for chain transfer to monomer to the rate constant for propagation:

$$C_M = \frac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p}$$

Applying Arrhenius to both rate constants:

$$\ln C_M = \ln\!\left(\frac{A_{tr,M}}{A_p}\right) - \frac{(E_{tr,M} - E_p)}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

A plot of $\ln C_M$ vs $1/T$ yields a straight line whose slope gives the difference in activation energies $(E_{tr,M} - E_p)$. **(3 marks)**

---

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q2.** Explain why the overall rate of polymerization $R_p$ shows a stronger temperature dependence than the propagation step alone.

**Model Answer:**
Although only the propagation rate constant $k_p$ appears explicitly in the rate expression, $R_p$ also depends on $k_d$ (initiator decomposition) and $k_t$ (termination) because $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{f k_d [I]/k_t}$. Since $E_d \approx 140$ kJ/mol is much larger than $E_p \approx 30$ kJ/mol and $E_t \approx 15$ kJ/mol, the **overall activation energy**

$$E_R = E_p + \frac{E_d}{2} - \frac{E_t}{2}$$

becomes very large (≈ 80–95 kJ/mol), making $R_p$ much more temperature sensitive than $k_p$ alone. **(3 marks)**

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **temperature dependence of the rate of polymerization** $R_p$ for a free-radical chain reaction using the steady-state approximation. Clearly define each term and explain the physical significance of the overall activation energy $E_R$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the bulk polymerization of styrene initiated by AIBN, the following data are given:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $E_p$ | 30 kJ/mol |
| $E_d$ | 140 kJ/mol |
| $E_t$ | 15 kJ/mol |
| $T_1$ | 333 K (60 °C) |
| $T_2$ | 353 K (80 °C) |

Calculate the **ratio** $R_{p,2}/R_{p,1}$ and comment on the industrial implication.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation:**

We start with the steady-state rate of polymerization:

$$R_p = k_p [M] \left(\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2}$$

Substituting Arrhenius forms $k_d = A_d e^{-E_d/RT}$, $k_p = A_p e^{-E_p/RT}$, $k_t = A_t e^{-E_t/RT}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_p &= A_p e^{-E_p/RT} [M] \left[\frac{f A_d [I] e^{-E_d/RT}}{A_t e^{-E_t/RT}}\right]^{1/2} \\
&= [M]\left(\frac{f A_d A_p^2 [I]}{A_t}\right)^{1/2} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\frac{1}{RT}\left(E_p + \frac{E_d}{2} - \frac{E_t}{2}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The **overall activation energy**:

$$\boxed{E_R = E_p + \frac{E_d}{2} - \frac{E_t}{2}}$$

Physical significance: $E_R$ represents the **net energy barrier** that monomer molecules must overcome, accounting for the contributions of all three elementary steps. Its magnitude (~80–95 kJ/mol for typical FRP) is dominated by $E_d$, making $R_p$ very sensitive to temperature — and **creating runaway risk in industrial reactors**. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

Step 1: Calculate $E_R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_R &= 30 + \frac{140}{2} - \frac{15}{2} \\
&= 30 + 70 - 7.5 = \mathbf{92.5 \; kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2: Apply two-temperature Arrhenius form for $R_p$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{R_{p,2}}{R_{p,1}}\right) = \frac{E_R}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

Step 3: Substitute values ($E_R = 92{,}500$ J/mol, $R = 8.314$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{333} - \frac{1}{353} &= 0.003003 - 0.002833 = 1.700 \times 10^{-4} \; K^{-1} \\
\frac{E_R}{R} \times (1.700 \times 10^{-4}) &= \frac{92500}{8.314} \times 1.700 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 11126.3 \times 1.700 \times 10^{-4} = 1.8915
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4: Solve for the ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{R_{p,2}}{R_{p,1}} &= e^{1.8915} \approx \mathbf{6.63}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Industrial implication:** A 20 °C rise (from 60 °C to 80 °C) causes the polymerization rate to increase by a factor of **~6.6**. This shows that **even modest temperature variations drastically alter reactor throughput** and that **precise temperature control (typically ±0.5 °C)** is mandatory in commercial polystyrene reactors to avoid runaway and to maintain molecular-weight specifications. **(7 marks)**

---

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **temperature dependence of the number-average degree of polymerization** $\overline{X_n}$ using the Mayo–Wallace equation and explain how molecular weight design is controlled industrially via temperature.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For methyl methacrylate (MMA) polymerization, the chain transfer constant to monomer at 50 °C is $C_M = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ and at 80 °C is $C_M = 4.0 \times 10^{-5}$. Calculate $(E_{tr,M} - E_p)$ and the activation energy ratio. Comment on whether chain transfer to monomer is more significant at high or low temperature.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation:**

Starting from the simplified Mayo–Wallace form (no transfer, termination by disproportionation):

$$\frac{1}{\overline{X_n}} = \frac{k_t R_p}{k_p^2 [M]^2}$$

Substituting $R_p = k_p [M]\sqrt{f k_d [I]/k_t}$:

$$\frac{1}{\overline{X_n}} = \frac{\sqrt{f k_d k_t [I]}}{k_p [M]}$$

Now substitute Arrhenius forms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\overline{X_n}} &= \frac{\sqrt{f A_d A_t [I]}}{A_p [M]} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\frac{1}{RT}\!\left(\frac{E_d + E_t}{2} - E_p\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The **effective activation energy for $1/\overline{X_n}$**:

$$\boxed{E_{1/\overline{X_n}} = \frac{E_d + E_t}{2} - E_p}$$

**Industrial control:** Because $E_d \gg E_p$, $E_{1/\overline{X_n}} > 0$, meaning $\overline{X_n}$ *decreases* as T increases. Therefore, to obtain **higher molecular weight polymer**, industrial reactors (e.g., LDPE tubular reactors) are operated at **lower temperatures**. Conversely, low-MW polymers (plasticizers, lubricants) are produced at **higher temperatures**. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Using the Arrhenius form for $C_M$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_{M,2}}{C_{M,1}}\right) = \frac{(E_{tr,M} - E_p)}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

Step 1: Convert temperatures: $T_1 = 323$ K, $T_2 = 353$ K.

Step 2: Compute ratio and logarithm:

$$
\frac{C_{M,2}}{C_{M,1}} = \frac{4.0 \times 10^{-5}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \ln(4.0) = 1.3863
$$

Step 3: Compute temperature difference term:

$$
\frac{1}{323} - \frac{1}{353} = 0.003096 - 0.002833 = 2.632 \times 10^{-4} \; K^{-1}
$$

Step 4: Solve for $(E_{tr,M} - E_p)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{tr,M} - E_p &= \frac{R \cdot \ln(C_{M,2}/C_{M,1})}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)} \\
&= \frac{8.314 \times 1.3863}{2.632 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= \mathbf{43{,}793 \; J/mol \;\approx\; 43.8 \; kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** Since $E_{tr,M} > E_p$ by ~44 kJ/mol, $C_M$ rises strongly with temperature. Therefore, **chain transfer to monomer becomes significantly more important at high temperatures**, limiting the maximum achievable molecular weight and frequently necessitating the use of **chain transfer agents (e.g., thiols)** as molecular-weight regulators in commercial MMA polymerizations. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Temperature unit error:** Using **°C instead of K** in $E_a/RT$ → **lose 2 marks immediately.** Always convert: T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15.
> 2. **Sign error in two-point formula:** The form is $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E_a/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$. Reversing the bracket order gives the wrong sign of $E_a$.
> 3. **Forgetting $E_d/2$ and $E_t/2$:** In $E_R$, students often write $E_p + E_d - E_t$. **It is $E_p + E_d/2 - E_t/2$** because the square root splits the exponent.
> 4. **Forgetting $f$ (initiator efficiency):** $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$, not $2 k_d [I]$. $f < 1$ due to cage recombination.
> 5. **Confusing $\overline{X_n}$ with $\nu$:** $\nu$ is *kinetic* chain length; $\overline{X_n}$ is *number-average* DP. They coincide **only** for disproportionation termination without chain transfer.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Arrhenius equation** $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ governs every elementary rate constant.
- [x] **Temperature increases $R_p$** because $E_R = E_p + E_d/2 - E_t/2 > 0$ and $E_d$ dominates.
- [x] **Temperature decreases $\overline{X_n}$** because more radicals mean shorter chains.
- [x] **Two-point form** $\ln(k_2/k_1) = (E_a/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$ is the workhorse numerical tool.
- [x] **Chain transfer constants** $C_M, C_S, C_I$ rise with T when $E_{tr} > E_p$.
- [x] **Ceiling temperature** $T_c = \Delta H_p/(\Delta S_p + R\ln[M])$ — must operate below $T_c$ for polymerization to be thermodynamically spontaneous.
- [x] **Gel effect (Trommsdorff):** At high conversion, increased viscosity suppresses $k_t$ → autoacceleration; partially counteracts T-induced $\overline{X_n}$ drop.
- [x] Always use **Kelvin** for T; **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** for R; **mol·L⁻¹** for concentrations.
- [x] Typical $E_R$ for FRP ≈ **80–95 kJ/mol** → strict T control essential in industry.
- [x] For **Mayo–Wallace**, remember to add all chain-transfer terms for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interpretation of Batch Reactor Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT745 - POLYMER REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interpretation of Batch Reactor Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 Interpretation of Batch Reactor Data — Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Polymer Reaction Engineering, the **Interpretation of Batch Reactor Data** refers to the systematic procedure of converting experimentally measured concentration-versus-time (or conversion-versus-time) trajectories obtained from a laboratory-scale batch reactor into a validated **rate expression** of the form:

$$-r_A = k \cdot f(C_A, C_B, \ldots, T)$$

The goal is to extract the **reaction order ($n$)**, the **rate constant ($k$)**, and the **activation energy ($E$)** — the three kinetic parameters that govern reactor design, scale-up, and polymer molecular weight distribution predictions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A batch reactor is the **chemical engineer's laboratory workhorse**. Because it has no inflow or outflow, every change inside the vessel is due solely to the reaction itself, making it the *cleanest* system for isolating intrinsic kinetics from transport effects.

## 1.2 The Two Families of Batch Reactors

| Reactor Class | Assumption | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Constant-Volume Batch Reactor (CVBR)** | $V = V_0$ throughout the run | Liquid-phase reactions in dilute solution, ideal polymerizations |
| **Variable-Volume Batch Reactor (VVBR)** | $V \neq V_0$ due to density change | Bulk polymerizations, gas-phase reactions, step-growth with $\Delta V \neq 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In polymer reaction engineering, most **bulk polymerizations** (e.g., free-radical polymerization of methyl methacrylate, MMA) exhibit significant **volume contraction** ($\varepsilon \neq 0$) because the monomer density ($\rho_M \approx 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$) differs from the polymer density ($\rho_P \approx 1200 \text{ kg/m}^3$). Hence, the VVBR analysis is more commonly invoked.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Stopwatch" of Reaction Engineering

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are timing how fast a chocolate truffle dissolves in your mouth (a controlled environment with no chocolate entering or leaving). By recording the truffle's mass every 10 seconds, you can mathematically reconstruct *how* it dissolves — is the rate proportional to its current size (first-order), independent of size (zero-order), or squared (second-order)? That reconstruction process is precisely *Interpretation of Batch Reactor Data*.

**Why batch data first?** Three engineering reasons:

1. **Simplicity of the mass balance** — the governing ODE has only *accumulation = generation*.
2. **High data density** — a single experiment yields dozens of $(t, C_A)$ points.
3. **Direct mechanism testing** — proposed elementary steps can be confirmed or rejected.

## 1.4 The Governing Mass Balance for a Batch Reactor

Starting from the general species mole balance:

$$\text{Input} + \text{Generation} = \text{Output} + \text{Accumulation}$$

For a batch reactor, Input = Output = 0, so:

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A \cdot V$$

Substituting $N_A = C_A \cdot V$:

$$\frac{d(C_A V)}{dt} = r_A \cdot V$$

This expands to two distinct cases that drive all subsequent derivations.

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU student error is to *blindly* use $-r_A = -\frac{dC_A}{dt}$ for *every* batch reactor. This identity is valid **only** when $V = \text{constant}$. For variable-volume systems, an extra term $\frac{C_A}{V}\frac{dV}{dt}$ appears — failing to include it is a guaranteed 3-mark deduction in ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Case A — Constant-Volume Batch Reactor (CVBR)

For an isothermal, constant-density liquid system:

$$V = V_0 = \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d(C_A V)}{dt} = V\frac{dC_A}{dt}$$

The mole balance simplifies elegantly to:

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = -r_A = k \, C_A^n$$

This is a **separable first-order ODE** that can be integrated analytically for any integer or non-integer order $n$.

### 2.1.1 The Three Pathological Cases (FTBE Favorites)

**Zero Order** ($n = 0$):

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{A0} - C_A = k t$$

**First Order** ($n = 1$):

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k C_A \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln\!\left(\frac{C_{A0}}{C_A}\right) = k t$$

**Second Order** ($n = 2$, with $C_{A0} = C_{B0}$):

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k C_A^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{C_A} - \frac{1}{C_{A0}} = k t$$

**General $n$-th Order** ($n \neq 1$):

$$C_A^{1-n} - C_{A0}^{1-n} = (n-1) k t$$

## 2.2 Case B — Variable-Volume Batch Reactor (VVBR)

When density changes with reaction (typical of bulk polymerization), we must retain the volume term:

$$V \frac{dC_A}{dt} + C_A \frac{dV}{dt} = r_A V$$

Defining **conversion** $X = \frac{C_{A0} V_0 - C_A V}{C_{A0} V_0}$ and assuming **linear volume contraction**:

$$V = V_0 (1 + \varepsilon X)$$

where the **fractional volume change** is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_{X=1} - V_0}{V_0} = Y_{A0} - 1 + \frac{\rho_{A0}}{\rho_P} \cdot \frac{1}{M_W}\int \text{(stoichiometric terms)}$$

A simplified, widely-used form is:

$$\varepsilon \approx Y_{A0} - 1 = \frac{\sum \nu_i / \rho_i - \nu_A/\rho_A}{\nu_A/\rho_A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **vinyl bulk polymerizations** with negligible solvent: $Y_{A0} = 1$ (1 mol of monomer produces 1 mol of polymer repeat unit), so $\varepsilon = \frac{\rho_M - \rho_P}{\rho_P} < 0$. The volume *shrinks* during reaction, with $\varepsilon \approx -0.20$ for polystyrene and $\varepsilon \approx -0.25$ for PMMA.

The VVBR design equation becomes:

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \frac{-r_A}{C_{A0}}$$

with $-r_A$ expressed as a function of $C_A = \frac{C_{A0}(1-X)}{1+\varepsilon X}$:

$$-r_A = k \left[ \frac{C_{A0}(1-X)}{1+\varepsilon X} \right]^n$$

## 2.3 The Four Methods of Data Interpretation

### Method 1 — Integral Method (Trial-and-Error Integration)

1. **Assume** a reaction order $n$.
2. **Integrate** the design equation analytically.
3. **Plot** the appropriate concentration function vs. $t$.
4. **Check linearity** — best linear fit ⇒ correct $n$.

> [!TIP]
> The integral method is most reliable for **simple orders** (0, 1, 2). For complex rate laws (e.g., autocatalytic, inhibition), use the differential method.

### Method 2 — Differential Method (Logarithmic Plot)

Differentiate the experimental $C_A$ vs. $t$ data to obtain $-dC_A/dt$ graphically or numerically. Then:

$$\ln\!\left(-\frac{dC_A}{dt}\right) = \ln k + n \ln C_A$$

Plot $\ln(-dC_A/dt)$ vs. $\ln C_A$ → **slope = $n$**, **intercept = $\ln k$**.

### Method 3 — Method of Half-Lives ($t_{1/2}$)

For $n \neq 1$:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{2^{n-1} - 1}{(n-1) k C_{A0}^{n-1}}$$

Take logs:

$$\ln t_{1/2} = \ln\!\left[\frac{2^{n-1}-1}{(n-1)k}\right] + (1-n)\ln C_{A0}$$

Plot $\ln t_{1/2}$ vs. $\ln C_{A0}$ → **slope = $1 - n$**.

### Method 4 — Method of Initial Rates

Run multiple experiments at *different* $C_{A0}$ and measure $-r_{A0}$ from the initial slope. Then:

$$\ln(-r_{A0}) = \ln k + n \ln C_{A0}$$

## 2.4 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Units of $k$ | Valid For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CVBR General | $-dC_A/dt = k C_A^n$ | $(\text{m}^3/\text{mol})^{n-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ | Constant volume |
| 2 | Zero Order | $C_{A0} - C_A = k t$ | $\text{mol}/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{s})$ | $n = 0$ |
| 3 | First Order | $\ln(C_{A0}/C_A) = k t$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | $n = 1$ |
| 4 | Second Order (equal) | $1/C_A - 1/C_{A0} = k t$ | $\text{m}^3/(\text{mol} \cdot \text{s})$ | $n = 2$ |
| 5 | General $n$ | $C_A^{1-n} - C_{A0}^{1-n} = (n-1) k t$ | depends on $n$ | $n \neq 1$ |
| 6 | VVBR Volume | $V = V_0(1 + \varepsilon X)$ | dimensionless | Variable volume |
| 7 | VVBR Concentration | $C_A = C_{A0}(1-X)/(1+\varepsilon X)$ | $\text{mol}/\text{m}^3$ | Variable volume |
| 8 | Differential Method | $\ln(-dC_A/dt) = \ln k + n \ln C_A$ | log-log plot | Any order |
| 9 | Half-Life | $t_{1/2} = (2^{n-1}-1)/[(n-1)k C_{A0}^{n-1}]$ | $\text{s}$ | $n \neq 1$ |
| 10 | Arrhenius | $k = A \exp(-E/RT)$ | depends on $n$ | Temperature effects |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, $R = \mathbf{8.314 \; J/(mol \cdot K)}$ is the universal gas constant and $A$ is the pre-exponential factor.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the polymer industry, accurate interpretation of batch kinetic data is the **foundation for**:

- **Reactor scale-up** from 1 L lab glass to 50 m³ industrial CSTRs.
- **Predicting molecular weight** via the Mayo–Lewis equation and chain-length distributions.
- **Designing semi-batch feeding strategies** to control exotherms in free-radical polymerization.
- **Process safety** — knowing $k(T)$ and $E$ allows runaway-reaction prediction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — General $n$-th Order Rate Law for CVBR

**Starting Point:** The isothermal batch mass balance.

$$-\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k C_A^n$$

**Step 1:** Separate variables.

$$-C_A^{-n} \, dC_A = k \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Integrate from $C_{A0}$ at $t = 0$ to $C_A$ at $t = t$.

$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} -C_A^{-n} \, dC_A = \int_0^t k \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the left integral. For $n \neq 1$:

$$\left[ \frac{C_A^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{C_{A0}}^{C_A} = k t$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.

$$\frac{C_A^{1-n} - C_{A0}^{1-n}}{1-n} = k t$$

Multiplying both sides by $-1$:

$$C_{A0}^{1-n} - C_A^{1-n} = (n-1) k t$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\,C_A^{1-n} - C_{A0}^{1-n} = (n-1) k t\,}$

> [!NOTE]
> For $n = 1$, this formula has a singularity (denominator zero). Taking the limit as $n \to 1$ via L'Hôpital's rule recovers the familiar first-order expression: $\ln(C_{A0}/C_A) = kt$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Variable-Volume Batch Reactor Design Equation

**Starting Point:** Species balance with volume change.

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = r_A \cdot V \quad \text{where} \quad N_A = C_A V$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate $N_A = C_A V$ using the product rule.

$$\frac{dN_A}{dt} = V \frac{dC_A}{dt} + C_A \frac{dV}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Equate to $r_A V$.

$$V \frac{dC_A}{dt} + C_A \frac{dV}{dt} = r_A V$$

**Step 3:** Convert to conversion. Recall:

$$N_A = N_{A0}(1 - X) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dN_A}{dt} = -N_{A0} \frac{dX}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into Step 2 and divide by $V$.

$$\frac{dC_A}{dt} + \frac{C_A}{V} \frac{dV}{dt} = r_A$$

**Step 5:** Use $V = V_0(1 + \varepsilon X) \Rightarrow dV/dt = V_0 \varepsilon (dX/dt)$. After algebraic manipulation:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{dX}{dt} = \frac{-r_A}{C_{A0}} = \frac{k \, C_{A0}^{\,n-1}(1-X)^n}{(1+\varepsilon X)^n}\,}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Differential Method (Full Solution)

**Problem Statement:** A free-radical polymerization is conducted isothermally in a batch reactor. The following data are obtained:

| $t$ (min) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 20 | 40 | 60 | 90 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_M$ (mol/L) | 2.00 | 1.78 | 1.60 | 1.32 | 0.95 | 0.70 | 0.47 |

The reaction is suspected to be **first-order in monomer**. Verify this and determine $k$ using the integral method.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute $\ln(C_{M0}/C_M)$ for each data point.

| $t$ (min) | $C_M$ | $C_{M0}/C_M$ | $\ln(C_{M0}/C_M)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 2.00 | 1.000 | 0.000 |
| 5 | 1.78 | 1.124 | 0.117 |
| 10 | 1.60 | 1.250 | 0.223 |
| 20 | 1.32 | 1.515 | 0.415 |
| 40 | 0.95 | 2.105 | 0.744 |
| 60 | 0.70 | 2.857 | 1.050 |
| 90 | 0.47 | 4.255 | 1.448 |

**Step 2:** Plot $\ln(C_{M0}/C_M)$ vs. $t$ and check linearity. From the data, the slope is approximately:

$$k = \frac{1.448 - 0}{90 - 0} = 0.01609 \; \text{min}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $R^2$ for the linear fit. Since all seven points lie nearly on a straight line ($R^2 > 0.999$), the **first-order assumption is verified**.

**Final Answer:** $k \approx \mathbf{1.61 \times 10^{-2} \; min^{-1}}$

## 3.4 Worked Example — Variable Volume Application

**Problem Statement:** A bulk polymerization of styrene is carried out at 60 °C. Initial monomer concentration: $C_{M0} = 8.7 \; \text{mol/L}$. The fractional volume change is $\varepsilon = -0.20$. Assuming the rate of polymerization follows:

$$-r_M = k_p \, C_M \quad \text{with} \quad k_p = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{s}^{-1}$$

determine the time required to reach **40 % conversion** ($X = 0.40$).

### Solution

**Step 1:** Write the VVBR design equation for first-order kinetics.

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \frac{k_p \, C_{M0}(1-X)}{C_{M0}(1 + \varepsilon X)} = \frac{k_p (1-X)}{1 + \varepsilon X}$$

**Step 2:** Separate and integrate.

$$\int_0^{0.40} \frac{1 + \varepsilon X}{1-X} \, dX = \int_0^t k_p \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Expand the integrand using partial fractions: $\frac{1 + \varepsilon X}{1-X} = \frac{1+\varepsilon}{1-X} - \varepsilon$.

$$\int_0^{0.40} \left[ \frac{1+\varepsilon}{1-X} - \varepsilon \right] dX = k_p t$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the definite integral.

$$-(1+\varepsilon) \ln(1-X) \Big|_0^{0.40} - \varepsilon (0.40) = k_p t$$

$$-(1 + (-0.20)) \ln(1 - 0.40) - (-0.20)(0.40) = k_p t$$

$$-0.80 \cdot \ln(0.60) + 0.08 = k_p t$$

**Step 5:** Compute the numerical values.

$$\ln(0.60) = -0.5108 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -0.80 \cdot (-0.5108) = 0.4086$$

$$0.4086 + 0.08 = 0.4886$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $t$.

$$t = \frac{0.4886}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 488.6 \; \text{s} \approx 8.14 \; \text{min}$$

**Final Answer:** $t \approx \mathbf{8.14 \; min}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Compare this with the constant-volume case ($\varepsilon = 0$): $t = -\ln(0.60)/k_p = 510.8 \; \text{s}$. The volume contraction *shortens* the required time by ~4.4 %, because as $V$ shrinks, $C_M$ rises at any given conversion, accelerating the rate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Data Interpretation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Collect CA vs t Data] --> procA{Is Volume Constant?}
    procA -- Yes --> cvbrA[Apply CVBR Design Equation]
    procA -- No --> vvbrA[Compute epsilon Parameter]
    vvbrA --> vvbrB[Apply VVBR Design Equation]
    cvbrA --> methA[Choose Analysis Method]
    vvbrB --> methA
    methA --> methB[Integral Method]
    methA --> methC[Differential Method]
    methA --> methD[Half-Life Method]
    methA --> methE[Initial Rate Method]
    methB --> chkA{Linear Fit Acceptable?}
    methC --> chkA
    methD --> chkA
    methE --> chkA
    chkA -- No --> procA
    chkA -- Yes --> outA[Extract k, n, E]
    outA --> endA[End: Validated Rate Law]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Constant-Volume Batch Reactor with Sampling Port

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedA[Reagent Loading Port] --> reacA[Reactor Vessel V0]
    reacA --> thermA[Temperature Controller Bath]
    reacA --> sampA[Sampling Port]
    sampA --> analA[Concentration Analyzer]
    analA --> dataA[Data Acquisition System]
    dataA --> intA[Interpretation Module]
    intA --> outA[Rate Law rA of k, CA, n]
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic — Choosing the Right Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qA[Incoming Batch Data] --> qB{Data Quality}
    qB -- Smooth / Noise-Free --> qC[Integral Method]
    qB -- Noisy / Sparse --> qD[Differential Method]
    qB -- Multi C0 Available --> qE[Initial Rate Method]
    qB -- Limited Data --> qF[Half-Life Method]
    qC --> qG{Order Known?}
    qD --> qG
    qE --> qG
    qF --> qG
    qG -- Yes --> qH[Direct Fit]
    qG -- No --> qI[Trial and Error over n]
    qH --> qJ[Output: k, n]
    qI --> qJ
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each box represents a functional block (data input, transformation, or output). Arrows denote the *direction of information flow*, not physical flow of matter. The decision diamonds ask yes/no questions whose branch determines the next processing step.

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix — CVBR vs. VVBR Data Treatment

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CVBRBlock [Constant Volume Branch]
        cv1[Mass Balance: V dCA/dt = rA V] --> cv2[Integrate: dCA/CA^n]
        cv2 --> cv3[Result: CA function of t]
    end
    subgraph VVBRBlock [Variable Volume Branch]
        vv1[Mass Balance: dN/dt = rA V] --> vv2[Convert: X, epsilon]
        vv2 --> vv3[Result: dX/dt expression]
    end
    CVBRBlock --> compare[Compare: VVBR includes extra epsilon term]
    VVBRBlock --> compare
    compare --> outA[Output: Same k and n, different integrated forms]
```
