The entire MWD can be compactly represented by its **statistical moments** $M_k$:

$$M_k = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} N_x \cdot M_x^k$$

where $N_x$ is the number of molecules of degree of polymerisation $x$ and $M_x = x \cdot M_0$ (with $M_0$ = molar mass of the repeat unit).

- **Zeroth moment ($k=0$):** $M_0 = \sum N_x$ → total number of molecules
- **First moment ($k=1$):** $M_1 = \sum N_x M_x$ → total mass of polymer
- **Second moment ($k=2$):** $M_2 = \sum N_x M_x^2$ → controls weight-average

> [!NOTE]
> **Why moments matter:** The moments form a **recursive differential equation set** (often called the *Smith-Ewart* or *generalised moment equations*) which can be integrated analytically for simple kinetic schemes, giving direct access to $M_n$, $M_w$ without solving for the full MWD.

### 2.2 MWD in Linear Step-Growth (Condensation) Polymerisation

In step-growth, any two reactive chain-ends can combine. The probability that a given functional group A has reacted is the **conversion** $p$ (also denoted $\alpha$ in some texts, but KTU uses $p$).

**Flory's Most Probable Distribution (1953):**

$$N_x = N_0 \cdot (1-p) \cdot p^{x-1}$$

$$W_x = x \cdot (1-p)^2 \cdot p^{x-1}$$

where $N_0$ is the initial number of monomer units.

**Key results:**

$$\boxed{X_n = \overline{DP}_n = \frac{1}{1-p}} \quad \text{(Carothers equation)}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{M_w}{M_n} = 1 + p}$$

At complete conversion ($p \to 1$): $X_n \to \infty$ and PDI $\to 2$ → **broader distribution**.

### 2.3 MWD in Linear Chain-Growth (Free Radical) Polymerisation

In free-radical polymerisation, chain growth is initiated by radicals, propagates rapidly, and terminates by either **combination** or **disproportionation**.

**Schulz-Flory (Geometric / Most-Probable) Distribution — Disproportionation/Chain Transfer termination:**

$$N_x = (1-p) \cdot p^{x-1}$$

$$W_x = x \cdot (1-p)^2 \cdot p^{x-1}$$

$$\frac{M_w}{M_n} = 2$$

**For Combination Termination (two radicals couple):**

$$N_x = (1-p)^2 \cdot (x-1) \cdot p^{x-2}$$

$$\frac{M_w}{M_n} = 1.5$$

**For living / anionic polymerisation (no termination):**

$$W_x = \frac{x \cdot e^{-\nu}}{\nu^{x-1} \cdot (x-1)!} \quad \text{(Poisson Distribution)}$$

where $\nu$ = number of monomer units added per active chain. This gives **PDI ≈ 1.0–1.1** (nearly monodisperse).

### 2.4 MWD in Non-Linear (Branching & Crosslinking) Polymerisation

Here Flory's statistical theory must be extended using the **branching coefficient** $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = p \cdot \rho$$

where $p$ = fraction of functional groups reacted, and $\rho$ = fraction of all functional groups belonging to **polyfunctional** monomers (i.e., monomers that can branch).

For an $f$-functional branch unit:

$$\alpha = p^2 \cdot \frac{(f-1) \cdot c}{c_0}$$

**Gel Point Criterion (Flory-Stockmayer):**

$$\boxed{p_c = \frac{1}{(f-1) \cdot \rho}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \boxed{\alpha_c = \frac{1}{f-1}}$$

- For $f=3$ (trifunctional, e.g., glycerol in alkyd resin): $p_c = 0.5$
- For $f=4$ (tetrafunctional, e.g., divinyl benzene): $p_c = 0.33$

Below $p_c$: only finite (sol) molecules; above $p_c$: an **infinite network (gel)** coexists with finite sol.

**Stockmayer's Non-Linear MWD (for trifunctional branching):**

$$W_x = \frac{(1-p)^2}{1+\alpha} \cdot \binom{x}{1+\text{branches}} \cdot \alpha^{(\text{branches})} \cdot p^{x-1-\text{branches}}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Situation | $M_n$ | $M_w/M_n$ (PDI) | Distribution Shape |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Step-growth (linear) | $\frac{M_0}{1-p}$ | $1+p$ | Most probable (geometric) |
| 2 | Free-radical, disproportionation | $\frac{M_0}{1-p}$ | $2$ | Schulz-Flory |
| 3 | Free-radical, combination | $\frac{M_0}{1-p}$ | $1.5$ | Skewed geometric |
| 4 | Living / anionic | $M_0 \cdot \nu$ | $1 + \frac{1}{\nu}$ | Poisson (narrow) |
| 5 | Ziegler-Natta (heterogeneous) | varies | $2$–$20$ | Broad, multi-site |
| 6 | Branched (pre-gel, $f=3$) | $\frac{M_0 (1+\alpha)}{1-p}$ | $1 + \frac{1}{1+\alpha}\left[1 + \alpha \cdot \frac{1+p}{1-p}\right]$ | Broadens as $p \to p_c$ |
| 7 | Branched (post-gel) | Sol fraction via Stockmayer | → $\infty$ | Bimodal (sol + gel) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** The PDI value directly dictates whether a polymer is suitable for a given application:
> - **Injection moulding:** Needs low PDI (≈ 2) for uniform flow.
> - **Blow moulding / film:** Needs high PDI (≈ 4–10) for melt strength.
> - **Engineering plastics:** Tight PDI for predictable mechanical performance.
> - **Tire rubber (vulcanised):** Crosslinked network — gel fraction determines elasticity.

### 2.6 The Three Reaction Classes and Their MWD Fingerprints

| Feature | Linear Step-Growth | Linear Chain-Growth | Non-Linear (Branching) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Monomer functionality | Bifunctional | Bifunctional | At least one polyfunctional |
| Chain architecture | Strictly linear | Strictly linear | Branched → crosslinked |
| Termination | No active centre | Radical combination/disprop. | Network forms |
| PDI at high conversion | → 2 | 1.5 or 2 | Can diverge to ∞ at gel point |
| Sol/Gel | No gel | No gel | **Yes**, post-gel |
| MWD analytical form | Geometric | Geometric / skewed | Stockmayer (complex) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Number-Average DP for Step-Growth (Carothers Equation)

**Starting point:** Let $N_0$ = initial number of monomer molecules (functionality = 2). Each reaction event removes **one** monomer unit (two chain-ends combine into one bond, and the number of molecules drops by 1).

**Step 1 — Count reacted functional groups:**
Total initial functional groups = $2N_0$ (bifunctional). Number of reacted groups = $2N_0 p$.

**Step 2 — Count molecules remaining:**
Each bond formed consumes 2 functional groups but creates 1 new molecule-pair connection. Number of bonds formed = $N_0 p$.

$$N = N_0 - N_0 p = N_0(1-p)$$

**Step 3 — Apply definition of number-average degree of polymerisation:**

$$X_n = \frac{\text{initial monomer units}}{\text{remaining molecules}} = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)}$$

$$\boxed{X_n = \frac{1}{1-p}}$$

> This is the **Carothers equation** (1932). At $p=0.99$, $X_n = 100$. At $p=0.999$, $X_n = 1000$.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Polydispersity Index for Step-Growth

**Step 1 — Probability that a randomly chosen chain has exactly $x$ units:**
A chain of $x$ units has $x-1$ reacted internal bonds and 1 unreacted end. Probability = $(1-p) \cdot p^{x-1}$ (Flory).

$$N_x = N_0 (1-p)^2 \cdot p^{x-1} \quad \text{(number of x-mers)}$$

(Note: $N_0(1-p)^2$ normalises using the two chain-ends.)

**Step 2 — Compute the zeroth moment (total molecules):**

$$M_0 = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} N_x = N_0(1-p)^2 \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} p^{x-1} = N_0(1-p)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{1-p} = N_0(1-p)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the first moment (total mass, in monomer units):**

$$M_1 = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x N_x = N_0(1-p)^2 \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x \, p^{x-1} = N_0(1-p)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{(1-p)^2} = N_0$$

(using $\sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x p^{x-1} = 1/(1-p)^2$).

**Step 4 — Compute the second moment:**

$$M_2 = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x^2 N_x = N_0(1-p)^2 \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x^2 \, p^{x-1}$$

Using the identity $\sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x^2 p^{x-1} = (1+p)/(1-p)^3$:

$$M_2 = N_0(1-p)^2 \cdot \frac{1+p}{(1-p)^3} = \frac{N_0(1+p)}{(1-p)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $X_n$ and $X_w$:**

$$X_n = \frac{M_1}{M_0} = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

$$X_w = \frac{M_2}{M_1} = \frac{N_0(1+p)/(1-p)}{N_0} = \frac{1+p}{1-p}$$

**Step 6 — Form the polydispersity index:**

$$\boxed{\frac{M_w}{M_n} = \frac{X_w}{X_n} = \frac{(1+p)/(1-p)}{1/(1-p)} = 1 + p}$$

At $p \to 1$ (full conversion): PDI $\to 2$. ✓ **Confirmed.**

### 3.3 Derivation C — PDI for Free-Radical with Disproportionation Termination

For a steady-state free-radical polymerisation, the probability of propagation to a chain of length $x$ follows the same geometric law. The number of "dead" chains of length $x$:

$$N_x = (1-p) p^{x-1}$$

**Step 1 — Compute moments (same procedure as Derivation B):**

$$M_0 = \sum N_x = \frac{1}{1-p}, \quad M_1 = \frac{1}{(1-p)^2}, \quad M_2 = \frac{1+p}{(1-p)^3}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $X_w/X_n$:**

$$X_n = \frac{M_1}{M_0} = \frac{1}{1-p}, \quad X_w = \frac{M_2}{M_1} = \frac{1+p}{1-p}$$

$$\frac{X_w}{X_n} = 1+p$$

**But at high conversion for chain-growth**, $p \to 1$ *asymptotically with monomer depletion*. In the limit:

$$\boxed{\lim_{p \to 1} \frac{M_w}{M_n} = 2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor 2 is the **characteristic fingerprint** of chain-transfer-dominated or disproportionation-terminated free-radical polymerisation. Polystyrene made by free-radical process typically shows PDI ≈ 1.5–2.

### 3.4 Derivation D — PDI for Combination Termination

When two growing radicals couple, the resulting dead chain is the sum of two sub-chains. The resulting chain-length distribution is a **convolution** of the geometric distribution with itself.

**Step 1 — Probability that a chain of length $x$ is formed by combination:**
Sum of two random sub-chains of lengths $i$ and $(x-i)$:

$$N_x = \sum_{i=1}^{x-1} N_i \cdot N_{x-i} \quad \text{(discrete convolution)}$$

**Step 2 — Closed-form result (Schulz, 1939):**

$$N_x = (1-p)^2 (x-1) p^{x-2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute moments:**

$$M_0 = \sum_{x=2}^{\infty} N_x = (1-p)^2 \sum_{x=2}^{\infty} (x-1) p^{x-2} = (1-p)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{(1-p)^2} = 1$$

(Recall: for combination, every two active chains become one dead chain, so $M_0 \to 1$ in the limit, but a more careful kinetic derivation retains $(1-p)$ factors.)

Using the rigorous expression $N_x = (1-p)(x-1)p^{x-2}$ for $x \geq 2$:

$$X_n = \frac{2}{1-p}, \quad X_w = \frac{2(2+p)}{(1-p)(1+p)}$$

$$\boxed{\lim_{p \to 1} \frac{M_w}{M_n} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5}$$

### 3.5 Derivation E — Gel Point Condition for Trifunctional Branching (Stockmayer)

**Setup:** We have a mixture of bifunctional A-A monomer (concentration $c_0$) and trifunctional A-B monomer where B can branch (concentration $c$).

**Step 1 — Define branching coefficient $\alpha$:**
The probability that a given A-functional group on a branch unit has reacted and is connected *via* a B-branch to *another* branch unit:

$$\alpha = p_A \cdot p_B \cdot (f-1) = p^2 \cdot (f-1) \cdot \frac{c}{c_0}$$

(For stoichiometric balance, $p_A = p_B = p$.)

**Step 2 — Apply Flory's tree-theorem:**
The number of distinct paths leading to an infinite network is non-zero only when the expected number of new branches generated by an existing branch exceeds 1.

**Mathematical criterion:** $\alpha \cdot (f-1) = 1$, but for $f$-functional unit, branching index of the unit = $(f-1)$, so:

$$\alpha_c = \frac{1}{f-1}$$

For $f=3$: $\alpha_c = 1/2 \Rightarrow p_c = 0.5$. ✓

**Step 3 — Sol fraction $W_s$ above gel point (Stockmayer):**

$$W_s = (1-p)^2 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \binom{f-1}{n} \alpha^n (1-\alpha)^{f-1-n} \cdot \left[\text{finite tree contribution}\right]$$

The closed form for $f=3$:

$$W_s = \left(\frac{1-\alpha}{\alpha}\right) \cdot \frac{p(1-p)^2}{p^2 - p + 1} \quad \text{when} \quad p > p_c$$

**Step 4 — Weight fraction of gel:**

$$\boxed{W_g = 1 - W_s}$$

> The gel fraction rises smoothly from 0 at $p_c$ to 1 as $p \to 1$.

### 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | Linear Step-Growth | Linear Chain-Growth (FRP) | Non-Linear (Branched) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $X_n$ formula | $1/(1-p)$ | $1/(1-p)$ (disprop.) or $2/(1-p)$ (comb.) | $1/(1-p\alpha)$ |
| PDI formula | $1+p$ | $2$ (disprop.) or $1.5$ (comb.) | $1 + \frac{1}{1+\alpha}\left[1 + \frac{\alpha(1+p)}{1-p}\right]$ |
| Gel point? | No | No | Yes, at $\alpha_c = 1/(f-1)$ |
| Sol/gel split | N/A | N/A | Yes, post-gel |
| Distribution type | Geometric (Schulz-Flory) | Geometric / Skewed-geometric | Stockmayer (generalised) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of MWD in Different Polymerisation Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef linearStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nonlinearStyle fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef inputStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    inputM[("Monomer Feed\nBifunctional / Polyfunctional")]:::inputStyle
    pDep["Reaction Pathway Selector"]:::inputStyle

    linearPath["LINEAR POLYMERISATION\n(Single chain, no branches)"]:::linearStyle
    nonlinearPath["NON-LINEAR POLYMERISATION\n(Branches, crosslinks)"]:::nonlinearStyle

    stepG["Step-Growth\nCondensation\nA-B / A-A + B-B"]:::linearStyle
    chainG["Chain-Growth\nFree Radical\nI --> R* --> Px"]:::linearStyle

    branchF["Branching\nf > 2 monomer\npresent"]:::nonlinearStyle
    crosslinkF["Crosslinking\n3-D network\nformation"]:::nonlinearStyle

    distLin["Distribution Engine\nGeometric / Poisson"]:::linearStyle
    distNL["Distribution Engine\nStockmayer\nSol-Gel Split"]:::nonlinearStyle

    pdiCalc["PDI Calculator\nMw / Mn"]:::outputStyle
    qualOut["Quality Output\nMw, Mn, PDI\nMelt Index"]:::outputStyle

    inputM --> pDep
    pDep -->|"Bifunctional only"| linearPath
    pDep -->|"Polyfunctional present"| nonlinearPath

    linearPath --> stepG
    linearPath --> chainG
    nonlinearPath --> branchF
    nonlinearPath --> crosslinkF

    stepG --> distLin
    chainG --> distLin
    branchF --> distNL
    crosslinkF --> distNL

    distLin --> pdiCalc
    distNL --> pdiCalc
    pdiCalc --> qualOut
```

### 4.2 MWD Curve Shapes — Comparative Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef boxStyle fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#1a4d80,stroke-width:2px
    classDef redStyle fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef grnStyle fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px

    A["STEP-GROWTH\nPDI -> 2\nBroad Geometric"]:::boxStyle
    B["FREE RADICAL\nCombination PDI = 1.5\nDisproportionation PDI = 2"]:::boxStyle
    C["LIVING / ANIONIC\nPDI approx 1.0\nPoisson Narrow"]:::grnStyle

    E["ZIEGLER-NATTA\nPDI 2 to 20\nMulti-site Broad"]:::redStyle

    A -->|"Monomer\nFunctionality f=2"| A1["Distribution:\nNx = N0(1-p) p^(x-1)"]:::boxStyle
    B -->|"Termination\nMechanism"| B1["Combination:\nNx = (1-p)² (x-1) p^(x-2)"]:::boxStyle
    C -->|"No Termination\nNo Transfer"| C1["Distribution:\nPoisson around nu"]:::boxStyle
    D -->|"f >= 3\np > pc"| D1["Stockmayer Distribution\n+ Sol-Gel Split"]:::redStyle
    E -->|"Heterogeneous\nSites"| E1["Superposition of\nMultiple Schulz-Flory"]:::redStyle
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
Define the following with one example each:
(a) Number-average molecular weight $M_n$
(b) Weight-average molecular weight $M_w$
(c) Polydispersity Index (PDI)

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Number-average molecular weight ($M_n$):** It is the total mass of all polymer molecules divided by the total number of molecules in the sample.

$$M_n = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i M_i}{\sum_{i} N_i}$$

Example: For a polymer sample with 5 chains of $M = 10{,}000$ g/mol and 5 chains of $M = 30{,}000$ g/mol, $M_n = (5 \times 10{,}000 + 5 \times 30{,}000)/10 = 20{,}000$ g/mol.

(b) **Weight-average molecular weight ($M_w$):** It is the average molecular weight weighted by the mass of each chain, giving greater importance to heavier chains.

$$M_w = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i M_i^2}{\sum_{i} N_i M_i}$$

For the same example: $M_w = (5 \times 10^8 + 5 \times 9 \times 10^8)/(5 \times 10{,}000 + 5 \times 30{,}000) = 2.5 \times 10^4$ g/mol.

(c) **Polydispersity Index (PDI):** The ratio $M_w/M_n$, indicating the breadth of the molecular weight distribution. PDI = 1 indicates monodisperse (theoretical), PDI > 1 indicates polydisperse (real). For the example: PDI = 1.25.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
Differentiate between linear and non-linear polymerisation with respect to molecular weight distribution. Mention the role of branching coefficient $\alpha$.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Linear Polymerisation | Non-Linear Polymerisation |
|---|---|---|
| Monomer type | Bifunctional only | At least one polyfunctional ($f \geq 3$) |
| Architecture | Straight chains | Branches → crosslinks → 3-D network |
| MWD | Schulz-Flory (geometric); PDI → 2 max | Stockmayer distribution; PDI can diverge |
| Gel point | Does not exist | Exists at $p_c = 1/[(f-1)\rho]$ |
| Examples | HDPE, PS, PVC, PMMA | LDPE, vulcanised rubber, epoxy |

**Role of branching coefficient $\alpha$:** It is defined as $\alpha = p^2 (f-1)(c/c_0)$, the probability that a randomly chosen branch point leads via a sequence of bonds to *another* branch point. It controls:
- the breadth of the MWD (PDI rises sharply as $\alpha \to 1$),
- the gel point (gel forms when $\alpha = 1/(f-1)$),
- the sol/gel split (post-gel).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the Carothers equation for number-average degree of polymerisation in linear step-growth polymerisation. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Starting from Flory's most-probable distribution, derive the expression for polydispersity index ($M_w/M_n$) in step-growth polymerisation. (7 Marks)

##### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Carothers Equation Derivation:**

*Step 1:* Let $N_0$ = total number of monomer molecules initially, each bifunctional (2 reactive groups per molecule).

*Step 2:* The total number of functional groups initially is $2N_0$. After conversion $p$, the number of *reacted* functional groups = $2N_0 p$. Since each condensation reaction consumes 2 functional groups and forms 1 bond, the number of bonds formed = $N_0 p$.

*Step 3:* Each bond formation event reduces the total number of molecules by 1. So, the number of molecules remaining in the system:

$$N = N_0 - (\text{bonds formed}) = N_0 - N_0 p = N_0(1-p)$$

*Step 4:* The number-average degree of polymerisation $X_n$ is the average number of monomer units per chain:

$$X_n = \frac{\text{initial monomer units}}{\text{remaining molecules}} = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)}$$

$$\boxed{X_n = \frac{1}{1-p}}$$

This is the **Carothers equation** (1932). When $p = 0.99$, $X_n = 100$. When $p = 0.999$, $X_n = 1000$.

**Part (b) — PDI Derivation:**

*Step 1:* Flory's most-probable distribution gives the number fraction of chains having exactly $x$ monomer units:

$$P(x) = (1-p) p^{x-1}$$

*Step 2:* Number of such chains $N_x$ (with the total number of molecules = $N_0(1-p)$):

$$N_x = N_0(1-p)^2 p^{x-1}$$

*Step 3:* Compute the three moments:

$$M_0 = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} N_x = N_0(1-p)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{1-p} = N_0(1-p)$$

$$M_1 = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x \cdot N_x = N_0(1-p)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{(1-p)^2} = N_0$$

$$M_2 = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x^2 \cdot N_x = N_0(1-p)^2 \cdot \frac{(1+p)}{(1-p)^3} = \frac{N_0(1+p)}{(1-p)}$$

*Step 4:* Calculate averages:

$$X_n = \frac{M_1}{M_0} = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

$$X_w = \frac{M_2}{M_1} = \frac{N_0(1+p)/(1-p)}{N_0} = \frac{1+p}{1-p}$$

*Step 5:* Polydispersity index:

$$\boxed{\frac{M_w}{M_n} = \frac{X_w}{X_n} = \frac{(1+p)/(1-p)}{1/(1-p)} = 1 + p}$$

**Conclusion:** At $p \to 1$, PDI $\to 2$. For step-growth polymers at high conversion, the most probable (broad) distribution is the limiting case.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the Schulz-Flory distribution for free-radical polymerisation. Derive the polydispersity index for the case of termination by disproportionation. (7 Marks)

**(b)** What is the gel point? Derive the Stockmayer criterion for the gel point in a polymerisation involving trifunctional monomers. Discuss the post-gel sol-gel distribution. (7 Marks)

##### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Schulz-Flory Distribution and PDI (Disproportionation):**

*Step 1:* In free-radical polymerisation, the probability of chain propagation to a chain of length $x$ follows a geometric series. The probability that a chain has grown to exactly $x$ units is:

$$P(x) = (1-p) p^{x-1}$$

where $p$ is the probability of propagation (versus termination/transfer).

*Step 2:* For termination by disproportionation, the dead chain has the same length as the growing chain at the moment of termination. The number of $x$-mers is:

$$N_x = (1-p) p^{x-1}$$

*Step 3:* Compute moments:

$$M_0 = \frac{1}{1-p}, \quad M_1 = \frac{1}{(1-p)^2}, \quad M_2 = \frac{1+p}{(1-p)^3}$$

*Step 4:* Calculate $X_n$ and $X_w$:

$$X_n = \frac{M_1}{M_0} = \frac{1}{1-p}, \quad X_w = \frac{M_2}{M_1} = \frac{1+p}{1-p}$$

*Step 5:* PDI:

$$\frac{M_w}{M_n} = 1 + p$$

As $p \to 1$ (long chains, kinetic chain length large):

$$\boxed{\lim_{p \to 1} \frac{M_w}{M_n} = 2}$$

**Significance:** PDI = 2 is the characteristic fingerprint of free-radical polymerisation with chain-transfer or disproportionation termination. This is why commodity FRP polymers (PS, PVC, PMMA) have $M_w/M_n \approx 1.5$–$2$.

**Part (b) — Gel Point and Stockmayer Criterion:**

*Step 1:* A **gel point** is the critical conversion at which an *infinite* polymer network (gel) first appears, coexisting with finite molecules (sol). Below $p_c$, only finite chains exist.

*Step 2:* For a polymerisation with trifunctional ($f=3$) monomers mixed with bifunctional monomers, define the **branching coefficient** $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = p^2 (f-1) \frac{c}{c_0}$$

where $p$ = conversion, $f$ = functionality of branch unit, $c$ = concentration of branch units, $c_0$ = total concentration of A-functional groups.

*Step 3:* Apply Flory's tree-theorem. A branch point is "successful" if it leads (through chain ends) to *at least one more* branch point. The expected number of new branches per existing branch is $\alpha \cdot (f-1)$. The gel forms when this expectation equals 1:

$$\alpha_c (f-1) = 1 \Rightarrow \alpha_c = \frac{1}{f-1}$$

For $f=3$ (trifunctional): $\alpha_c = 1/2$, giving $p_c = 0.5$ (for stoichiometric case $c/c_0 = 1$).

*Step 4:* **Post-gel sol fraction (Stockmayer, 1943):** For $f=3$, the sol fraction (weight of finite molecules) is:

$$W_s = \left(\frac{1-\alpha}{\alpha}\right) \cdot \frac{p(1-p)^2}{p^2 - p + 1}, \quad p > p_c$$

The gel fraction is $W_g = 1 - W_s$, which grows from 0 at $p = p_c$ to 1 as $p \to 1$.

*Step 5:* **MWD in post-gel regime:** The system is *bimodal* — the MWD is a sum of the sol distribution and the gel (effectively infinite-mass peak). The PDI of the total system formally diverges to infinity at the gel point, reflecting the appearance of the infinite-mass component.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $p$ with $\alpha$:** Students often interchange the *conversion* $p$ and the *branching coefficient* $\alpha$. Remember: $p$ = fraction of functional groups reacted; $\alpha$ = probability of *branch-to-branch* connection (depends on $p$, $f$, and concentration of polyfunctional monomer).
> 2. **Forgetting stoichiometry:** The gel point formula $p_c = 1/[(f-1)\rho]$ uses $\rho$ = fraction of functional groups on polyfunctional monomers. If stoichiometry is non-stoichiometric, use the more general Flory formula.
> 3. **Mixing $X_n$ and $M_n$:** $X_n$ is the degree of polymerisation (number of repeat units); $M_n = X_n \cdot M_0$ where $M_0$ is the molar mass of the repeat unit. Don't substitute $M_0 = 1$ unless the question explicitly says so.
> 4. **Forgetting the convolution for combination:** In combination termination, students often incorrectly use the disproportionation formula. Recall: combination gives PDI = 1.5, not 2.
> 5. **Gel point is NOT a phase change:** It is a percolation-type mathematical transition. The gel appears sharply but is *infinitely* dilute at $p_c$; its mass grows continuously above $p_c$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Board-Exam Ready):**

- **MWD Definition:** A statistical description of polymer chain-length population; characterised by $M_n$, $M_w$, $M_z$, and PDI = $M_w/M_n$.
- **$M_n$ uses count statistics, $M_w$ uses mass statistics; always $M_z \geq M_w \geq M_n$.**
- **Step-growth (linear) PDI = $1+p$; as $p \to 1$, PDI $\to 2$ (Schulz-Flory / most-probable distribution).**
- **Carothers equation: $X_n = 1/(1-p)$ — fundamental for condensation polymerisation.**
- **Free-radical PDI:** Disproportionation → 2; Combination → 1.5; Living/anionic → ~1 (Poisson).
- **Branching coefficient $\alpha$:** Probability that a branch point leads to another branch; gel forms at $\alpha_c = 1/(f-1)$.
- **Trifunctional gel point:** $p_c = 0.5$ (stoichiometric); Tetrafunctional: $p_c = 0.33$.
- **Post-gel:** Bimodal MWD; sol fraction $W_s$ calculated via Stockmayer; gel fraction $W_g = 1 - W_s$.
- **Moment method:** $M_k = \sum N_x M_x^k$ gives $M_n = M_1/M_0$, $M_w = M_2/M_1$, $M_z = M_3/M_2$.
- **Key distributions:**
  - Geometric (Schulz-Flory): $N_x = (1-p)p^{x-1}$
  - Combination: $N_x = (1-p)^2(x-1)p^{x-2}$
  - Poisson: $W_x = x e^{-\nu} / [\nu^{x-1}(x-1)!]$
  - Stockmayer (branched): generalisation involving $\alpha$ and branch multiplicity.
- **Real-world PDI targets:** Injection moulding ≈ 2; Film/blow moulding ≈ 4–10; Engineering plastics ≈ 1.5–2; Thermosets (crosslinked) → gel network.
- **Why MWD matters in industry:** Determines rheology, mechanical strength, optical clarity, processing window, and ultimate product performance. Reactor type (CSTR vs PFR), temperature, residence time, and catalyst type are *all* tuned to achieve a target MWD.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modelling of Bulk and Solution Polymerisation Reactors and Precipitation Polymerization: Reactor Modelling to Interpret MWD of Industrial Polymers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT745 - POLYMER REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modelling of Bulk and Solution Polymerisation Reactors and Precipitation Polymerization: Reactor Modelling to Interpret MWD of Industrial Polymers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bulk Polymerization Reactor Modelling

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Bulk (mass) polymerization is a heterogeneous free-radical polymerization process in which the reaction proceeds in the pure monomer phase without any added solvent or diluent. Reactor modelling of bulk polymerization involves writing coupled **mass, energy, and species balance equations** over the reacting mixture, accounting for the continuously increasing viscosity, gel effect (Trommsdorff–Norrish effect), and the formation of a polymer-rich phase that influences the molecular weight distribution (MWD).

> [!NOTE]
> Bulk polymerization is the **industrially preferred route** for PMMA, PS, LDPE, and PVC because it yields the purest polymer (no solvent contamination) and the highest space-time yield.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of bulk polymerization as *baking a cake in its own juice* — there is no added water (solvent), only the raw ingredients (monomer) reacting with a leavening agent (initiator). As the reaction proceeds, the batter thickens (viscosity rises), the heat generated cannot escape easily (thermal runaway risk), and the texture becomes uneven at the edges if not stirred (mixing becomes critical). Just as a baker must control oven temperature, an engineer must control jacket temperature, agitation, and conversion to obtain a uniform product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants / Standard Metrics:**
> - **Gel effect onset conversion:** ~10–20 % for MMA at 70 °C
> - **Auto-acceleration factor:** $k_t$ drops by 2–3 orders of magnitude near high conversion
> - **Standard MWD breadth parameter:** $\dfrac{\bar{M_w}}{\bar{M_n}} \approx 1.5$–$2.0$ for typical free-radical bulk polymerization

---

## 1.2 Solution Polymerization Reactor Modelling

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Solution polymerization is a homogeneous free-radical polymerization in which the monomer and initiator are dissolved in an inert, non-reactive solvent. The solvent acts as a **heat-transfer medium** and viscosity reducer, allowing isothermal operation. Reactor modelling requires accounting for **chain transfer to solvent** and dilution effects on kinetics, leading to a broader but more controllable molecular weight distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> Solution polymerization is the workhorse route for **SBR, PVA, polyacrylates, and acrylic coatings** because the low viscosity permits excellent heat removal in large CSTR cascades.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine dissolving sugar (monomer) in water (solvent) and then slowly evaporating the water as the sugar caramelizes into a long-chain polymer. The water keeps the mixture stirrable and cool — preventing the "burning" (runaway exotherm) you would get if you tried to caramelize dry sugar. The trade-off: some sugar chains break when water molecules collide with them (chain transfer to solvent), giving a broader, more diverse polymer length distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants for Solution Polymerization:**
> - **Typical solvent load:** 30–60 wt % of reactor charge
> - **Chain transfer constant** $C_s = k_{tr,s}/k_p$ ranges from $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$
> - **Target $\bar{M_w}/\bar{M_n}$:** 1.8–3.5 depending on solvent choice

---

## 1.3 Precipitation Polymerization Reactor Modelling

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Precipitation (heterogeneous) polymerization is a free-radical polymerization in which the monomer is initially soluble in the reaction medium, but the **polymer formed is insoluble** and precipitates as a separate phase from the moment of its birth. Reactor modelling requires the simultaneous treatment of a continuous (liquid) phase and a dispersed (polymer-rich) phase using **population balance equations (PBE)** combined with kinetic balances.

> [!NOTE]
> Precipitation polymerization is industrially exploited for **PAN (polyacrylonitrile), PVC, and polyelectrolytes** where the product is obtained as a fine powder directly from the reactor.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of making rock candy — you dissolve sugar in hot water, but as it cools the sugar "precipitates" out as crystals (long chains) on the string. In precipitation polymerization, the polymer chains are *born* in solution but immediately become "insoluble rocks" that cluster together. Because the radicals become trapped inside these clusters, termination slows dramatically — a phenomenon exploited industrially but requiring careful population-balance modelling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heterogeneous Kinetics Marker:**
> - **Termination rate constant drop:** $k_t$ can fall by 3–5 orders of magnitude inside precipitated particles
> - **Captive radical lifetime:** minutes to hours (vs. milliseconds in solution)
> - **Resulting MWD:** often **bimodal** due to dual-site polymerization (continuous + particle phase)

---

## 1.4 Molecular Weight Distribution (MWD) Interpretation

The unifying goal of all three reactor models above is to **predict and interpret MWD** of the produced polymer, characterized quantitatively by:

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}, \qquad \bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$$

where $N_i$ is the number of chains of chain length $i$ and $M_i$ is the corresponding molecular weight. The ratio $\bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$ is the **polydispersity index (PDI)**, a key quality metric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Highlighted MWD Benchmarks:**
> - Flory "most probable" (disproportionation/chain transfer dominated): PDI = 2.0
> - Combination termination dominant: PDI = 1.5
> - Living polymerization (anionic): PDI → 1.0
> - Industrial "broad MWD" grades: PDI = 3–10 (e.g., LDPE from autoclave)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generalized Free-Radical Polymerization Kinetics

All three reactor types (bulk, solution, precipitation) share the same elementary kinetic steps; only the *boundary conditions* and *physical assumptions* differ.

### 2.1.1 The Four Elementary Steps

| Step | Reaction | Rate Expression |
|------|----------|-----------------|
| **Initiation** | $I \xrightarrow{k_d} 2R^\bullet$; $R^\bullet + M \xrightarrow{k_i} P_1^\bullet$ | $R_i = 2f k_d [I]$ |
| **Propagation** | $P_n^\bullet + M \xrightarrow{k_p} P_{n+1}^\bullet$ | $R_p = k_p [M][M^\bullet]$ |
| **Termination (combination)** | $P_n^\bullet + P_m^\bullet \xrightarrow{k_{tc}} M_{n+m}$ | $R_{tc} = k_{tc}[M^\bullet]^2$ |
| **Termination (disproportionation)** | $P_n^\bullet + P_m^\bullet \xrightarrow{k_{td}} M_n + M_m$ | $R_{td} = k_{td}[M^\bullet]^2$ |
| **Chain transfer to monomer** | $P_n^\bullet + M \xrightarrow{k_{tr,m}} M_n + P_1^\bullet$ | $R_{tr,m} = k_{tr,m}[M][M^\bullet]$ |
| **Chain transfer to solvent** | $P_n^\bullet + S \xrightarrow{k_{tr,s}} M_n + P_1^\bullet$ | $R_{tr,s} = k_{tr,s}[S][M^\bullet]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The total radical concentration at quasi-steady-state (QSSA) in a homogeneous system is:
> $$[M^\bullet]_{QSSA} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$
> where $k_t = k_{tc} + k_{td}$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---------|------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | $R_p = k_p[M]\sqrt{\dfrac{2fk_d[I]}{k_t}}$ | Polymerization rate (homogeneous QSSA) | mol L$^{-1}$ s$^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_{n,0}} + C_m + C_s \dfrac{[S]}{[M]}$ | Mayo equation (instantaneous DP$_n$) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\bar{X}_w/\bar{X}_n = 1 + p$ (disprop.) or $\approx 2$ (comb.) | PDI for most-probable distribution | PDI ≥ 1.5 |
| 4 | $k_t = k_{t,0} \exp\{-\alpha(f-f_c)\}$ for $f > f_c$ | Gel effect (Trommsdorff) correlation | Used in BULK only |
| 5 | $R_p = k_p[M][M^\bullet]_p$ with $[M^\bullet]_p$ from PBE | Precipitation polymerization rate | mol L$^{-1}$ s$^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $\lambda_k = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n^k [P_n]$ | $k$-th moment of "live" chains | mol L$^{-1}$ |
| 7 | $\mu_k = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n^k [M_n]$ | $k$-th moment of "dead" chains | mol L$^{-1}$ |
| 8 | $\dfrac{d\mu_k}{dt} = k_{td}\lambda_0 \lambda_k + \tfrac{1}{2}k_{tc}\sum_{i+j=n} \lambda_i \lambda_j$ | Moment equation for dead chains | Used in PBE closure |
| 9 | $\bar{X}_n = \mu_1 / \mu_0$ ; $\bar{X}_w = \mu_2 / \mu_1$ | Number- and weight-aver. DP | $\bar{X}_n \le \bar{X}_w$ always |
| 10 | $C_s = k_{tr,s}/k_p$ | Chain transfer constant to solvent | Dimensionless; tabulated |
| 11 | $N_A = \int \nu(L,t) \, dL$ | Total particle number in PBE | \# m$^{-3}$ |
| 12 | Batch time $\tau = \dfrac{[M]_0 - [M]_f}{R_p}$ | Space-time for target conversion | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> For BULK: $S = 0$ ⇒ $C_s[S]/[M] = 0$ in Mayo equation. For SOLUTION: $S > 0$ broadens MWD. For PRECIPITATION: the gel effect dominates even at low conversion because the polymer-rich phase traps radicals.

---

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

**Why quasi-steady-state assumption (QSSA)?**
Radicals are produced and consumed at rates vastly larger than their accumulation rate. Their lifetime (~1 s) is orders of magnitude smaller than the batch time (hours). Setting $d[M^\bullet]/dt = 0$ collapses stiff ODEs into algebraic expressions.

**Why gel effect (Trommsdorff effect)?**
In bulk and precipitation polymerization, viscosity rises with conversion. Diffusion-controlled termination slows ($\sim$3 orders of magnitude) while propagation remains chemically controlled. The result: $\bar{X}_n$ shoots up at intermediate conversion, and the MWD becomes broad and sometimes bimodal.

**Why chain transfer to solvent matters in solution polymerization?**
Each transfer event terminates one growing chain and starts another. The mean chain length drops, and the distribution broadens. Selecting a solvent with low $C_s$ (e.g., toluene vs. CCl$_4$) is a *molecular design lever* for tuning MWD.

**Why Population Balance Equations (PBE) for precipitation?**
The precipitated particles form a *new population* with its own size distribution. Mass balances on a continuous spectrum of particle sizes are required — a single lumped equation cannot capture the heterogeneous kinetics inside the particles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Method of Moments for Free-Radical Polymerization (Batch Reactor)

### 3.1.1 Governing ODEs for Live and Dead Chains

Define:
- $\lambda_k = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n^k [P_n]$ — moment of **live** radical chains
- $\mu_k = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n^k [M_n]$ — moment of **dead** chains

For free-radical kinetics with initiation, propagation, termination (combination + disproportionation), and chain transfer, the moment balance in a **batch reactor** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d\lambda_0}{dt} &= R_i - (k_{td}+k_{tc})\lambda_0^2 - k_{tr,m}\lambda_0[M] - k_{tr,s}\lambda_0[S] \\
\dfrac{d\lambda_1}{dt} &= R_i + k_p[M]\lambda_0 - (k_{td}+k_{tc})\lambda_0\lambda_1 - k_{tr,m}\lambda_0[M] - k_{tr,s}\lambda_0[S] \\
\dfrac{d\lambda_2}{dt} &= R_i + k_p[M](2\lambda_1+\lambda_0) - (k_{td}+k_{tc})\lambda_0\lambda_2 - k_{tr,m}\lambda_0[M] - k_{tr,s}\lambda_0[S] \\
\dfrac{d\mu_0}{dt} &= \left(k_{td}+\tfrac{1}{2}k_{tc}\right)\lambda_0^2 + (k_{tr,m}[M]+k_{tr,s}[S])\lambda_0 \\
\dfrac{d\mu_1}{dt} &= (k_{td}+k_{tc})\lambda_0\lambda_1 + (k_{tr,m}[M]+k_{tr,s}[S])\lambda_0 \\
\dfrac{d\mu_2}{dt} &= (k_{td}+k_{tc})\lambda_0\lambda_2 + (k_{tr,m}[M]+k_{tr,s}[S])\lambda_0 + k_{tc}\lambda_1^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of $\mu_0$ Equation

**Step 1:** Identify all events that *create* a dead chain of length $n$:
- Disproportionation: $P_n^\bullet + P_m^\bullet \rightarrow M_n + M_m$ — rate $k_{td}[P_n^\bullet][P_m^\bullet]$
- Combination: $P_i^\bullet + P_j^\bullet \rightarrow M_{i+j}$ — rate $k_{tc}[P_i^\bullet][P_j^\bullet]$ (here $i+j = n$)
- Chain transfer: $P_n^\bullet + M \rightarrow M_n + P_1^\bullet$ — rate $k_{tr,m}[M][P_n^\bullet]$
- Chain transfer to solvent: similar with $k_{tr,s}[S][P_n^\bullet]$

**Step 2:** Write the population balance for a dead chain of length $n$:

$$
\dfrac{d[M_n]}{dt} = k_{td}[P_n^\bullet]\sum_{m}[P_m^\bullet] + \tfrac{1}{2}k_{tc}\sum_{i+j=n}[P_i^\bullet][P_j^\bullet] + (k_{tr,m}[M]+k_{tr,s}[S])[P_n^\bullet]
$$

**Step 3:** Sum over all $n$ from 1 to $\infty$ to obtain the zeroth moment of dead chains:

$$
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{d[M_n]}{dt} = k_{td}\lambda_0 \lambda_0 + \tfrac{1}{2}k_{tc}\lambda_0^2 + (k_{tr,m}[M]+k_{tr,s}[S])\lambda_0
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $k_{td}\lambda_0^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}k_{tc}\lambda_0^2 = (k_{td}+\tfrac{1}{2}k_{tc})\lambda_0^2$:

$$
\dfrac{d\mu_0}{dt} = \left(k_{td}+\tfrac{1}{2}k_{tc}\right)\lambda_0^2 + (k_{tr,m}[M]+k_{tr,s}[S])\lambda_0
$$

This matches the formula sheet entry #8.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of $\bar{X}_n$ and $\bar{X}_w$ from Moments

$$
\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{\mu_1}{\mu_0}, \qquad \bar{X}_w = \dfrac{\mu_2}{\mu_1}
$$

**Proof (instantaneous chain length):** At high conversion, with steady $[M^\bullet]$, the rate of chain formation (dead chain *number*) is $R_N = R_i + (k_{tr,m}[M]+k_{tr,s}[S])[M^\bullet]$ and the rate of monomer consumption is $R_p = k_p[M][M^\bullet]$. Then:

$$
\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{R_N}{R_p} = \dfrac{R_i + (k_{tr,m}[M]+k_{tr,s}[S])[M^\bullet]}{k_p[M][M^\bullet]} = \dfrac{R_i}{R_p} + C_m + C_s\dfrac{[S]}{[M]}
$$

Substituting $R_i = 2fk_d[I]$ and the QSSA expression recovers the Mayo equation. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Gel Effect Correlation for Bulk Polymerization

The Trommsdorff–Norrish effect is captured by an empirical correlation for $k_t$ as a function of fractional conversion $f$:

$$
k_t(f) = 
\begin{cases}
k_{t,0} & f \le f_c \\
k_{t,0} \exp\left[-A\left(f - f_c\right)\right] & f > f_c
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 1 — Diffusion-control onset:**
At conversion $f_c$, polymer chains begin to entangle; chain mobility drops; $k_t$ falls.

**Step 2 — Semi-empirical fit:**
The constant $A$ is fitted experimentally (e.g., for MMA at 70 °C, $A \approx 6.5$, $f_c \approx 0.15$).

**Step 3 — Effect on $\bar{X}_n$:**
As $k_t$ drops, $[M^\bullet]$ rises (since $[M^\bullet] = \sqrt{2fk_d[I]/k_t}$), so $R_p$ accelerates and $\bar{X}_n$ increases sharply.

**Step 4 — Effect on MWD:**
The local chain length distribution is **no longer most-probable**; it becomes skewed toward very long chains, pushing $\bar{M}_w/\bar{M}_n$ well above 2.0.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: PBE for Precipitation Polymerization

In precipitation polymerization, polymer particles nucleate and grow. Define $N(L,t) \, dL$ = number of particles per unit volume with size in $[L, L+dL]$.

$$
\dfrac{\partial N}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial L}\left(G(L,t)\, N\right) = B(L,t) - D(L,t)
$$

where:
- $G(L,t) = $ particle growth rate = $k_p[M]_p \rho_p^{-1}$ (monomer flux converted to length)
- $B(L,t) = $ nucleation rate (Dirac delta at critical size $L^*$)
- $D(L,t) = $ death rate (coagulation/aggregation)

**Step 1 — Nucleation:** Particles appear at critical size $L^* = (36\pi v_p^2 N_A^2 / \rho_p^2)^{1/3}$ via oligomer precipitation. Source term: $B = R_{nucl}\delta(L-L^*)$.

**Step 2 — Growth:** Once nucleated, particles swell with monomer. Growth rate $G = k_p[M]_p / (N_A \rho_p)$.

**Step 3 — Mass balance closure:** Total polymer mass = mass in particles = $\rho_p \int_0^\infty \tfrac{\pi}{6}L^3 N(L,t)\,dL$.

**Step 4 — Dual-site kinetics:** Polymerization proceeds in BOTH the continuous phase (low $R_p$) and the particle phase (high $R_p$ because of trapped radicals). The total rate is:

$$
R_{p,\text{total}} = k_p[M]_c [M^\bullet]_c + k_p[M]_p [M^\bullet]_p
$$

with $[M^\bullet]_p$ given by a separate balance inside the particle (often called the "Smith–Ewart" equation in emulsion polymerization, adapted here for precipitation).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generalized Polymerization Reactor Model Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reactor Feed: Monomer M, Initiator I, Solvent S] --> B[Mixing and Heat Transfer Block]
    B --> C[Kinetic Rate Block: Ri, Rp, Rt]
    C --> D[Species Mass Balances: dM/dt, dI/dt, dS/dt]
    C --> E[Moment Equations: lambda k, mu k]
    E --> F[MWD Output: Mn, Mw, PDI]
    D --> F
    C --> G[Energy Balance: dT/dt]
    G --> B
    D --> H[Viscosity Model]
    H --> I[Gel Effect Corrector: kt = kt f]
    I --> C
    E --> J[Population Balance PBE]
    J --> K[Particle Size Distribution]
    K --> F
    F --> L[Validation with GPC / SEC Data]
    L --> M[Industrial MWD Database]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Three Reactor Modes Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BULK[BULK POLYMERIZATION]
        B1[Pure Monomer] --> B2[High Viscosity]
        B2 --> B3[Gel Effect Active]
        B3 --> B4[High Mn and Broad MWD]
    end
    subgraph SOL[SOLUTION POLYMERIZATION]
        S1[Monomer + Solvent] --> S2[Low Viscosity]
        S2 --> S3[Chain Transfer to Solvent]
        S3 --> S4[Lower Mn and Broad MWD]
    end
    subgraph PPT[PRECIPITATION POLYMERIZATION]
        P1[Monomer Soluble] --> P2[Polymer Insoluble]
        P2 --> P3[Particle Phase Forms]
        P3 --> P4[Bimodal MWD and High Mn]
    end
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Method of Moments Computational Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Initialize: t=0, lambda k = 0, mu k = 0] --> S1[Compute Radical Conc by QSSA]
    S1 --> S2[Compute Growth and Termination Rates]
    S2 --> S3[Update lambda k by ODEs]
    S3 --> S4[Update mu k by ODEs]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Xn, Xw, PDI]
    S5 --> S6{Conversion target reached?}
    S6 -- No --> S7[Advance dt]
    S7 --> S1
    S6 -- Yes --> S8[Output MWD curve and moments]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Industrial MWD Interpretation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Industrial Reactor] --> B[Online SEC or GPC]
    B --> C[Chromatogram dt vs Log M]
    C --> D[Deconvolution to Peaks]
    D --> E[Assign to Mechanism]
    E --> F[Bulk: Single Broad Peak]
    E --> G[Solution: Broad with Solvent Tail]
    E --> H[Precipitation: Bimodal]
    F --> I[Quality Control Report]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams map the **information flow** in modelling — they show how kinetics, moment equations, and population balances couple to predict the experimentally observed MWD. The coupling loop between viscosity → gel effect → kinetics is the unique feature of bulk and precipitation systems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the gel (Trommsdorff) effect in bulk polymerization and state its consequences on the MWD of the polymer produced.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The gel effect (Trommsdorff–Norrish effect) is the auto-acceleration of polymerization rate observed at intermediate-to-high conversion in bulk polymerization, caused by the **diffusion-controlled drop in the termination rate constant $k_t$** as viscosity rises. Consequences on MWD:
- $\bar{M}_n$ increases sharply at the onset conversion $f_c$ (~10–20 %).
- The MWD broadens and can become bimodal.
- $\bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$ rises well above the Flory value of 2.0 (often 3–5).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Explain why chain transfer to solvent is critical in determining MWD during solution polymerization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Chain transfer to solvent terminates a growing polymer radical and starts a new chain, governed by $C_s = k_{tr,s}/k_p$. As $C_s$ or $[S]/[M]$ increases, the **number-average chain length $\bar{X}_n$ decreases** (per the Mayo equation) and the **molecular weight distribution broadens** because newly initiated chains grow in parallel with older ones. Industrially, solvent selection is a key lever for tailoring $\bar{M}_n$ and PDI.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the method-of-moments equations for the zeroth, first, and second moments of **dead** chains in a batch free-radical polymerization reactor, including chain transfer to monomer and solvent. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk polymerization of styrene is conducted in a batch reactor at 80 °C. Given: $k_p = 2.0 \times 10^3$ L mol$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$, $k_{t,0} = 1.2 \times 10^7$ L mol$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$, $k_d = 4.0 \times 10^{-5}$ s$^{-1}$, $f = 0.6$, $[I]_0 = 0.02$ mol L$^{-1}$, $[M]_0 = 8.0$ mol L$^{-1}$, $C_m = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$. Compute (i) the initial $R_p$ and (ii) the initial $\bar{X}_n$ assuming chain transfer to monomer is the dominant chain-stopping event. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
[Refer to Section 3.1.2 of these notes. State the four creation events for dead chains, write the species balance for $M_n$, sum over $n$, and arrive at the three moment equations for $\mu_0, \mu_1, \mu_2$.]

**(b) Computation (7 marks):**

**Step (i) — Initial $R_p$:**
QSSA radical concentration:

$$
[M^\bullet] = \sqrt{\dfrac{2fk_d[I]_0}{k_{t,0}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 0.6 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.02}{1.2 \times 10^7}}
$$

$$
[M^\bullet] = \sqrt{\dfrac{9.6 \times 10^{-7}}{1.2 \times 10^7}} = \sqrt{8.0 \times 10^{-14}} = 2.83 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{mol L}^{-1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
R_p = k_p[M]_0[M^\bullet] = 2.0 \times 10^3 \times 8.0 \times 2.83 \times 10^{-7} = 4.53 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol L}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}
$$

**Step (ii) — Initial $\bar{X}_n$ (chain transfer dominated):**

$$
\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{C_m} = \dfrac{1}{1.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.0 \times 10^{4}
$$

(If initiator termination is also included: $\bar{X}_n = 1/[C_m + R_i/R_p]$ with $R_i = 2fk_d[I]_0 = 9.6 \times 10^{-7}$ mol L$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$, giving negligible correction.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a population balance equation (PBE), explain how **precipitation polymerization** differs in MWD prediction from bulk and solution polymerization. Discuss the origin of bimodality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CSTR is used for continuous solution polymerization of methyl methacrylate (MMA) in ethyl acetate. The feed contains $[M]_0 = 4.0$ mol L$^{-1}$, $[I]_0 = 0.01$ mol L$^{-1}$, $[S]_0 = 6.0$ mol L$^{-1}$. Mean residence time $\tau = 3600$ s. Given: $k_p = 1.5 \times 10^3$ L mol$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$, $k_t = 1.0 \times 10^7$ L mol$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$, $k_d = 3.0 \times 10^{-5}$ s$^{-1}$, $f = 0.55$, $C_s = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$. Compute the steady-state monomer conversion and $\bar{X}_n$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) PBE-based explanation (7 marks):**
[Refer to Section 3.3 of these notes. Highlight the dual-site nature, the role of nucleation, and the bimodality arising from the superposition of (i) low-MW chains grown in the continuous phase and (ii) high-MW chains grown inside the precipitated particles where radical termination is suppressed.]

**(b) CSTR steady-state solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Steady-state radical concentration in CSTR:**

$$
[M^\bullet] = \sqrt{\dfrac{2fk_d[I]}{k_t}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 0.55 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.01}{1.0 \times 10^7}} = 5.74 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{mol L}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Steady-state monomer balance:**

$$
\tau = \dfrac{[M]_0 - [M]}{R_p} \quad \Rightarrow \quad [M]_0 - [M] = \tau k_p [M][M^\bullet]
$$

$$
1.0 - \dfrac{[M]}{[M]_0} = \tau k_p [M^\bullet] \cdot \dfrac{[M]}{[M]_0} \cdot [M]_0 / [M]_0
$$

Let $x = ([M]_0 - [M])/[M]_0$ be the conversion. Then $[M] = [M]_0(1-x)$ and:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{[M]_0 x}{k_p [M]_0 (1-x) [M^\bullet]} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \dfrac{\tau k_p [M^\bullet]}{1 + \tau k_p [M^\bullet]}
$$

Compute $\tau k_p [M^\bullet] = 3600 \times 1.5 \times 10^3 \times 5.74 \times 10^{-8} = 0.310$. Then:

$$
x = \dfrac{0.310}{1.310} = 0.237 = 23.7\%
$$

**Step 3 — Instantaneous $\bar{X}_n$ (Mayo):**

$$
\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = C_s \dfrac{[S]}{[M]} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times \dfrac{6.0}{4.0 \times (1-0.237)} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.965 = 4.91 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\bar{X}_n = 2035
$$

(Ignoring initiator termination since $R_i / R_p \approx 3.3 \times 10^{-6}$.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in this module:**
> 1. **Confusing $\lambda_k$ and $\mu_k$:** Live-chain vs. dead-chain moments are different; using the wrong one in the moment-balance derivation loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ in combination termination:** The factor $\tfrac{1}{2} k_{tc}$ appears in $d\mu_0/dt$ because each combination event creates one dead chain, not two.
> 3. **Applying the QSSA to a CSTR incorrectly:** In a CSTR, the QSSA for radicals gives the SAME algebraic form as in a batch (because radicals are extremely short-lived), but the *monomer* balance is algebraic, not ODE.
> 4. **Ignoring the gel effect in bulk polymerization at high conversion:** At $f > 0.2$ for MMA, $k_t$ drops by orders of magnitude; using constant $k_t$ overestimates $R_p$ and underestimates $\bar{M}_n$.
> 5. **Treating precipitation polymerization as homogeneous:** The single largest conceptual error. The dual-phase PBE is mandatory.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bulk polymerization** is pure monomer; gel effect and high viscosity dominate; broad MWD with PDI > 2.
- **Solution polymerization** uses a diluent solvent; lower viscosity, better heat removal, but $C_s$ broadens MWD via chain transfer.
- **Precipitation polymerization** is heterogeneous from the start; polymer precipitates; PBE needed; often bimodal MWD.
- The **Mayo equation** $\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_{n,0}} + C_m + C_s \dfrac{[S]}{[M]}$ is the *master key* to instantaneous chain length.
- **Method of moments** with $\lambda_k, \mu_k$ converts an infinite ODE system into 6 closed equations that yield $\bar{X}_n$ and $\bar{X}_w$.
- **QSSA** gives $[M^\bullet] = \sqrt{2fk_d[I]/k_t}$ — the foundation of all free-radical polymerization rates.
- **Gel effect correlation:** $k_t = k_{t,0} \exp[-A(f - f_c)]$ for $f > f_c$; mandatory in bulk and precipitation models.
- **PDI benchmarks:** 1.0 (living), 1.5 (combination), 2.0 (Flory), 3–10 (industrial broad MWD, bimodal).
- **Heat removal** is the limiting design factor in bulk reactors; solution reactors decouple heat transfer and kinetics.
- For CSTR steady state: $x = \dfrac{\tau k_p [M^\bullet]}{1 + \tau k_p [M^\bullet]}$ is the universal monomer-conversion formula.
- **Examiners reward:** correct identification of dominant chain-stopping event; consistent units; clear separation of live and dead chain moments.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suspension Polymerization: Principles, Reactor Modelling and MWD Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT745 - POLYMER REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Suspension Polymerization: Principles, Reactor Modelling and MWD Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Suspension Polymerization** (also termed *Pearl Polymerization* or *Bead Polymerization*) is a heterophase polymerization technique in which a monomer, essentially immiscible with the continuous phase (usually **water**), is dispersed mechanically as discrete liquid droplets (size range **0.01 cm to 1.0 cm**) within that continuous phase. Polymerization is initiated by a **monomer-soluble (oil-soluble) free-radical initiator** dissolved in the droplets. Each droplet acts as an independent micro-reactor where bulk-polymerization-like kinetics prevail.

**The four obligatory ingredients of any suspension polymerization recipe are:**

1. **Continuous Phase:** Deionized water (typically **50 – 80 wt %** of the total charge) — provides heat removal and dispersion medium.
2. **Dispersed Phase:** Vinyl monomer + oil-soluble initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide, AIBN, lauroyl peroxide).
3. **Suspending Agent / Stabilizer:** Water-soluble protective colloid (PVA, PVP, CMC, methyl cellulose) or finely divided inorganic powder (Ca₃(PO₄)₂, MgCO₃, BaSO₄).
4. **Mechanical Agitator:** Stirred-tank reactor providing controlled shear for droplet breakup and dispersion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Boiling Ocean of Pearls"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine pouring a few drops of vegetable oil into a pot of vigorously boiling water. The oil spontaneously breaks up into spherical droplets because oil and water do not mix. If, instead of oil, you put **liquid styrene + initiator** in water and stir, you get **millions of tiny styrene droplets** floating like pearls in the ocean. Now heat the pot — the initiator decomposes inside *each pearl*, polymerization proceeds inside *every individual droplet*, and you finally harvest solid **polystyrene beads** filtered from the water.
> The water is **NOT a reactant**; it is the *cooling jacket* and *anti-stick coating* (via stabilizers) that prevents the pearls from re-merging back into a single sticky lump.

### 1.3 Why Suspension Polymerization is Industrially Important

> [!TIP]
> Suspension polymerization is the preferred industrial route for **PVC (polyvinyl chloride)**, **expanded polystyrene (EPS)**, **PMMA pearls**, **ion-exchange resins**, and **SAN beads**. It solves the two biggest headaches of bulk polymerization:
> - **Viscosity runaway** (auto-acceleration / Trommsdorff effect) is contained because each droplet is small.
> - **Heat removal** is trivial because water has a heat capacity ≈ **4.18 J g⁻¹ K⁻¹**, ~**4× that of monomer**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Range |
|---|---|
| Drop/particle diameter | **50 µm – 2 mm** |
| Water-to-monomer weight ratio | **1:1 to 4:1** |
| Agitator speed | **200 – 1000 rpm** |
| Stabilizer concentration | **0.01 – 1.0 wt %** of water phase |
| Reactor temperature | **40 – 90 °C** |
| Reactor volume (industrial) | **10 – 200 m³** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> Suspension polymerization is governed by **three concurrent sub-processes**. The KTU examiner expects you to enumerate them.

**Step 1 — Droplet Formation (Physical Stage):**
Mechanical agitation supplies turbulent shear forces. These shear forces overcome the interfacial tension ($\sigma$) holding the monomer bulk together. The monomer mass breaks into droplets of characteristic size.

**Step 2 — Droplet Stabilization (Colloidal Stage):**
Stabilizer molecules migrate to the monomer-water interface and form a protective film that:
- Prevents coalescence (collision-merging) of droplets.
- Provides steric/electrostatic repulsion.

**Step 3 — Polymerization Inside Each Droplet (Chemical Stage):**
The oil-soluble initiator decomposes thermally inside each droplet, generating radicals. Chain propagation, termination, and transfer reactions occur *exactly as in bulk polymerization*, because the droplet is essentially a bulk mini-reactor.

### 2.2 The Critical Force Balance on a Droplet

The mean droplet diameter ($D_p$) at steady state is set by the dynamic equilibrium between **two opposing forces**:

- **Dispersing force (shear):** proportional to $\rho_c \, N^2 \, D_i^3$ (where $\rho_c$ is continuous-phase density, $N$ is impeller speed, $D_i$ is impeller diameter).
- **Cohesive force (interfacial tension):** proportional to $\sigma \, g_c$ (where $\sigma$ is interfacial tension and $g_c$ is a gravitational constant).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD EQUATION — Mean Sauter Mean Diameter:**
> $$D_{32} \;=\; C \left(\dfrac{\sigma^{\,0.6}}{\rho_c^{\,0.6}\, N^{1.2}\, D_i^{\,0.8}}\right) \left(\dfrac{\mu_d^{\,0.1}}{\mu_c^{\,0.1}}\right) \left(\dfrac{V_d}{V_c}\right)^{0.1}$$
> Where $\mu_d$ = dispersed-phase viscosity, $\mu_c$ = continuous-phase viscosity, $V_d / V_c$ = phase ratio.

**Interpretation for the student:** Higher $N$ (stirring) → smaller beads. Higher $\sigma$ → bigger beads. Higher stabilizer concentration → smaller beads (because it reduces effective $\sigma$).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **All exam-relevant equations in one consolidated table. No `|` characters used — vertical bars rendered with `\vert`.**

| # | Concept | Equation | Description of Terms |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Weber number (dimensionless) | $\text{We} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_c\, N^2\, D_i^3}{\sigma\, g_c}$ | Ratio of inertial (dispersing) force to interfacial (cohesive) force |
| 2 | Sauter mean diameter | $D_{32} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum n_i D_i^3}{\sum n_i D_i^2}$ | Surface-volume mean diameter of bead population |
| 3 | Rate of polymerization inside a droplet | $R_p \;=\; k_p\, [M]\,\sqrt{\dfrac{f\, k_d\, [I]}{k_t}}$ | Bulk-like free-radical kinetics, [M] = monomer conc. inside droplet |
| 4 | Number-average DP (general) | $\bar{X}_n \;=\; \dfrac{R_p}{R_t + R_{tr,M} + R_{tr,S}}$ | $R_t$ = termination, $R_{tr,M}$ = transfer to monomer, $R_{tr,S}$ = transfer to solvent/CTA |
| 5 | $\bar{X}_n$ with chain transfer to monomer | $\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_{n,0}} + C_M$ | $C_M = k_{tr,M}/k_p$ is the chain-transfer constant to monomer |
| 6 | $\bar{X}_n$ with chain transfer to solvent/CTA | $\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_{n,0}} + C_S\,\dfrac{[S]}{[M]}$ | $C_S = k_{tr,S}/k_p$ |
| 7 | Polydispersity Index (PDI) | $\text{PDI} \;=\; \dfrac{\bar{X}_w}{\bar{X}_n}$ | $\text{PDI} \approx 1.5$ for termination by combination; $\approx 2.0$ for disproportionation |
| 8 | $\bar{X}_w$ for combination termination | $\bar{X}_w \;=\; \dfrac{2}{\left(2 - \dfrac{\bar{X}_n}{\bar{X}_{n,0}}\right)^{2}}\left[1 - \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\bar{X}_n}{\bar{X}_{n,0}}\right)^{2}\right]\bar{X}_n$ | Most free-radical systems use this |
| 9 | Conversion vs time (batch droplet) | $x \;=\; 1 - \exp\!\left(-k_p \sqrt{\dfrac{f k_d [I]_0}{k_t}}\,t\right)$ | Valid when [I] is approximately constant (low $k_d$) |
| 10 | Heat removal capacity of water | $Q \;=\; \dot{m}_w\, C_{p,w}\, \Delta T$ | Demonstrates why water is the perfect heat sink |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Why does every engineer in the PVC industry care about $D_{32}$ and PDI?
> - **$D_{32}$** controls downstream handling: beads that are too fine clog filters; beads that are too coarse take too long to plasticize in the extruder.
> - **PDI** controls mechanical properties: a wider MWD gives better melt strength for extrusion; a narrow MWD gives better transparency and impact strength.
> - **Heat removal via water** is the reason suspension PVC reactors can be scaled up to **200 m³** whereas bulk polymerization reactors rarely exceed **50 m³**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mean Droplet Diameter from Force Balance

**Starting principle:** At dynamic steady state, the rate of droplet breakup by shear equals the rate of droplet coalescence by collision. The droplet size distribution is set by the balance between disruptive (turbulent) and restoring (interfacial + viscous) stresses.

**Step 1 — Disruptive (shear) stress imparted by impeller:**

$$\tau_d \;=\; \rho_c \, N^2 \, D_i^2$$

This is the kinetic energy per unit volume of the turbulent eddies generated by an impeller of diameter $D_i$ rotating at speed $N$.

**Step 2 — Restoring (interfacial) stress resisting breakup:**

$$\tau_r \;=\; \sigma \left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} + \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)$$

For a spherical droplet of radius $r$, both principal radii equal $r$, giving:

$$\tau_r \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\sigma}{r}$$

**Step 3 — Setting the stresses equal at the critical droplet radius ($r_{crit}$):**

$$\rho_c \, N^2 \, D_i^2 \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\sigma}{r_{crit}}$$

**Step 4 — Solving for $r_{crit}$:**

$$r_{crit} \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\sigma}{\rho_c \, N^2 \, D_i^2}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to diameter and introduce the dimensionless Weber number:**

$$D_{32} \;=\; 2\,r_{crit} \;=\; \dfrac{4\,\sigma}{\rho_c \, N^2 \, D_i^2}$$

Define the critical Weber number $\text{We}_{crit}$ such that breakup occurs when $\text{We} \geq \text{We}_{crit}$:

$$\text{We}_{crit} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_c \, N^2 \, D_i^3}{\sigma \, g_c}$$

Combining these and rearranging:

$$\boxed{\;D_{32} \;=\; C_1 \left(\dfrac{\sigma \, g_c}{\rho_c \, N^2 \, D_i^3}\right)^{0.6} \left(\dfrac{\mu_d}{\mu_c}\right)^{0.1} \left(\dfrac{V_d}{V_c}\right)^{0.1}\;}$$

where $C_1$ is an empirical constant (≈ **0.068** for stirred tanks). This matches Eq. 2 of the formula sheet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** To halve the bead size, the agitator speed must be increased by a factor of $2^{1/0.6} \approx 3.17$. Agitation is a very powerful handle for particle size control.

### 3.2 Derivation: Number-Average Degree of Polymerization ($\bar{X}_n$)

**Starting principle:** By definition,

$$\bar{X}_n \;=\; \dfrac{\text{rate of monomer consumption}}{\text{rate of chain termination (any form)}}$$

Numerator — rate of monomer consumption:

$$R_p \;=\; -\dfrac{d[M]}{dt} \;=\; k_p\, [M]\, [M^\bullet]$$

Denominator — total rate of chain-ending events. Each termination event kills 2 radicals (in combination) or 2 radicals (in disproportionation), so the *rate of chain termination* is:

$$R_t \;=\; 2\, k_t\, [M^\bullet]^2$$

(coefficient 2 — each termination consumes two chains).

Including chain transfer to monomer ($M$) and chain transfer to solvent/CTA ($S$):

$$R_{tr,M} \;=\; k_{tr,M}\, [M]\, [M^\bullet]$$

$$R_{tr,S} \;=\; k_{tr,S}\, [S]\, [M^\bullet]$$

Therefore:

$$\bar{X}_n \;=\; \dfrac{k_p\, [M]\, [M^\bullet]}{2\, k_t\, [M^\bullet]^2 + k_{tr,M}\, [M]\, [M^\bullet] + k_{tr,S}\, [S]\, [M^\bullet]}$$

Cancel $[M^\bullet]$ from numerator and denominator:

$$\bar{X}_n \;=\; \dfrac{k_p\, [M]}{2\, k_t\, [M^\bullet] + k_{tr,M}\, [M] + k_{tr,S}\, [S]}$$

Now use the steady-state radical concentration:

$$[M^\bullet]_{ss} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{f\, k_d\, [I]}{k_t}}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{\;\bar{X}_n \;=\; \dfrac{k_p\, [M]}{2\, k_t \sqrt{\dfrac{f\, k_d\, [I]}{k_t}} + k_{tr,M}\, [M] + k_{tr,S}\, [S]}\;}$$

If only chain transfer to monomer matters (the usual case for styrene in suspension), set $k_{tr,S} = 0$:

$$\bar{X}_n \;=\; \dfrac{k_p\, [M]}{2 \sqrt{f\, k_d\, k_t\, [I]} + k_{tr,M}\, [M]}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $k_p\, [M]$:

$$\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} \;=\; \dfrac{2\sqrt{f\, k_d\, k_t\, [I]}}{k_p\, [M]} + \dfrac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_{n,0}} + C_M$$

where $\bar{X}_{n,0}$ is the DP in the absence of chain transfer, and $C_M \equiv k_{tr,M}/k_p$ is the Mayo chain-transfer constant to monomer.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem statement (model):** Styrene suspension polymerization is carried out at 80 °C with benzoyl peroxide initiator. Given:
> - $k_p = 3.0 \times 10^{2}$ L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹
> - $k_t = 1.5 \times 10^{7}$ L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹
> - $k_d = 5.0 \times 10^{-5}$ s⁻¹
> - $f = 0.6$
> - $[M]_0 = 8.7$ mol L⁻¹ (bulk styrene)
> - $[I]_0 = 0.02$ mol L⁻¹
> - $C_M = 6 \times 10^{-5}$
> Assume termination by combination. Compute $\bar{X}_n$, $\bar{X}_w$, and PDI.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute steady-state radical concentration:**

$$[M^\bullet]_{ss} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{f\, k_d\, [I]_0}{k_t}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{0.6 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.02}{1.5 \times 10^{7}}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\dfrac{6.0 \times 10^{-7}}{1.5 \times 10^{7}}} \;=\; \sqrt{4.0 \times 10^{-14}} \;=\; 2.0 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{mol L}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\bar{X}_n$:**

Using the DP equation derived above:

$$\bar{X}_n \;=\; \dfrac{k_p\, [M]}{2\, k_t\, [M^\bullet]_{ss} + k_{tr,M}\, [M]}$$

First compute $k_{tr,M} = C_M \times k_p = 6 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.0 \times 10^{2} = 1.8 \times 10^{-2}$ L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹.

$$2\, k_t\, [M^\bullet]_{ss} \;=\; 2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{7} \times 2.0 \times 10^{-7} \;=\; 6.0 \; \text{s}^{-1}$$

$$k_{tr,M}\, [M] \;=\; 1.8 \times 10^{-2} \times 8.7 \;=\; 0.157 \; \text{s}^{-1}$$

$$k_p\, [M] \;=\; 3.0 \times 10^{2} \times 8.7 \;=\; 2610 \; \text{s}^{-1}$$

$$\bar{X}_n \;=\; \dfrac{2610}{6.0 + 0.157} \;=\; \dfrac{2610}{6.157} \;\approx\; 424$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\bar{X}_w$ for combination termination:**

The full Flory most-probable distribution for combination termination gives:

$$\bar{X}_w \;=\; \dfrac{(2 + p)(1 + p)}{2(1 + p/2)^2}\,\bar{X}_n$$

where $p = \bar{X}_n / \bar{X}_{n,0}$ is the probability of propagation without chain transfer. Computing $\bar{X}_{n,0}$:

$$\bar{X}_{n,0} \;=\; \dfrac{k_p\, [M]}{2\, k_t\, [M^\bullet]_{ss}} \;=\; \dfrac{2610}{6.0} \;=\; 435$$

$$p \;=\; \dfrac{424}{435} \;=\; 0.975$$

For $p \to 1$, $\bar{X}_w / \bar{X}_n \to 1.5$, so:

$$\bar{X}_w \;\approx\; 1.5 \times 424 \;=\; 636$$

**Step 4 — Compute PDI:**

$$\text{PDI} \;=\; \dfrac{\bar{X}_w}{\bar{X}_n} \;=\; \dfrac{636}{424} \;\approx\; 1.50$$

**Conclusion:** As expected for free-radical combination termination with negligible chain transfer, PDI ≈ **1.5**.

### 3.4 Reactor Modeling Assumption (Exam-Relevant)

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU insight:** Because each monomer droplet is small (≪ 1 mm), heat transfer within a droplet is fast, and the droplet is well-mixed internally. The **macroscopic reactor** (the stirred tank containing billions of droplets) is therefore modeled as:
> - **Batch reactor** if the entire charge is polymerized without adding/removing monomer.
> - **CSTR** if monomer is fed continuously and beads withdrawn.
> The polymerization kinetics inside each droplet are identical to **bulk polymerization**, so all the rate equations derived for bulk polymerization apply directly to a suspension droplet.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Suspension Polymerization Reactor

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of an industrial suspension polymerization reactor system. All node IDs are purely alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid v10+ syntax rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Water Reservoir] --> B1[Reactor Charge Tank]
    A2[Suspending Agent] --> B1
    A3[Monomer Feed] --> B2[Initiator Mixing Vessel]
    A4[Oil-Soluble Initiator] --> B2
    B2 --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Stirred Tank Reactor STR]
    C1 --> D1[Steam Jacket Heating]
    C1 --> D2[Cooling Water Coil]
    C1 --> E1[Droplet Formation Zone]
    E1 --> E2[Polymerization Inside Droplets]
    E2 --> F1[Bead Slurry Discharge]
    F1 --> G1[Centrifuge / Filter]
    G1 --> G2[Washing Stage]
    G2 --> G3[Drying Stage]
    G3 --> H1[Polymer Bead Product]
    G1 --> G4[Water Recycle Loop]
    G4 --> A1
    C1 --> I1[Temperature Controller]
    C1 --> I2[Agitator Speed Controller]
    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C1
```

**Reading the diagram:** The water + suspending agent is charged with monomer + initiator into the stirred tank reactor. Steam jacket heats the reactor to initiation temperature; cooling coils remove the exothermic heat of polymerization. Droplets form under agitation, polymerize internally, and exit as a slurry. The slurry is centrifuged, washed, and dried to yield the final bead product. The aqueous filtrate is recycled back to the water reservoir.

### 4.2 Microscopic Schematic — Droplet Mechanics Inside the Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Continuous Water Phase] --> P2[Shear Field from Impeller]
    P2 --> P3[Droplet Breakup]
    P3 --> P4[Smaller Droplets Stabilized by PVA Layer]
    P4 --> P5[Initiator Decomposition Inside Droplet]
    P5 --> P6[Radical Chain Propagation]
    P6 --> P7[Polymer Bead Solidification]
    P7 --> P8[Stabilizer Film Prevents Coalescence]
    P8 --> P1
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Suspension vs Bulk Polymerization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BulkPoly[Bulk Polymerization]
        B1[Pure Monomer + Initiator] --> B2[Viscosity Rises Sharply]
        B2 --> B3[Heat Removal Difficult]
        B3 --> B4[Autoacceleration Risk]
    end
    subgraph SuspPoly[Suspension Polymerization]
        S1[Monomer Droplets in Water] --> S2[Each Droplet = Mini Bulk Reactor]
        S2 --> S3[Water Absorbs Heat]
        S3 --> S4[Viscosity Stays Low]
        S4 --> S5[Easy Scaleup to 200 m3]
    end
    BulkPoly --> Compare[Tradeoff Analysis]
    SuspPoly --> Compare
    Compare --> Decision[Choose Suspension for Large Scale PVC EPS PMMA]
```
