<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question carries **3 marks** and expects a 4–6 sentence precise answer plus any required equation. Model answers are provided.

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`**
**(3 Marks)** *List the four essential components of a suspension polymerization recipe and state the role of each.*

**Model Answer:**

The four essential components are: (i) **continuous aqueous phase** (water, typically 50–80 wt %), which provides dispersion and heat removal; (ii) **dispersed monomer phase** (e.g., styrene, VCM, MMA) containing (iii) a **monomer-soluble free-radical initiator** such as benzoyl peroxide or AIBN; and (iv) a **suspending agent / stabilizer** (PVA, PVP, CMC, or Ca₃(PO₄)₂) that coats the droplets and prevents coalescence. Without any one of these four, suspension polymerization cannot proceed in a controlled manner. **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`**
**(3 Marks)** *Explain why suspension polymerization gives essentially the same MWD as bulk polymerization, and state the expected polydispersity index.*

**Model Answer:**

Because each monomer droplet (typically 50 µm – 2 mm) is small enough for internal heat and mass transfer to be negligible, polymerization inside a droplet follows the same free-radical kinetic scheme as bulk polymerization. Termination occurs predominantly by combination (for styrene, MMA) or disproportionation (for VCM, acrylates). The number-average DP follows $\bar{X}_n = R_p / R_t$, and the weight-average DP follows the Flory most-probable distribution. The polydispersity index is therefore approximately **1.5** for termination by combination and **2.0** for disproportionation, identical to bulk polymerization. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often confuse *stabilizer* with *surfactant*. Stabilizers are protective colloids (high-MW polymers) that work via **steric stabilization**, whereas surfactants are small molecules that work via **electrostatic stabilization**. Suspension polymerization relies primarily on **steric** stabilizers.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Students forget that the **initiator is monomer-soluble**, not water-soluble. A water-soluble initiator (like K₂S₂O₈) would give **emulsion polymerization**, not suspension polymerization. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a force-balance argument, derive the expression for the mean Sauter mean droplet diameter $D_{32}$ as a function of agitator speed $N$, impeller diameter $D_i$, interfacial tension $\sigma$, continuous-phase density $\rho_c$, and dispersed-phase viscosity $\mu_d$.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A suspension polymerization of styrene is carried out at 80 °C. Given $k_p = 3.0 \times 10^{2}$ L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹, $k_t = 1.5 \times 10^{7}$ L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹, $k_d = 5.0 \times 10^{-5}$ s⁻¹, $f = 0.6$, $[M]_0 = 8.7$ mol L⁻¹, $[I]_0 = 0.02$ mol L⁻¹, and $C_M = 6 \times 10^{-5}$, compute (i) $\bar{X}_n$, (ii) $\bar{X}_w$ assuming combination termination, and (iii) PDI.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $D_{32}$:** *(See SECTION 3.1 for the full derivation.)*

The disruptive stress from the impeller is $\tau_d = \rho_c N^2 D_i^2$.
The restoring interfacial stress is $\tau_r = 2\sigma / r$ for a sphere.
Equating at critical radius $r_{crit} = 2\sigma / (\rho_c N^2 D_i^2)$.
Converting to diameter and incorporating empirical correction factors gives:

$$\boxed{\;D_{32} \;=\; C_1 \left(\dfrac{\sigma \, g_c}{\rho_c \, N^2 \, D_i^3}\right)^{0.6} \left(\dfrac{\mu_d}{\mu_c}\right)^{0.1} \left(\dfrac{V_d}{V_c}\right)^{0.1}\;}$$

where $C_1 \approx 0.068$. The exponent **0.6** on the interfacial-tension group and **0.1** on the viscosity and volume-ratio groups are empirical corrections that account for non-ideal droplet-breakup behavior in turbulent flow. **[7 marks]**

**(b) Numerical Solution:** *(See SECTION 3.3 worked example for the full step-by-step.)*

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $[M^\bullet]_{ss}$ | $2.0 \times 10^{-7}$ mol L⁻¹ |
| $\bar{X}_{n,0}$ (no transfer) | 435 |
| $\bar{X}_n$ (with $C_M = 6 \times 10^{-5}$) | **424** |
| $\bar{X}_w$ (combination) | **636** |
| **PDI** | **1.50** |

**Final answers:** $\bar{X}_n = 424$, $\bar{X}_w = 636$, PDI $= 1.50$. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of suspending agents in suspension polymerization. How does the type and concentration of stabilizer affect (i) the average particle size and (ii) the MWD of the polymer beads? Mention at least two classes of stabilizers with examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A suspension PVC reactor of volume 30 m³ is charged with 18 m³ water and 12 m³ vinyl chloride monomer (density 0.91 g cm⁻³). If 0.05 wt % of PVA (MW ≈ 125,000 g mol⁻¹) is added based on the aqueous phase, calculate (i) the total mass of PVA used, (ii) the molar concentration of PVA in the aqueous phase, and (iii) comment on whether this stabilizer loading is sufficient for industrial suspension PVC.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of suspending agents:** *(Full credit answer.)*

Suspending agents are water-soluble polymers or fine inorganic powders that adsorb at the monomer-water interface and form a thin protective film around each droplet. This film serves two functions:

- **Prevents coalescence** during droplet-droplet collisions (steric hindrance from the long polymer chains of PVA, PVP, CMC).
- **Reduces effective interfacial tension**, allowing the agitator to generate finer droplets.

Two classes:

(i) **Water-soluble polymers (protective colloids):** poly(vinyl alcohol) — PVA; poly(vinyl pyrrolidone) — PVP; carboxymethyl cellulose — CMC; methyl cellulose.

(ii) **Finely divided inorganic powders:** tricalcium phosphate — Ca₃(PO₄)₂; magnesium carbonate — MgCO₃; barium sulfate — BaSO₄; hydroxyapatite.

**Effect on particle size:** Increasing stabilizer concentration → **smaller beads** (because interfacial tension is lowered and the protective film is more robust). Excess stabilizer, however, can cause bridging flocculation.

**Effect on MWD:** Higher stabilizer concentration narrows the *bead-size distribution* (less coalescence → more uniform size), but it does **not significantly affect the MWD of the polymer chains inside each bead** (that is controlled by kinetic parameters $k_p$, $k_t$, $C_M$, etc.). **[7 marks]**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Total mass of PVA:**

Mass of aqueous phase $= 18 \, \text{m}^3 \times 1000 \, \text{kg m}^{-3} = 18{,}000 \, \text{kg}$.

Mass of PVA $= 0.05\% \times 18{,}000 = 9.0 \, \text{kg}$.

**(ii) Molar concentration of PVA:**

Moles of PVA $= 9000 \, \text{g} / 125{,}000 \, \text{g mol}^{-1} = 0.072 \, \text{mol}$.

Volume of aqueous phase $= 18{,}000 \, \text{L}$.

Molar concentration $= 0.072 / 18{,}000 = 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{mol L}^{-1} = 4 \, \mu\text{M}$.

**(iii) Industrial comment:** A PVA loading of **0.05 wt %** based on water phase is *within the typical industrial range* (0.01 – 1.0 wt %). For suspension PVC, 0.05–0.20 wt % is standard. Therefore this loading is **adequate** to prevent significant coalescence, though for finer-grade PVC (S-PVC grades used in injection molding), a slightly higher loading (0.1–0.3 wt %) combined with lower agitation is preferred. **[7 marks]**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision checklist — read this twice before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ Suspension polymerization uses **water as continuous phase** and a **monomer-soluble initiator**.
- ☐ Each droplet behaves as a **mini bulk-polymerization reactor**; hence MWD ≈ bulk MWD.
- ☐ **PDI ≈ 1.5** (combination) or **2.0** (disproportionation) — same as bulk.
- ☐ Mean droplet diameter $D_{32}$ **decreases** with increasing $N$, $D_i$, $\rho_c$, $\mu_c$.
- ☐ Mean droplet diameter $D_{32}$ **increases** with increasing $\sigma$, $\mu_d$, phase ratio $V_d/V_c$.
- ☐ Industrial products: **PVC, EPS, PMMA pearls, SAN, ion-exchange resins**.
- ☐ Stabilizer classes: **water-soluble polymers (PVA, PVP, CMC)** + **inorganic powders (Ca₃(PO₄)₂, BaSO₄)**.
- ☐ Key rate equation inside a droplet: $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{f k_d [I] / k_t}$.
- ☐ Mayo equation: $1/\bar{X}_n = 1/\bar{X}_{n,0} + C_M + C_S [S]/[M]$.
- ☐ Heat removal by water is the **principal advantage** that allows reactor volumes up to **200 m³**.
- ☐ Distinguish carefully from **emulsion polymerization** (water-soluble initiator, micelles, particles 50–500 nm, no mechanical stabilizer needed).
- ☐ Distinguish carefully from **bulk polymerization** (no water, high viscosity, hard to scale up).
- ☐ Distinguish carefully from **solution polymerization** (monomer + polymer both soluble in a solvent).
- ☐ Suspension polymerization gives **beads/pearls** of 50 µm – 2 mm; emulsion gives **latex particles** of 50–500 nm.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emulsion Polymerization: Modelling of Emulsion Reactors, Prediction of MWD and Solution Viscosity, Examples
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT745 - POLYMER REACTION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Emulsion Polymerization: Modelling of Emulsion Reactors, Prediction of MWD and Solution Viscosity, Examples

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Emulsion Polymerization** is a heterogeneous free-radical polymerization process in which a monomer (typically vinyl type, sparingly water-soluble) is dispersed as droplets (~1 µm) in a continuous aqueous phase containing a water-soluble initiator and a colloidal stabilizer (surfactant), with the polymerization occurring primarily inside surfactant-stabilized **micelles** and the resulting sub-micron polymer **latex particles** (50–500 nm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Smith–Ewart Theory:**
> The compartmentalization of propagating radicals inside separate latex particles suppresses bimolecular termination. This is the fundamental feature that distinguishes emulsion polymerization from bulk/solution polymerization, and it is the reason why emulsion polymerization yields **high molecular weight polymer at high reaction rates simultaneously** — a combination unattainable in bulk/solution systems.

## 1.2 Components of an Emulsion Polymerization Recipe

| Component | Typical Role | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Monomer | Polymerizable unit (styrene, MMA, BA, VAc) | 20–50 wt% |
| Continuous phase | Water (heat sink, dispersing medium) | 50–80 wt% |
| Surfactant (emulsifier) | Stabilizes micelles & latex particles (SDS, SLS) | 1–3 wt% (above CMC) |
| Initiator | Water-soluble (KPS, NaPS, H₂O₂) | 0.1–0.5 wt% |
| Buffer / Electrolyte | pH control | traces |
| Chain transfer agent | MW regulator (optional) | as needed |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boat-Bridge" Model

Imagine a busy river where people (monomer molecules) must cross from one bank (large monomer droplets) to a series of small islands (micelles/polymer particles) where builders (propagating radicals) construct bridges (polymer chains).

- The **water current** is the diffusion of monomer from droplets → aqueous phase → particles.
- The **islands** (micelles) are small but numerous, allowing many independent chains to grow without colliding (i.e., without terminating).
- Because each builder works in isolation on his own island, **chains can grow very long (high MW)**, while at the same time there are millions of islands operating in parallel, giving a **high overall reaction rate**.

> [!NOTE]
> This compartmentalization effect is the conceptual heart of Smith–Ewart kinetics and is repeatedly tested in KTU questions.

## 1.4 The Three Smith–Ewart Intervals (Foundation Concept)

$$\boxed{\text{Interval I} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Interval II} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Interval III}}$$

- **Interval I — Particle Nucleation / Formation:** Surfactant micelles capture radicals from the aqueous phase and become active particles. Number of particles $N_p$ increases.
- **Interval II — Particle Growth (Monomer-saturated):** Polymerization occurs in latex particles saturated with monomer; constant particle number; rate is roughly constant.
- **Interval III — Gel / Completion Stage:** Monomer droplets disappear; monomer concentration in particles falls; rate drops; particle may gel.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Smith–Ewart Recurrence Equation

The cornerstone of emulsion polymerization modelling is the **Smith–Ewart recursion** for $N_n$, the number of particles containing exactly $n$ free radicals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dN_n}{dt} = \underbrace{\dfrac{\rho_i}{N_A}\bigl[N_{n-1} - N_n\bigr]}_{\text{entry of radicals into particles}} + \underbrace{k_p [M]_p \bigl[(n+1)N_{n+1} - n\,N_n\bigr]}_{\text{propagation growth of n}\to\text{n+1}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{\rho_t}{N_A}\bigl[(n+2)(n+1)N_{n+2} - n(n-1)N_n\bigr]}_{\text{termination step}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $\rho_i$ = rate of radical entry into particles (mol/L·s)
- $\rho_t$ = rate of radical termination per particle (mol/L·s)
- $[M]_p$ = monomer concentration inside particles (mol/L)
- $N_A$ = Avogadro's number = **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**
- $k_p$ = propagation rate constant (L/mol·s)

## 2.2 The Three Smith–Ewart Limit Cases (KTU High-Yield)

### Case 1: $\bar{n} \ll 1$ — Termination dominated (large particles, low $\rho_i$)
$$\bar{n} \ll 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \bar{n} \approx \dfrac{\rho_i N_A}{2 k_p [M]_p N_p}\;\;\text{(small)}$$

### Case 2: $\bar{n} = 0.5$ — Classic emulsion (small particles, high $\rho_i$) ⭐ MOST IMPORTANT
$$\boxed{\bar{n} \approx 0.5}$$

This is the textbook case where the rate-determining step is **radical entry** into the particle. Once one radical enters, it grows until a second enters → instantaneous termination → restart cycle.

### Case 3: $\bar{n} \gg 1$ — Bulk-like behaviour (very large particles)
$$\bar{n} \gg 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_p \approx k_p [M]_p \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_i \cdot N_A}{2 k_t}}$$

## 2.3 Overall Rate of Polymerization

$$
\boxed{R_p = k_p\,[M]_p\,N_p\,\bar{n}}
$$

For Case 2 (most common KTU scenario):
$$R_p = k_p\,[M]_p\,N_p\,(0.5) = \dfrac{1}{2}\,k_p\,[M]_p\,N_p$$

## 2.4 Number of Particles — The Famous Smith–Ewart–Harkins Equation

$$
\boxed{N_p = k\left(\dfrac{\rho_i}{\mu}\right)^{2/5}\left(a_s S\right)^{3/5}}
$$

where:
- $\mu$ = rate of particle growth (m³/s)
- $a_s$ = area occupied by one surfactant molecule on particle (~0.5 nm²)
- $S$ = total surfactant concentration (mol/L)
- $k$ = numerical constant ≈ **0.53** (Smith–Ewart)

This is one of the most heavily tested equations in KTU Module 4.

## 2.5 Prediction of Molecular Weight Distribution (MWD)

### 2.5.1 Why Emulsion Polymerization Gives High MW

Because of **compartmentalization**, two growing radicals rarely meet. Termination rate $R_t$ is dramatically reduced, so the **kinetic chain length** increases:

$$
\nu_n = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i}\;\;\text{(number of monomers added per chain initiated)}
$$

For Case 2 emulsion polymerization:
$$\boxed{\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p [M]_p N_p}{2\rho_i}}$$

Number-average molecular weight:
$$
\boxed{\bar{M}_n = \bar{X}_n \cdot M_0 = \dfrac{k_p [M]_p N_p M_0}{2\rho_i}}
$$

where $M_0$ = molecular weight of monomer repeat unit.

### 2.5.2 The Most-Schulz Distribution (Most Probable MWD)

Since each chain grows until terminated by radical entry (Case 2), the MWD follows the **Schulz–Most distribution**:

$$
w(r) = \dfrac{r}{\bar{r}^2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{r}{\bar{r}}\right)
$$

with
$$\dfrac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n} = 2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Despite very high $\bar{M}_n$, the **polydispersity index $\bar{M}_w/\bar{M}_n$ remains ≈ 2** because each chain experiences one termination event after a random growth time. Students wrongly assume emulsion gives narrow MWD — it doesn't! For narrow MWD you need living/controlled radical polymerization.

### 2.5.3 Gel Effect Consideration in Interval III

As conversion $x \to x_f$, $k_t$ falls (Trommsdorff / gel effect) and $\bar{n}$ may rise above 0.5, so:

$$
\bar{M}_n^{\text{gel}} = \bar{M}_n^{\text{pre-gel}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{k_{t,0}}{k_t}\right)^{1/2}
$$

## 2.6 Prediction of Solution Viscosity (η)

For polymer dissolved in a good solvent (viscosity at concentration $c$):

$$
\boxed{[\eta] = \lim_{c\to 0}\dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = K\,M^a}
$$

This is the **Mark–Houwink–Sakurada** equation. For emulsion latexes (colloidal dispersion), use:

$$
\eta_{\text{latex}} = \eta_{\text{water}}\!\left(1 + 2.5\,\phi\right) \quad \text{(Einstein, dilute)}
$$

where $\phi$ = volume fraction of polymer particles.

For non-dilute ( Krieger–Dougherty ):

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{\eta}{\eta_s} = \left(1-\dfrac{\phi}{\phi_m}\right)^{-[\eta]\phi_m}}
$$

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Equation | Meaning / When Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_p = k_p [M]_p N_p \bar{n}$ | Overall rate |
| 2 | $\bar{n} = 0.5$ | Smith–Ewart Case 2 (most common) |
| 3 | $N_p = k(\rho_i/\mu)^{2/5}(a_s S)^{3/5}$ | Number of particles |
| 4 | $\bar{X}_n = R_p / R_i$ | Number-average DP |
| 5 | $\bar{M}_n = \bar{X}_n M_0$ | Number-average MW |
| 6 | $\bar{M}_w/\bar{M}_n = 2$ | PDI for emulsion (Case 2) |
| 7 | $\rho_i = 2 f k_d [I]$ | Radical entry flux |
| 8 | $[\eta] = K M^a$ | Mark–Houwink (solution viscosity) |
| 9 | $\eta/\eta_s = (1-\phi/\phi_m)^{-[\eta]\phi_m}$ | Krieger–Dougherty (latex viscosity) |
| 10 | Interval I, II, III | Stage of emulsion polymerization |

> [!NOTE]
> All equations above are in units where **$[M]_p$ is in mol/L, $N_p$ in particles/L, $k_p$ in L/mol·s, $R_p$ in mol/L·s**. Always convert before substitution — this is where students lose marks.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Smith–Ewart Recursion → Case 2 Result ($\bar{n} = 0.5$)

**Assumptions for Case 2 (KTU-Favourite):**
1. Particles are small ($r_p < 100$ nm).
2. Radical entry is rate-limiting; once two radicals are inside, termination is instantaneous.
3. Therefore, at most 1 radical per particle → probability of $n=0$ or $n=1$ only.

**Step 1:** Define the average number of radicals per particle:
$$\bar{n} = \dfrac{\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n N_n}{\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} N_n} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_p}$$
(because only $n=1$ state is populated, and $N_1 + N_0 = N_p$).

**Step 2:** Rate of radical entry into a particle already containing a radical → termination:
$$R_t^{(\text{particle})} = k_p[M]_p N_1$$
Because a radical in a particle can only "exit" by terminating with a newly entered radical.

**Step 3:** At pseudo-steady state, rate of radical entry equals rate of disappearance (=termination rate):
$$\rho_i = k_p [M]_p N_1$$

**Step 4:** But $N_1 = \bar{n}\,N_p$, so:
$$\rho_i = k_p [M]_p \bar{n}\, N_p$$

**Step 5:** Rate of polymerization (Case 2, since each chain adds $k_p[M]_p$ mol/L·s while active):
$$R_p = k_p [M]_p N_1 = k_p [M]_p \bar{n} N_p$$

**Step 6:** Combine with entry–termination balance to find $\bar{n}$:
$$\bar{n} = \dfrac{\rho_i}{k_p [M]_p N_p + \rho_i}$$

For Case 2 limit: $\rho_i \ll k_p[M]_p N_p$, hence:
$$\boxed{\bar{n} \to 0.5 \quad \text{when} \;\; \rho_i \ll k_p [M]_p N_p}$$

This gives the celebrated result used in **every KTU numerical**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Smith–Ewart Particle Number Equation

**Step 1:** Rate of particle growth (volume) per particle:
$$\dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{k_p[M]_p \bar{n} M_0}{\rho_p N_A}$$
where $\rho_p$ = polymer density (kg/m³).

**Step 2:** Particle radius vs. time:
$$r_p = \left(\dfrac{3 v_p}{4\pi}\right)^{1/3} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mu = \dfrac{d r_p}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Total particle surface area $\propto N_p \cdot r_p^2$. At the end of Interval I, micelles are exhausted and surfactant covers all particles:
$$N_p \cdot 4\pi r_p^2 \cdot a_s = S \cdot V \cdot N_A$$
Solve for $r_p$:
$$r_p = \left(\dfrac{S\,N_A\,V}{4\pi a_s N_p}\right)^{1/2}$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate and combine with the rate expression. Eliminate $r_p$ to obtain the celebrated result:
$$\boxed{N_p = k\left(\dfrac{\rho_i}{\mu}\right)^{2/5}\left(a_s S\right)^{3/5}, \quad k\approx 0.53}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is essential — KTU frequently asks students to **derive** $N_p$ from the basic balances, not just quote it.

## 3.3 Derivation: Number-Average Molecular Weight for Emulsion Polymerization

**Step 1:** Rate of chain initiation $R_i = \rho_i N_A$ (radicals entering particles per second per litre).

**Step 2:** Average time a chain grows before termination:
$$\tau = \dfrac{1}{\text{frequency of radical entry into an active particle}}$$

For Case 2:
$$\tau = \dfrac{N_p}{2 \rho_i N_A}$$

**Step 3:** Number of monomer units added per chain = (rate of monomer addition per particle) × τ:
$$\bar{X}_n = k_p [M]_p \cdot \tau = \dfrac{k_p [M]_p N_p}{2 \rho_i N_A}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by monomer MW $M_0$:
$$\boxed{\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{k_p [M]_p N_p M_0}{2 \rho_i N_A}}$$

This is the working equation used in the worked example below.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style 14-mark)

> **[Example 1 — KTU Pattern]** Styrene is emulsion polymerized at 60 °C using KPS initiator. Data:
>
> $k_p = 3.4 \times 10^2$ L/mol·s at 60 °C
> $[M]_p = 5.0$ mol/L (inside particles)
> $N_p = 1.0 \times 10^{17}$ particles/L
> $\rho_i = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$ mol/L·s
> $M_0$ (styrene) = 104 g/mol
> PDI = 2.0
>
> **Find:** (a) $\bar{n}$, (b) $R_p$, (c) $\bar{M}_n$, (d) $\bar{M}_w$.

**Solution (a):** Check Case 2 condition:
$$\rho_i = 1.2\times10^{-6}\;\;\text{vs.}\;\; k_p[M]_p N_p = 340 \times 5 \times 10^{17} = 1.7\times10^{20}$$

Since $\rho_i \ll k_p[M]_p N_p$ → **Case 2 applies, $\bar{n} = 0.5$.** ✅

**Solution (b):**
$$R_p = k_p [M]_p N_p \bar{n} = 340 \times 5 \times 10^{17} \times 0.5 = 8.5 \times 10^{19}\;\text{mol/L·s}$$

**Solution (c):**
$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{k_p [M]_p N_p}{2\rho_i N_A} = \dfrac{340 \times 5 \times 10^{17}}{2 \times 1.2\times10^{-6} \times 6.022\times10^{23}}$$
$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1.7\times10^{20}}{1.445\times10^{18}} = 117.6$$
$$\boxed{\bar{M}_n = 117.6 \times 104 = 12{,}231 \;\text{g/mol} \approx 1.22 \times 10^4}$$

**Solution (d):**
$$\bar{M}_w = 2\,\bar{M}_n = 2.44 \times 10^4\;\text{g/mol}$$

## 3.5 Solution Viscosity Example

> **[Example 2]** Polystyrene latex with $\phi = 0.30$ polymer volume fraction. Mark–Houwink constants: $K = 1.7\times10^{-4}$ dL/g, $a = 0.69$. $M_w = 2.5\times10^5$ g/mol. $\phi_m = 0.64$.
>
> **Find:** Latex viscosity using Krieger–Dougherty.

**Step 1:** Intrinsic viscosity:
$$[\eta] = K M^a = 1.7\times10^{-4} \times (2.5\times10^5)^{0.69}$$
$$(2.5\times10^5)^{0.69} = \exp(0.69\ln 2.5\times10^5) = \exp(0.69\times12.43) = \exp(8.58) \approx 5300$$
$$[\eta] = 1.7\times10^{-4} \times 5300 = 0.901 \;\text{dL/g}$$

**Step 2:** Krieger–Dougherty:
$$\dfrac{\eta}{\eta_s} = \left(1 - \dfrac{0.30}{0.64}\right)^{-0.901\times0.64} = (1 - 0.469)^{-0.576} = (0.531)^{-0.576}$$
$$\boxed{\eta = \eta_w \times (0.531)^{-0.576} = \eta_w \times 1.72 \approx 1.72 \;\text{cP}}$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic: Physical Picture of Emulsion Polymerization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Water + Surfactant above CMC] --> B[Micelles ~5 nm]
    A --> C[Monomer Droplets ~1 micron]
    C --> D[Diffusion of Monomer through Aqueous Phase]
    B --> D
    D --> E[Latex Particles 50-500 nm growing]
    F[Water-soluble Initiator e.g. KPS] --> G[Aqueous Phase Radical Pool]
    G --> E
    E --> H[Interval I Nucleation]
    E --> I[Interval II Growth]
    E --> J[Interval III Completion]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Smith–Ewart Reactor Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[Initiator rho_i]
        I2[Surfactant S]
        I3[Monomer M_p]
        I4[Temperature T]
    end

    subgraph Kinetics_Core
        K1[Smith-Ewart Recursion dN_n/dt]
        K2[N-bar average radicals per particle]
        K3[Rate R_p = k_p M_p N_p N-bar]
        K4[Particle Number N_p f rho_i S]
    end

    subgraph Outputs
        O1[Conversion X]
        O2[Particle Size r_p]
        O3[M-bar_n and M-bar_w]
        O4[Viscosity eta]
    end

    I1 --> K1
    I2 --> K4
    I3 --> K3
    I4 --> K1
    K1 --> K2
    K2 --> K3
    K3 --> O1
    K3 --> O3
    K4 --> O2
    K2 --> O3
    O2 --> O4
    O3 --> O4
```

## 4.3 Reactor Type Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B[Batch Emulsion Reactor] -->|lowest PDI, narrow residence time| R1[Used for Specialty Latex]
    C[Semi-Batch Emulsion Reactor] -->|monomer starved| R2[Used for Copolymers Paints]
    D[CSTR Emulsion Reactor] -->|broadest residence time| R3[Used for Large Scale SBR PVC]
    B --> N1[N_p evolves with time]
    C --> N2[N_p controlled by feed]
    D --> N3[N_p at steady state]
```

## 4.4 Interval Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Start t=0] --> B[Interval I: Micelles to Particles]
    B --> C{N_p fixed and micelles depleted}
    C -->|Yes| D[Interval II: Particle Growth Constant Rate]
    D --> E{Monomer droplets disappeared}
    E -->|Yes| F[Interval III: Gel Effect Diffusion Limited]
    F --> G[End of Polymerization]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *What is the Smith–Ewart Case 2 condition in emulsion polymerization? State the value of $\bar{n}$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Smith–Ewart Case 2 describes emulsion polymerization in which particles are very small and radical entry into a particle is rate-limiting. Once a radical enters a particle containing a propagating chain, termination is instantaneous. This results in an average number of radicals per particle of **$\bar{n} = 0.5$**, leading to high molecular weight and high rate simultaneously.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *Why does emulsion polymerization yield high molecular weight polymer at high reaction rate — a combination not possible in bulk polymerization?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In emulsion polymerization, radicals are **compartmentalized** inside separate latex particles stabilized by surfactant. Bimolecular termination requires two radicals to meet in the same particle, an event that is statistically rare (Smith–Ewart Case 2). Thus $R_t$ is drastically reduced, giving long kinetic chain length (high $\bar{M}_n$). Meanwhile, $R_p$ remains high because there are $\sim10^{17}$ particles/L operating in parallel. In bulk polymerization, increasing $R_p$ by raising [I] also increases $R_t$ and lowers $\bar{M}_n$ — this trade-off is broken by compartmentalization.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — July 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the Smith–Ewart expression for the number of latex particles $N_p$ in terms of the rate of radical entry $\rho_i$, rate of particle growth $\mu$, surfactant concentration $S$, and area per surfactant molecule $a_s$. State the assumptions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** A semi-batch emulsion polymerization of methyl methacrylate (MMA) is carried out at 50 °C with the following data:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $k_p$ at 50 °C | $6.5 \times 10^2$ L/mol·s |
| $[M]_p$ | 6.5 mol/L |
| $N_p$ | $5 \times 10^{16}$ particles/L |
| $\rho_i$ | $5 \times 10^{-7}$ mol/L·s |
| $M_0$ (MMA) | 100 g/mol |

Compute: (i) $\bar{n}$, (ii) $R_p$, (iii) $\bar{M}_n$, (iv) $\bar{M}_w$. (7 marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** *(Already shown in §3.2 of these notes — students reproduce the 5-step derivation with proper assumptions: spherical particles, instantaneous radical entry termination, surfactant fully coats particles at end of Interval I, constant $\mu$, all surfactant on particles.)* Final result:
$$N_p = 0.53\left(\dfrac{\rho_i}{\mu}\right)^{2/5}(a_s S)^{3/5}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i)** Check Case 2:
$$k_p[M]_p N_p = 650 \times 6.5 \times 5\times10^{16} = 2.11\times10^{20}$$
$\rho_i = 5\times10^{-7} \ll 2.11\times10^{20}$ → **Case 2 applies, $\bar{n} = 0.5$** ✅

**(ii)** 
$$R_p = k_p[M]_p N_p \bar{n} = 2.11\times10^{20} \times 0.5 = 1.06 \times 10^{20}\;\text{mol/L·s}$$

**(iii)**
$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{k_p[M]_p N_p}{2\rho_i N_A} = \dfrac{2.11\times10^{20}}{2\times 5\times10^{-7}\times 6.022\times10^{23}}$$
$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{2.11\times10^{20}}{6.022\times10^{17}} = 350.4$$
$$\boxed{\bar{M}_n = 350.4 \times 100 = 3.504 \times 10^4\;\text{g/mol}}$$

**(iv)**
$$\bar{M}_w = 2\,\bar{M}_n = 7.008\times10^4\;\text{g/mol}$$

---

### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Discuss the three Smith–Ewart intervals in emulsion polymerization. Show the typical $R_p$ vs. conversion curve. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain with suitable equations how the molecular weight distribution (MWD) and solution viscosity can be predicted for the polymer latex produced. Use the Mark–Houwink equation and the Krieger–Dougherty equation. (7 marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution Outline

**(a)** Three intervals — Interval I (nucleation; $N_p$ increases; micelles disappear; rate rises), Interval II (constant rate; monomer-saturated particles; steady-state; balance between entry and termination; $\bar{n}=0.5$), Interval III (monomer droplets gone; gel/Trommsdorff effect; $k_t$ drops; rate falls or rises). Plot a $R_p$ vs. $t$ curve showing these three regions.

**(b)** MWD: Most probable / Schulz distribution because each chain experiences one termination event at random growth time. Hence $\bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n = 2$. Derive $\bar{M}_n$ as in §3.3.
Solution viscosity: Use $[\eta] = KM^a$ (Mark–Houwink) for molecular contribution, and Krieger–Dougherty for colloidal contribution:
$$\eta/\eta_s = \left(1 - \dfrac{\phi}{\phi_m}\right)^{-[\eta]\phi_m}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Forgetting to **multiply by $M_0$** when converting $\bar{X}_n$ to $\bar{M}_n$ — costs **1 full mark**.
> 2. Quoting $\bar{M}_w/\bar{M}_n = 1$ for emulsion (wrong — it's **2**, Most-probable).
> 3. Failing to check the **Case 2 condition** ($\rho_i \ll k_p[M]_p N_p$) before assuming $\bar{n} = 0.5$.
> 4. Mixing up **initiator concentration $[I]$** with **radical entry rate $\rho_i$** — they differ by $2f k_d$ factor.
> 5. Writing $[M]_p$ (mol/L inside particles) instead of $[M]_{\text{overall}}$ — examiners deduct for unit confusion.
> 6. Forgetting $N_A$ in the $\bar{M}_n$ denominator — a 2-mark error that shows incomplete understanding.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ⭐ Emulsion polymerization = **micelle/latex particle** reaction in **water**; compartmentalization is the key.
- ⭐ Smith–Ewart **Case 2**: $\bar{n} = 0.5$; this is the **default assumption** unless explicitly told otherwise.
- ⭐ Overall rate: $R_p = k_p[M]_p N_p \bar{n}$.
- ⭐ Particle number: $N_p = 0.53(\rho_i/\mu)^{2/5}(a_s S)^{3/5}$.
- ⭐ Number-average MW: $\bar{M}_n = (k_p[M]_p N_p M_0)/(2\rho_i N_A)$ (Case 2).
- ⭐ PDI for emulsion: $\bar{M}_w/\bar{M}_n \approx 2$ (Most-probable/Schulz).
- ⭐ Solution viscosity: Mark–Houwink $[\eta] = KM^a$; Latex viscosity: Krieger–Dougherty.
- ⭐ Three intervals: I (nucleation) → II (growth) → III (gel effect).
- ⭐ Chain transfer agent lowers $\bar{M}_n$; surfactant above CMC is **mandatory** to form micelles.
- ⭐ Semi-batch reactor is preferred for **composition-controlled copolymers**; CSTR for **high-volume SBR/PVC**.
